name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page for solar installation services that loads dynamic homepage content, highlights key tools, and routes users to quoting, consultation, savings, packages, installers, financing, and resources.

core_features: Load homepage content (hero text, value propositions, primary actions, highlight stats) via getHomePageContent and bind it to interactive sections.
Display and route primary calls-to-action (quote, consultation, savings calculator, packages, installers, financing, system builder, blog) using the actionKey values returned by getHomePageContent.
Show a preview list of featured solar packages using getFeaturedSolarPackagesForHome(maxItems), with navigation into the full Solar Packages page and package-specific context via URL parameters.
Show a preview list of featured articles/resources using getFeaturedArticlesForHome(maxItems), with navigation into the main blog/resources listing via URL parameters.
Provide a consolidated tools/shortcuts area that uses the primaryActions from getHomePageContent to give quick access to the main tools and pages.
Respect URL parameters (e.g., counts, sections, property type, pre-selected actions) to control which sections are emphasized, how many items are loaded, and how navigation URLs are constructed.
user_workflows: Workflow: Arrive on Home and go directly to a key tool: (1) User navigates to index.html (optionally with an action URL parameter). (2) homepage-data-loader calls getHomePageContent; hero-action-panel renders heroTitle, heroSubtitle, primaryActions, and highlightStats. (3) If the action URL parameter matches a primaryActions.actionKey, hero-action-panel either focuses/highlights that action or auto-triggers the corresponding navigation. (4) User clicks the desired primary action button; click handler maps actionKey to the appropriate target page (get_quote.html, consultation_booking.html, savings_calculator.html, solar_packages.html, system_builder.html, installers_search.html, financing_overview.html, or blog_list.html) and redirects, optionally propagating relevant URL parameters such as zip or property_type.

Workflow: Review featured solar packages from Home: (1) User loads index.html; homepage-data-loader reads packages_max and property_type from the URL if present. (2) homepage-data-loader calls getFeaturedSolarPackagesForHome(maxItems derived from packages_max or system default). (3) featured-packages-preview receives the packages array and optionally filters or sorts them client-side using SolarPackage.system_type based on property_type URL parameter. (4) User scrolls to or is auto-scrolled to the featured packages section if section=packages in the URL. (5) User clicks on a specific featured package card; click handler navigates to solar_packages.html with URL parameters including source=home and packageId set to the clicked package id, plus any existing context like property_type or zip. (6) Alternatively, the user clicks a “view all” control in this component; handler navigates to solar_packages.html?source=home while preserving relevant context parameters.

Workflow: Explore educational resources from Home: (1) User loads index.html; homepage-data-loader reads articles_max from the URL if present. (2) homepage-data-loader calls getFeaturedArticlesForHome(maxItems derived from articles_max or system default). (3) featured-articles-preview shows the returned articles list (title and summary or equivalent fields from Article). (4) If section=resources in the URL, scroll-router scrolls the page to the featured-articles-preview component. (5) User clicks an individual article teaser; click handler navigates to blog_list.html with parameters such as source=home and highlightArticleId set to the Article.id to allow the blog list page to highlight that article. (6) User may also click a “view all resources” control to go to blog_list.html?source=home without a specific article highlighted.

Workflow: Use Home as a property-type oriented entry: (1) User lands on index.html with property_type in the URL (e.g., residential or commercial). (2) homepage-data-loader passes maxItems to getFeaturedSolarPackagesForHome; featured-packages-preview uses property_type from the URL to filter or prioritize packages client-side using SolarPackage.system_type. (3) hero-action-panel and tools-quick-access-strip include this property_type value when constructing navigation URLs (e.g., appending property_type to get_quote.html and consultation_booking.html links), so downstream pages can pre-select the same property type. (4) User clicks into any tool or package, and the property_type parameter is propagated in the navigation URL.

Workflow: Pre-select a navigation destination from an external link: (1) User comes from an external campaign link to index.html with an action parameter matching a primaryActions.actionKey (for example, get_quote or savings_calculator) and optionally with zip and property_type. (2) homepage-data-loader fetches homepage content; hero-action-panel or tools-quick-access-strip detects the action parameter. (3) Depending on configuration, the component either immediately redirects to the mapped destination or focuses that specific CTA (e.g., scrolls it into view and visually emphasizes it). (4) When redirecting, the navigation handler forwards any context parameters (zip, property_type, source=home) to the target page’s URL.
interactions: On page load, invoke getHomePageContent with no parameters; bind heroTitle, heroSubtitle, primaryActions, valueProps, and highlightStats to their respective components.

On page load, determine maxItems for featured packages from the packages_max URL parameter if present and valid; call getFeaturedSolarPackagesForHome(maxItems) and store the returned packages array in page state.

On page load, determine maxItems for featured articles from the articles_max URL parameter if present and valid; call getFeaturedArticlesForHome(maxItems) and store the returned articles array in page state.

When a user clicks any primaryActions item in hero-action-panel or tools-quick-access-strip, handle the click by mapping primaryActions.actionKey to a target URL and navigate using window.location with appropriate query parameters (e.g., source=home, property_type, zip, section).

When a user clicks a featured package card, construct a navigation URL to solar_packages.html that includes source=home and packageId equal to the SolarPackage.id, plus any Home page context parameters that should be preserved (e.g., property_type, zip), and navigate to that URL.
When a user clicks the "view all packages" control in featured-packages-preview, navigate to solar_packages.html with source=home and any context parameters, without including a specific packageId.

When a user clicks an article teaser in featured-articles-preview, navigate to blog_list.html with source=home and highlightArticleId set to the Article.id, preserving any additional context parameters from the Home URL.
When a user clicks the "view all resources" control in featured-articles-preview, navigate to blog_list.html with source=home and any existing context parameters.

If the section URL parameter is present and matches a known section key (for example, packages or resources), scroll-router scrolls to the corresponding component’s DOM anchor after the relevant data has been loaded and rendered.

If the action URL parameter matches a primaryActions.actionKey, hero-action-panel (or a dedicated action-router component) either auto-triggers the navigation to the corresponding destination or sets state to highlight that single CTA as pre-selected; the implementation chooses whether to auto-redirect or only emphasize.
All URL-based interactions avoid storing view state in localStorage; instead, they compute state from current URL parameters and in-memory data fetched via the assigned interfaces.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page derives its dynamic state entirely from URL parameters and API responses, without using localStorage or static hardcoded data. On every load, the page calls getHomePageContent with no parameters, then calls getFeaturedSolarPackagesForHome and getFeaturedArticlesForHome with maxItems determined from URL parameters when available. The packages_max URL parameter (if present and a valid positive integer) determines the maxItems argument for getFeaturedSolarPackagesForHome; otherwise, the implementation uses an internal system default without changing the URL. The articles_max URL parameter is handled similarly for getFeaturedArticlesForHome. A property_type URL parameter (when equal to a supported value such as residential or commercial) is not sent to any Home interfaces, but is stored in page state to filter or prioritize the SolarPackage items client-side based on SolarPackage.system_type. The same property_type parameter is appended to outgoing navigation URLs for tools that accept property type on their own pages (for example, get_quote.html?property_type=...). A zip URL parameter is not required for Home functionality but, if present, is passed through on outgoing navigation links (such as installers_search.html?zip=...) so downstream pages can localize results. The section URL parameter (e.g., section=packages, section=resources) determines which section the scroll-router will scroll into view after data has loaded, without affecting the data retrieval. The action URL parameter, when it matches a primaryActions.actionKey returned by getHomePageContent, is used to pre-select or auto-navigate to the corresponding destination page; any other unknown value is ignored safely. Additional parameters like source are controlled by Home when generating outgoing URLs (e.g., source=home) to allow target pages to know the navigation origin; these are not used to control Home logic itself, aside from optional analytics or highlighting that can be implemented without extra interfaces. Because all state is driven from URL parameters and live API responses, reloading the page with the same URL reconstructs the same Home state and behavior.

components.functionality: On initial page load (and on URL change if implemented as a client-side router), fetches all required Home data by calling getHomePageContent, getFeaturedSolarPackagesForHome, and getFeaturedArticlesForHome, then stores the responses in memory/state for child components to consume.

Displays the main introductory text and primary calls-to-action using the data returned from getHomePageContent. It renders the hero title and subtitle, shows highlight statistics, and renders a set of primary action buttons based on primaryActions. It also interprets the action URL parameter to optionally auto-trigger or emphasize a particular CTA.

Presents the list of key value propositions for the solar installation services using the valueProps array from getHomePageContent. Each item binds to its icon, title, and description fields and is purely informational, without external navigation.

Renders a compact list of the most important tools and destinations (such as savings calculator, system builder, find installers, financing, and resources) derived from primaryActions. It filters primaryActions by their actionKey and exposes them as secondary shortcuts that mirror the behavior of the main CTAs.

Displays a subset of featured SolarPackage items for quick browsing on the Home page. It consumes the packages array from getFeaturedSolarPackagesForHome, optionally filters or orders them based on the property_type URL parameter and SolarPackage.system_type, and provides navigation to the full Solar Packages page or a context-specific package view via URL parameters.

Shows a limited list of featured or recent Article items on the Home page. It binds Article fields (such as title and summary) from the articles array returned by getFeaturedArticlesForHome and allows navigation into blog_list.html with highlightArticleId and source parameters, as well as a control to view the full resources list.

Provides persistent top-level navigation shortcuts to key pages using the same primaryActions set returned from getHomePageContent. It ensures that actions like quote and consultation are always accessible from the header and maps their actionKey values to the correct outgoing routes.

Reads the section URL parameter and, after data has loaded and the corresponding components have rendered, scrolls the viewport to the associated section anchor (for example, featured-packages-preview for section=packages or featured-articles-preview for section=resources). It also updates focus where appropriate for accessibility.

Centralizes the mapping between primaryActions.actionKey values and their destination URLs for all CTAs on the Home page. It reads the action URL parameter on load and, if it matches a known actionKey from getHomePageContent.primaryActions, triggers either an automatic redirect or informs hero-action-panel to emphasize that action. It also constructs outgoing URLs with propagated context parameters such as zip, property_type, and source=home.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the mockup: a full-width header/navigation at the very top, followed immediately by a full-width hero section with a centered headline and primary actions. Beneath the hero, the main body is organized into a two-column layout within a constrained content container: the left column (about two-thirds of the width) carries the primary narrative content (value props, featured packages, featured articles), while the right column (about one-third) functions as a sidebar that surfaces tools and shortcuts in a single, structured card. Invisible functional components (data loader, scroll router, action router) sit behind this visual hierarchy and do not disrupt the flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the mockup: a full-width header/navigation at the very top, followed immediately by a full-width hero section with a centered headline and primary actions. Beneath the hero, the main body is organized into a two-column layout within a constrained content container: the left column (about two-thirds of the width) carries the primary narrative content (value props, featured packages, featured articles), while the right column (about one-third) functions as a sidebar that surfaces tools and shortcuts in a single, structured card. Invisible functional components (data loader, scroll router, action router) sit behind this visual hierarchy and do not disrupt the flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header/navigation assembly occupies roughly 18–20% of the initial viewport height (a shallow info/navigation presence), the hero uses about 40–45% of the viewport for a bold solar-focused statement, and the remaining space is dedicated to the two-column main content. Major sections (hero, main content bands) are separated by substantial vertical margins of 48–64px. Within each section, elements are spaced with 16–24px vertical gaps. Horizontally, a centered content container (max width around 1140–1200px) uses an implicit 12-column grid: the left column spans approximately 8/12 (65–70%) and the right sidebar spans 4/12 (30–35%), with a 24–32px gutter between them. Cards and panels use 24–32px internal padding on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density increases gradually from top to bottom. The header/navigation region is information-dense but compact, using tight but readable spacing and clear alignment. The hero is intentionally spacious: large typography, generous padding, and minimal elements over the image overlay. The main content area is denser but still breathable: the left column hosts stacked sections (value props, packages, articles) with clear card boundaries and 24–32px padding, while the right sidebar shows a single, well-structured tools card. No area feels crowded; whitespace around cards and between sections keeps the overall impression professional and corporate rather than cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern similar to the mockup. The eye begins at the top left with the logo and starting navigation in the header, moves right across the main navigation links and any top-level CTAs, then diagonally down into the center of the hero where the bold headline and hero actions dominate against the darkened solar image. From there, the eye moves down and slightly left into the main content’s left column (value propositions and key offerings), then sweeps right to the elevated tools sidebar card. As the user scrolls, the flow continues vertically through the left-column sections (featured packages then featured articles) while the sidebar tools remain visually anchored, reinforcing the rightward leg of the Z flow on longer pages.

layout.overall_description: The homepage presents as a polished, corporate solar services site built on a familiar WordPress-style layout. A full-width header sticks to the very top of the viewport, with a compact info strip and navigation bar providing immediate orientation and a primary route to quoting and consultation flows. Directly beneath, a wide hero image of solar panels with a subtle dark-blue overlay spans edge-to-edge, occupying nearly half of the first screen. Centered within this hero, a bold white headline and a focused action panel float against the image, giving the user a clear next step without visual noise. Below the hero, the content narrows into a centered container that adopts a two-column grid: the left two-thirds of the page host a vertically stacked sequence of white cards and sections showcasing why solar with this provider is valuable, what packages are available, and a preview of educational articles. Running alongside, the right one-third acts as a steady sidebar hosting a single, elevated tools card that surfaces key calculators, savings tools, and quick links to installers and financing. Spacing between these major blocks is generous—48–64px gaps between sections, 16–24px between items—resulting in a modern, moderately spacious marketing layout. Invisible behavioral components (data loading overlay, scroll-aware routing, and action routing) sit atop or behind these visual layers without claiming visible space, keeping the overall aesthetic clean while enabling dynamic navigation and interactions. On smaller screens, the two-column layout gracefully collapses: the hero remains prominent, while the sidebar tools move below the hero and above the main content stack, preserving the intended hierarchy and flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header and navigation occupy a full-width band anchored to the very top of the page, spanning 100% of the viewport width and sitting above the hero section. Visually it reads as a two-tier header within one component: a shallow top info strip and a slightly taller navigation bar beneath it. The entire header area is constrained horizontally by a centered content container (max ~1140–1200px wide) with 24–32px horizontal padding on smaller screens. The top info strip is approximately 48px tall (about 6 × the 8px base unit), with a solid corporate blue background. Within this strip, on the left side, concise location or service-area text is aligned to the left edge of the container, while on the right side, a compact cluster of contact details (phone, email) and a small, high-contrast 'Get a Free Quote' button are aligned to the right; the vertical center of this content aligns with the center of the strip. Directly beneath, separated by only a 1px border or subtle shadow, the main navigation bar is about 64–72px tall. It has a white background with a light bottom shadow to distinguish it from the hero below. The site logo sits at the left side of this bar, aligned vertically centered. To the right of the logo, primary navigation links run horizontally, spaced 24–32px apart, with an optional secondary CTA button (e.g., 'Schedule Consultation') aligned flush right, matching the top CTA visually. The total combined header height is roughly 112–120px, corresponding to around 18–20% of the desktop viewport height. The hero section begins immediately below this header without a gap. On scroll, the main navigation bar behaves as sticky: it remains fixed to the top of the viewport (with the info strip scrolled away), preserving quick access to navigation while the rest of the page content moves beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The component is positioned at the very top of the document flow and spans full width. The main navigation bar portion is sticky and remains fixed to the top of the viewport after scrolling past the initial info strip; the top info strip scrolls away with the page. The header does not overlap the hero at rest but defines the upper boundary for the hero section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hero-action-panel sits directly beneath the header-navigation-shortcuts component, touching it with no visible gap. It occupies the full width of the viewport and approximately 40–45% of the viewport height on desktop, with a minimum height around 480px and a maximum around 640px to maintain impact. A solar installation photograph fills the background, covered by a dark blue semi-transparent overlay to ensure strong contrast for overlaid text. Content is constrained within the same centered container used in the header (max ~1140–1200px, with 24–32px horizontal padding). Within this container, the hero content is vertically centered within the hero’s height: imagine an invisible vertical center line where the main headline and actions align. The primary headline (large white text) is centered horizontally within the container and sits roughly at the visual middle of the hero. Beneath the headline, a shorter subheading spans 60–70% of the container width and is also center-aligned, creating a strong central focal block. Below this, a horizontal row of 1–2 primary buttons (e.g., 'Get a Free Quote', 'Estimate My Savings') is centered; each button has 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with 16–24px of horizontal space between them. There is approximately 24px of vertical space between the headline and subheading, and another 24px between the subheading and the buttons. The bottom of the hero has 48–64px of clear vertical space before the main content section begins, ensuring the hero feels open and prominent. On larger screens, this entire on-image block feels like a clean, centered cluster; on smaller screens, the same content remains centered but the width of text and buttons adjusts to 90–100% of the container width, maintaining at least 16–24px padding from the viewport edges. The hero sets the main visual tone and anchors the user before they move into the two-column content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The hero is a static block in the normal document flow, directly following the header. It scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page, with no fixed or sticky behavior. Background image covers the area (background-size: cover) and remains attached to the hero section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The value-props-list marks the top of the main content area and sits immediately below the hero-action-panel, separated by a generous 48–64px vertical gap. It lives inside a centered content container (max ~1140–1200px wide) that introduces the two-column layout. Within this container, the implicit 12-column grid is applied: the left main column spans roughly 8 columns (about 65–70% of the container width), and the right sidebar spans 4 columns (about 30–35%), separated by a 24–32px gutter. The value-props-list occupies the top of the left column, aligning its top edge horizontally with the top of the tools-quick-access-strip in the right column. Visually, it appears as a white card or lightly elevated section that takes the full width of the left column, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. At the top of the card, a section heading like 'Why Go Solar With Us' is left-aligned, followed by 16–24px of vertical space before the list of value propositions begins. Each value proposition is arranged as a small media block, with an icon or small graphic on the far left and a short title and 1–2 lines of descriptive text to the right. These items are stacked vertically with 16–24px between them, keeping the section readable but reasonably dense. The bottom of this card sits roughly 32–40px above the featured-packages-preview component, making it feel clearly separated but still part of a continuous left-column story.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The component is static within the main content flow, occupying the upper portion of the left column of the two-column grid. It does not have sticky or fixed behavior; its position relative to the right sidebar tools card is locked at initial load (top edges aligned).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-quick-access-strip forms the core of the right sidebar and occupies the top portion of the right column in the main content container. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the value-props-list on the left, reinforcing the main two-column band. It spans the entire width of the right column (about 30–35% of the container) and appears as a vertically oriented, white, elevated card to visually echo the 'Categories' sidebar card from the mockup. Internally, it has 24–32px of padding on all sides. At the top of the card, a concise heading such as 'Quick Tools' is left-aligned. Beneath the heading, 16–24px of vertical space precedes the shortcut content. The tools themselves are presented as a vertical strip of pill-like buttons or compact tiles, each spanning the full width of the card: for example, 'Savings Calculator', 'Compare Packages', 'Find Installers', 'Financing Options', 'View Resources'. Each item has 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, with 8–12px of vertical gap between items, giving the impression of a neat stack. The overall height of the card is enough to feel substantial but not overwhelming—likely around 260–360px depending on the number of tools, and the card stops well above the fold on smaller desktops, making it easy to scan. Below the last tool item, there is 24–32px of padding before the bottom of the card. Additional sidebar content could appear below this in the same column if needed, but in this configuration it functions as the primary sidebar element. The left column’s sections (value props, packages, articles) scroll beside this card, making it a stable visual reference on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The tools card sits in the right sidebar as part of the two-column grid. It behaves as a sticky sidebar element: once the user scrolls down such that the top of the card reaches approximately 24px below the sticky navigation bar, it remains fixed in that position within the viewport until the bottom of the main content container is reached. This keeps key tools accessible without occupying additional global header space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-packages-preview component continues the left-column narrative immediately below the value-props-list. There is a 32–40px vertical gap between the bottom of the value-props-list card and the top of this section, keeping them clearly separated but visually related. It uses the same centered content container and 8/12 left-column span. The section begins with a left-aligned heading such as 'Featured Solar Packages', positioned flush with the left column edge. About 16–24px below the heading, a short explanatory line or subheading may appear, followed by another 16–24px of vertical space before the packages grid itself. The packages are arranged in a grid that fills the width of the left column: on desktop, two or three package cards appear per row, each card taking roughly 30–48% of the left column width with 16–24px gutters between cards. Each package card is a white panel with 24–32px padding, featuring a concise title, key stats (system size, estimated savings), a short bullet list of benefits, and a small 'View Details' or 'Get Quote' button at the bottom. The cards stack vertically as needed, forming a block that may extend several rows deep. There is 24–32px of vertical spacing between card rows. The lower edge of the packages section sits roughly 48px above the featured-articles-preview component, giving the impression of another substantial, but distinct, section within the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component is static and occupies the middle portion of the left column within the main content container. It scrolls naturally with the page. There is no sticky or fixed behavior, but its height ensures that, while the user scrolls through the package cards, the tools-quick-access-strip in the right sidebar often remains in view (due to its sticky behavior), reinforcing the Z-pattern of attention.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-articles-preview sits in the left column below the featured-packages-preview, continuing the main vertical content flow. There is a generous 48px vertical gap between the bottom of the packages grid and the top of this section to clearly signal a change in content type from offerings to educational resources. It spans the same 8/12 left-column width of the content container. A left-aligned heading such as 'Latest Solar Insights' or 'From Our Blog' appears at the top, flush with the left edge of the left column. Below the heading, a short subheading or description may appear, separated by 16–24px of vertical space. The articles are then listed in a vertical stack, typically 3–4 items visible on the homepage. Each article preview is arranged as a horizontal card within the left column: a thumbnail image on the left occupying about 25–30% of the card width, and text content (title, short excerpt, meta info) on the right occupying the remaining 70–75%. The card has 24px of internal padding and a subtle border or shadow to distinguish it from the background. There is 24–32px of vertical spacing between article cards. At the very bottom of the section, aligned right, a 'View All Articles' or 'Browse Resources' link or button can appear, sitting about 16–24px below the last article card. The right sidebar tools card remains adjacent to this section visually; because the sidebar component is shorter, it may appear to end around the middle of the article list’s height, with whitespace below it in the right column, which is consistent with a typical sidebar pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The component is static and occupies the lower portion of the left column in the main content container. It scrolls naturally with the page, with its top and bottom positions relative to the right sidebar determined by the height of the sidebar tools card and the total height of the left-column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-data-loader is an infrastructural component that controls when the rest of the homepage content appears. When active (e.g., while fetching dynamic homepage content, packages, and articles), it overlays the entire viewport, effectively sitting above all other visible components. The overlay spans 100% of the viewport width and height (covering the header, hero, and main content) and uses a semi-transparent white or light-blue background to dim the underlying layout. At the center of the viewport, a small loading indicator and message appear, aligned both horizontally and vertically, ensuring users understand that data is being loaded. Under normal circumstances (once data is loaded), the overlay is removed completely, leaving no visible trace or spacing in the layout. All other visual components (header, hero, two-column content) retain their described positions and sizes irrespective of this component’s presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The component has no permanent spatial footprint in the document flow. When active, it uses a fixed-position, full-screen overlay anchored to the viewport (top: 0, left: 0, width: 100vw, height: 100vh) with a higher z-index than all other components, and it prevents interaction with the underlying UI. When inactive, it is effectively absent from the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scroll-router functions as a behavioral layer tied to the viewport rather than as a visible UI element. It does not render any visual component on the page and therefore does not occupy physical space in the layout. Conceptually, it monitors the user’s scroll position relative to key sections (hero, value props, packages, articles) and can update navigation states—for example, highlighting corresponding links in the header-navigation-shortcuts or triggering smooth scrolling when a navigation shortcut is clicked. Its presence is purely logical: the visible outcome is that as users scroll down through the two-column layout, the correct navigation item in the sticky header appears active or highlighted, enhancing orientation without adding any standalone visual block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No rendered UI and no participation in the document flow. It is effectively a non-visual, fixed behavioral controller bound to the viewport and scroll events, influencing other components’ states (e.g., active nav link styling) without altering their spatial arrangement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The action-router is another non-visual controller component that orchestrates navigation when users interact with key calls-to-action across the page. It does not display any buttons or panels by itself and has no visible footprint. Instead, it sits logically behind the hero-action-panel, tools-quick-access-strip, featured-packages-preview, and featured-articles-preview, intercepting clicks on CTAs such as 'Get a Free Quote', 'Estimate Savings', 'View Package Details', or 'Read Article'. Based on these interactions, it routes users either to specific sections within the page (via smooth scrolling to anchors) or to dedicated routes (e.g., quoting or consultation flows), all without introducing any additional visible layout elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The component has no spatial behavior in the visual layer: it does not render into the DOM in a way that affects layout. It is effectively an invisible overlay of logic attached to interactive elements, influencing navigation and routing only. Its presence does not change the size, position, or flow of any other component.

name: Get a Quote
title: Get a Quote
description: This page collects basic property and electricity usage information, allows users to select a desired solar system size, and submits the data to create a QuoteRequest and associated QuotePlans. After successful submission, the user is redirected to the Quote Results page using the created quoteRequestId in the URL.

core_features: Load dynamic form options and helper text by calling getQuoteFormOptions on page initialization (property type labels, system size options, roof area and bill helper text).
Parse incoming URL parameters (zip, propertyType, roofAreaSqft, averageBill, systemSizeKw) on load and use them to prefill corresponding form fields when valid.

Provide a structured quote input form with required fields for ZIP code, property type, roof area, average monthly bill, desired system size, and an optional notes field, all mapped to QuoteRequest fields.
Apply client-side validation rules to ensure all required QuoteRequest fields are present and well-formed before allowing submission.
Submit form data through createQuoteRequestAndGeneratePlans, creating a QuoteRequest and generating related QuotePlans based on user inputs.
Handle submission states including loading (while the interface call is in progress), success (navigate to Quote Results), and error (display non-technical error feedback and allow retry).
Redirect the user to quote_results.html with the quoteRequestId from the created QuoteRequest as a URL parameter after successful submission.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Direct access with empty form: User navigates to get_quote.html without URL parameters -> Page initializes and calls getQuoteFormOptions -> Form fields render with no prefilled values -> User enters ZIP, selects property type, enters roof area and average monthly bill, selects desired system size, optionally adds notes -> User clicks the submit button -> Form validation runs; if valid, createQuoteRequestAndGeneratePlans is called -> On success, the page redirects to quote_results.html?quoteRequestId={createdQuoteRequest.id}.

Workflow 2 – Prefilled from another tool: User comes from another page (e.g., savings_calculator.html) with URL parameters such as zip, averageBill, systemSizeKw -> Page initializes, parses URL parameters, and calls getQuoteFormOptions -> Form fields are prefilled based on URL values when they are valid and compatible with loaded options (e.g., propertyType must match one of the returned propertyTypeOptions) -> User reviews and edits any field as needed -> User submits the form -> Validation runs, then createQuoteRequestAndGeneratePlans is called -> On success, user is redirected to quote_results.html?quoteRequestId={createdQuoteRequest.id}.

Workflow 3 – Handling validation errors: User lands on get_quote.html (with or without prefilled parameters) -> User enters some information but omits a required field (for example, roof area) or enters an invalid value (e.g., non-numeric text in a numeric field) -> User clicks submit -> Validation handler flags specific fields with errors and prevents submission -> User corrects the highlighted fields -> Validation re-runs and passes -> createQuoteRequestAndGeneratePlans is invoked and, on success, user is redirected to quote_results.html with the created quoteRequestId.

Workflow 4 – Handling interface errors: User completes the form and clicks submit -> Validation passes -> createQuoteRequestAndGeneratePlans is called -> The interface returns an error (e.g., network or server issue) -> Submission handler shows a generic error message, keeps the form values intact, and exits loading state -> User can adjust inputs if desired and click submit again -> On a successful retry, user is redirected to quote_results.html?quoteRequestId={createdQuoteRequest.id}.

Workflow 5 – Adjusting prefilled values: User arrives with URL parameters prefilled (for example, propertyType and systemSizeKw coming from solar_packages.html) -> The form shows these preselected values -> User changes property type or desired system size to something else from the loaded options -> The updated selections are stored in the current QuoteRequest form state and override any original URL-based defaults for this session -> User submits form -> New QuoteRequest and QuotePlans reflect the updated values, and user is redirected to Quote Results with the new quoteRequestId.
interactions: On page load, the page logic triggers two actions: parsing incoming URL parameters into a temporary state object, and calling getQuoteFormOptions to retrieve propertyTypeOptions, systemSizeOptionsKw, roofAreaHelpText, and averageBillHelpText.

When getQuoteFormOptions returns successfully, the form initializes its dropdown options for property type and system size, the helper text for roof area and average bill fields, and then applies any compatible URL-based prefill values to these fields.
When the user types into the ZIP code input, an onFieldChange handler updates the QuoteRequest form state and clears any existing validation error for that field.

When the user selects a property type from the dropdown, an onChange handler stores the selected value in the QuoteRequest form state; if a propertyType URL parameter was present, it is used only as an initial value and can be overridden by this interaction.

When the user enters a numeric value (roof area, average monthly bill), onFieldChange updates the corresponding numeric fields in the QuoteRequest form state; additional onFieldBlur or onValidate checks ensure the values are numeric and positive before allowing submission.

When the user selects a desired system size from the system size dropdown, an onChange handler updates desired_system_size_kw in the QuoteRequest form state; if a systemSizeKw URL parameter was provided, it is used to preselect a matching option when available.

As the user moves between fields, a validation handler may run onBlur or on-demand to mark fields as valid or invalid, updating the form’s error state and enabling or disabling the submit button accordingly.

When the user clicks the submit button (e.g., labeled 'Calculate quote' or 'See estimate'), an onSubmit handler runs full-form validation; if any required field is missing or invalid, submission is blocked, and the corresponding errors are surfaced to the user.

If validation passes, the onSubmit handler sets a loading state and calls createQuoteRequestAndGeneratePlans with the current form state values mapped to interface parameters (zipCode, propertyType, roofAreaSqft, averageMonthlyBill, desiredSystemSizeKw, notes).

On successful response from createQuoteRequestAndGeneratePlans, an onSuccess handler reads the returned quoteRequest.id value and performs a navigation to quote_results.html with quoteRequestId set in the query string; the plans array returned by the interface is not rendered on this page but may be relied upon by the backend for the results page.

If createQuoteRequestAndGeneratePlans returns an error, an onError handler clears the loading state, shows a user-friendly error message, and leaves the form values unchanged so the user can retry without re-entering data.

If the user clicks the browser back button from the Quote Results page to return to this page, the browser’s standard behavior may restore previous input values; the page logic does not rely on URL parameters alone in this case but will re-run getQuoteFormOptions to ensure options and helper text are current.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is initialized from a combination of URL parameters and dynamic interface data, with no use of localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. On load, the page parses the following query parameters, if present: zip, propertyType, roofAreaSqft, averageBill, systemSizeKw. These parameters are stored in a temporary prefill state, then getQuoteFormOptions is called. Once form options are loaded, the prefill state is reconciled with them: (1) The zip parameter directly pre-populates the ZIP code field. (2) The propertyType parameter is applied only if it exactly matches one of the value entries in the returned propertyTypeOptions; otherwise, the property type field remains unselected. (3) The roofAreaSqft parameter is parsed as a number and applied only if it is a valid numeric value; invalid or missing values result in an empty field. (4) The averageBill parameter is parsed as a number and applied only if valid. (5) The systemSizeKw parameter is applied only if it matches one of the values in systemSizeOptionsKw; if not, the system size dropdown remains unselected. URL-provided values act solely as initial defaults; any subsequent user interaction with the form fields overrides these defaults in the in-memory QuoteRequest form state. The submit action does not modify the page URL beyond triggering navigation to quote_results.html with quoteRequestId={createdQuoteRequest.id}. The Get a Quote page does not add or update query parameters after initial load, and it does not persist state via localStorage, cookies, or other client-side persistence. All state beyond URL-prefilled values exists only in memory for the lifetime of the page instance.

components.functionality: On initial page load, this component parses incoming URL parameters (zip, propertyType, roofAreaSqft, averageBill, systemSizeKw), stores them in temporary prefill state, and invokes getQuoteFormOptions. After receiving propertyTypeOptions, systemSizeOptionsKw, roofAreaHelpText, and averageBillHelpText, it initializes the form’s option lists, helper text, and sets initial field values where the prefill data is compatible with the loaded options.

This component renders and manages the main quote input form, including fields for ZIP code, property type dropdown, roof area, average monthly bill, system size dropdown, and an optional notes area. It maintains the in-memory representation of the pending QuoteRequest, updates state on each field change, and coordinates with validation and submission handlers.

This component manages the property type selection within the form, using the propertyTypeOptions returned from getQuoteFormOptions. It applies a URL-based propertyType prefill if the value matches one of the available options and updates the QuoteRequest.property_type field whenever the user changes the selection.

This component manages the desired system size selection, populating its options from systemSizeOptionsKw returned by getQuoteFormOptions. It preselects an option when systemSizeKw is provided in the URL and matches an available value, and it updates QuoteRequest.desired_system_size_kw when the user selects a different system size.

This component centralizes validation logic for the quote form. It ensures that required QuoteRequest fields (zip_code, property_type, roof_area_sqft, average_monthly_bill, desired_system_size_kw) are present and correctly formatted, and that numeric fields contain valid numbers. It runs on field blur and before submission, updates the error state for each field, and controls whether the submit action is allowed.

This component is responsible for orchestrating the submission of the form data. After successful validation, it calls createQuoteRequestAndGeneratePlans with the mapped QuoteRequest fields (zipCode, propertyType, roofAreaSqft, averageMonthlyBill, desiredSystemSizeKw, notes), manages loading and error states, and on success retrieves quoteRequest.id from the response and triggers navigation to quote_results.html with quoteRequestId set to that id. It does not display the returned QuotePlans but relies on their association with the QuoteRequest for later use on the results page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a slim full-width info bar at the very top, a full-width white navigation bar beneath it, then a full-width hero image band with a dark overlay and a centered 'Get a Quote' headline plus supporting text. Below the hero, the main content area uses a two-column layout: the left 65–70% of the width is occupied by a prominent quote-input-form card (taking the place of the mockup’s main project image), while the right 30–35% is reserved for a supportive sidebar (e.g., benefits, trust signals), visually similar to the 'Categories' card. Within the form, the property-type-selector and system-size-selector are arranged following a simple internal grid (stacked on mobile, two-column row on desktop), keeping the same left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a slim full-width info bar at the very top, a full-width white navigation bar beneath it, then a full-width hero image band with a dark overlay and a centered 'Get a Quote' headline plus supporting text. Below the hero, the main content area uses a two-column layout: the left 65–70% of the width is occupied by a prominent quote-input-form card (taking the place of the mockup’s main project image), while the right 30–35% is reserved for a supportive sidebar (e.g., benefits, trust signals), visually similar to the 'Categories' card. Within the form, the property-type-selector and system-size-selector are arranged following a simple internal grid (stacked on mobile, two-column row on desktop), keeping the same left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top info bar occupies roughly 8–10% of the viewport height, the navigation bar about 10–12%, and the hero section approximately 40–45% of the initial viewport height. The hero has generous top and bottom padding so the headline and subcopy feel centered and spacious. Below the hero, the main content block begins after a vertical gap of about 48–64px. The quote-input-form card fills about two-thirds of the content width on the left with 24–32px internal padding, while the sidebar area on the right occupies the remaining one-third. Within the form, individual fields and selectors are spaced 16–24px apart vertically, and grouped rows (like property-type-selector alongside system-size-selector on desktop) follow a comfortable 24px horizontal gap.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately spacious marketing feel: the hero is very open, with a bold headline and minimal clutter. The form card below is denser than the hero but still uses consistent 16–24px vertical spacing between fields and sections so it never feels cramped. Key interactive areas like the system-size-selector are given slightly more breathing room and, where appropriate, a card-like treatment to stand out. Inline validation and status messaging are compact (8–12px spacing) so they provide feedback without bloating the layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the Z-pattern of the mockup. The user’s eye starts at the top left (logo/address in the info bar), moves right across contact details and the primary header CTA, then diagonally down to the centered hero headline and supporting copy. From there it travels down to the left, landing on the large quote-input-form card as the main focus of the page. Inside the form, the eye moves top-to-bottom through basic fields, hits the property-type-selector first, then is drawn slightly rightward to the more visually emphasized system-size-selector. Finally, attention moves to the bottom-right of the form where the submit/CTA button is placed. When active, the quote-form-loader temporarily takes over as the visual focus with a centered overlay spinner, while validation and submission handlers subtly modulate the experience without disrupting the main flow.

layout.overall_description: The Get a Quote page feels like a professional, corporate solar-services landing page structured in a modern WordPress style. At the very top, a slim, full-width info bar spans edge to edge with the company’s location or brief contact details aligned to the left and quick-contact items (phone, email, perhaps a mini CTA) aligned to the right. Below it, a clean white navigation bar stretches across the screen with the logo on the left and primary navigation links on the right, occupying a bit more vertical space than the info bar. Beneath this chrome, a full-width hero image with a dark blue-tinted overlay fills nearly half the viewport height, with a bold white 'Get Your Solar Quote' headline and a short subheading centered both horizontally and vertically, giving a confident, open first impression. Scrolling slightly reveals the main content area: a two-column section on a very light gray or white background. On the left, a large, white quote-input-form card sits in the top-left of this section, spanning about two-thirds of the width, with a subtle drop shadow and 24–32px of internal padding. Its fields are neatly stacked in a vertical flow, with key controls like property type and system size arranged in a simple responsive grid. On the right, aligned to the top of the form, a narrower sidebar card (not one of the listed components, but part of the overall page layout) mirrors the mockup’s 'Categories' block, offering brief reassurance copy or benefits, and visually balancing the form. The user’s attention naturally moves from the hero headline to the substantial form on the left, then to the lighter sidebar on the right. When data is being prepared or submitted, a semi-transparent overlay dims the main content while a centered loader keeps focus. Validation and submission feedback appear directly within the form, preserving the overall clean, structured look while guiding users efficiently through the quote request process.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-form-loader is not visible by default and only appears when the quote data is being initialized or when a submission is in progress. When active, it presents as a full-screen (or full-content-area) overlay that sits above the main content region, starting just below the navigation bar and extending to the bottom of the viewport. The overlay covers both the left quote-input-form card and the right sidebar column, slightly dimming them with a semi-transparent dark layer. At the center of the viewport, a compact loading card (roughly 320–400px wide) appears with 24–32px padding, a spinner or progress indicator, and a short 'Preparing your custom solar quote…' message, all centered horizontally and vertically. This card maintains the site’s white background and subtle shadow, ensuring it feels like a deliberate, polished modal rather than a raw spinner. On narrower screens, the loader still stretches edge to edge, with the card scaling down to maintain at least 24px padding from the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed overlay relative to the viewport: when active, it sits above all page content, centered both vertically and horizontally, and prevents interaction with the underlying form. It does not affect the underlying layout structure; instead, it floats over it and disappears once loading or submission is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-input-form is the dominant interactive element in the main content area and is visually analogous to the main project image in the mockup. It is placed in the left column of the primary two-column layout, beginning roughly 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero section. On desktop, it occupies about 65–70% of the available content width, leaving approximately 30–35% on the right for the sidebar card. The form sits within a white card with a subtle drop shadow and slightly rounded corners, separated from the page background by 24–32px of padding on all sides. The top of the card might include a concise title like 'Tell Us About Your Property' left-aligned within the card. Inside, form inputs are arranged in a clear vertical flow. Each field group (including the property-type-selector and system-size-selector) is separated by 16–24px of vertical space to maintain readability. On wider screens, certain related fields are arranged in two-column rows inside the card using an internal grid: for example, the property-type-selector might sit in the left half of a row while the system-size-selector occupies the right half, with a 24px horizontal gap between them. On tablet and mobile, the form card still spans nearly the full width of the screen (minus responsive page padding), and all fields stack to 100% width. The bottom of the form card houses the primary submit button aligned to the right, with 16–24px of padding beneath it to keep it visually grounded and distinct from the card’s edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, anchored to the left side of the main two-column layout. It expands vertically based on content and remains scrollable with the page. The form’s width adjusts responsively with breakpoints; the two-column internal field layout collapses to a single column on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The property-type-selector lives inside the quote-input-form card as one of the upper field groups, appearing within the top third of the form’s vertical flow. It is visually presented as a standard form control block: a left-aligned label at the top, followed by the control area (radio buttons, dropdown, or pill-style options) directly beneath. The label is separated from the control by about 8px of vertical space, and the entire selector block has 16–24px of vertical margin above and below to clearly distinguish it from adjacent fields. On desktop within the form’s internal grid, the property-type-selector typically occupies the left half of a two-column row, with its right edge aligned to the centerline of the form card and a 24px gap separating it from the system-size-selector on the right. The control itself spans the full width of its half-column, with internal control padding of 8–12px vertically and 16–20px horizontally (for segmented controls) to maintain tap targets and legibility. On tablet and mobile, the property-type-selector becomes a full-width block in the form card, stacking above the system-size-selector with the same 16–24px vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and responsive within the form’s layout. It does not float or stick; instead, it flows with the form content. In multi-column layouts, it locks to the left side of its row; in single-column layouts it expands to 100% of the form card’s width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The system-size-selector is also placed inside the quote-input-form card but is given slightly heightened visual emphasis because it is a key quote driver, analogous to the visually distinct 'Categories' sidebar in the mockup. On desktop, it typically occupies the right half of the same two-column row as the property-type-selector, aligning its left edge with the internal grid centerline and leaving a 24px gap between them. To differentiate it, the system-size-selector may be presented in a light-tinted card within the form: a subtle colored background (e.g., very light blue) or a thin accent border, with 16–24px of internal padding around its options. A concise label such as 'Preferred System Size' appears at the top, left-aligned, with about 8px spacing before the control area. Option tiles or sliders inside this selector are arranged either in a horizontal row or a 2×N grid, each option having 8–12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding to feel tappable and clear. This creates a small 'island' of focus within the form, drawing the eye slightly rightward and reinforcing the Z-flow pattern. On smaller viewports, the system-size-selector breaks out of the side-by-side layout and becomes a full-width section stacked directly below the property-type-selector, retaining its slightly elevated card-like styling but respecting the same 16–24px vertical spacing as other fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form but visually emphasized. It participates in the internal two-column grid on larger screens and collapses to a single column on smaller screens. It does not stick or float, but its card-like styling makes it stand out as a focused decision area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-form-validation-handler has no independent visual container but governs how validation states appear around the form fields inside the quote-input-form card. When errors occur, each affected field (including property-type-selector and system-size-selector) displays a compact error message line directly beneath the control, aligned to the left, using a smaller font size and a red or accent color. These inline messages add about 8–12px of vertical height per field. There may also be a global validation summary row positioned at the top of the form card content, immediately below the form title and above the first field group. This summary appears as a full-width bar within the card, lightly tinted (e.g., pale red/yellow), with 12–16px internal padding and a brief message like 'Please fix the highlighted fields.' The summary bar spans the entire width of the card’s content area and pushes the rest of the form downward when visible, preserving the underlying grid and spacing rhythm. The error borders around invalid fields use a 1–2px colored outline, maintaining the same layout dimensions while adding clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Entirely inline and reactive; it does not reserve permanent space. Error messages and the summary bar appear and disappear within the existing form card, pushing content down when present but not altering the overall two-column page layout. All behavior remains bound to the scroll position of the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-request-submission-handler operates around the bottom section of the quote-input-form card, where the primary submit button is located. Visually, the submit button is placed at the bottom-right of the form content area, aligned with the right edge of the internal grid, with 16–24px of spacing above any card bottom padding. When the user initiates submission, the handler triggers a loading state on this button: the button may expand slightly, dim, or show a spinner within it, while remaining in the same bottom-right position. If submission succeeds quickly, the user is redirected and no additional visual element is needed on this page. If there is a brief 'processing' period, a small status text line (e.g., 'Creating your quote…') can appear inline directly below the button, left-aligned with the button, using a small font and 8–12px vertical spacing. If submission fails, a concise error banner similar to the validation summary may appear near the top of the form card, and/or a small error note appears beneath the button, both using existing spacing conventions. The handler does not introduce any standalone panel or modal beyond the loader overlay controlled by quote-form-loader.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form layout. The submit button is fixed in position at the bottom of the form card (relative to the card, not the viewport) and scrolls with the page. Loading and error states reuse that same spatial slot without altering the card’s width or the two-column page structure. If combined with the quote-form-loader, the handler can trigger the global overlay while preserving the underlying button position.

name: Quote Results
title: Quote Results
description: Displays estimated solar plans for a specific quote request, with sortable results and navigation to detailed quotes.

core_features: Fetch and display all QuotePlans associated with a specific QuoteRequest via getQuotePlansForRequest.
Show key QuoteRequest context (such as basic input details) so users know which property and quote the plans belong to.
Provide sorting controls using availableSortOptions and appliedSort from getQuotePlansForRequest (e.g., by estimated monthly payment, total price).
Allow users to change sort criteria and direction, updating URL parameters and reloading plans accordingly.
Display a list of quote plan cards with key metrics (system_size_kw, total_price, estimated_monthly_payment, loan_term_years, apr where available).
Enable click on a quote plan to navigate to quote_detail.html with the selected quotePlanId.
user_workflows:
Viewing quote results after submitting the Get a Quote form:
1. User submits the Get a Quote form on get_quote.html, which redirects to quote_results.html with a quoteRequestId URL parameter.
2. quote_results.html initializes by reading quoteRequestId (and optional sortBy, sortDirection) from the URL.
3. The page controller calls getQuotePlansForRequest with quoteRequestId, sortBy, and sortDirection.
4. The interface returns quoteRequest, plans, availableSortOptions, and appliedSort.
5. The page shows a brief quoteRequest summary and renders all plans using the appliedSort order.
6. User reviews the plans and optionally clicks any plan card to open its details on quote_detail.html.

Changing the sort order of plans:
1. User sees a sort control populated from availableSortOptions and the currently appliedSort.
2. User selects a different sort option (sortBy) from the dropdown or control.
3. The sort component updates the URL parameters (sortBy and sortDirection) without removing quoteRequestId.
4. The page controller detects the URL change and re-calls getQuotePlansForRequest with updated sortBy and sortDirection.
5. The updated plans list is rendered with the new appliedSort; the sort controls reflect the new appliedSort values.

Reversing sort direction:
1. User clicks a sort direction toggle (e.g., ascending/descending) in the sort bar.
2. The sort component updates sortDirection in the URL while preserving quoteRequestId and sortBy.
3. The page controller calls getQuotePlansForRequest again with the new sortDirection.
4. Plans are re-rendered according to the new direction; appliedSort.sortDirection is shown as active in the UI.

Opening a quote detail from results:
1. User reviews the list of plan cards.
2. User clicks anywhere on a plan card designated as selectable.
3. The plan list component triggers navigation to quote_detail.html, passing the selected plan's id as quotePlanId in the URL.
4. quote_detail.html uses quotePlanId to load and show full quote details.
5. If the user navigates back to quote_results.html (browser back or a link from quote_detail.html that preserves quoteRequestId and optional sort params), the page reloads using those URL parameters and re-renders the same sorted list.

Handling missing or empty results:
1. If quote_results.html loads without a quoteRequestId, the page controller shows an error state explaining that no quote request was specified and prompts the user to return to the Get a Quote flow.
2. If getQuotePlansForRequest returns successfully but the plans array is empty, the page shows an empty-state message indicating that no plans were generated for this request and suggests returning to get_quote.html or adjusting inputs.
3. Sorting controls remain visible but disabled or hidden when there are no plans, depending on implementation.
interactions: On page init, read quoteRequestId, sortBy, and sortDirection from the URL, validate quoteRequestId presence, and call getQuotePlansForRequest.
On successful response from getQuotePlansForRequest, update in-memory state: store quoteRequest, plans, availableSortOptions, and appliedSort, then render summary and plans.

On error from getQuotePlansForRequest (e.g., invalid quoteRequestId or network failure), show an error state component with retry capability (re-calling getQuotePlansForRequest with current URL params).
On changing sortBy in the sort control, update the sortBy URL parameter, optionally reset sortDirection to a default (e.g., ascending) in the URL, then re-fetch via getQuotePlansForRequest.
On toggling sortDirection, update sortDirection in the URL while keeping quoteRequestId and sortBy unchanged, then re-fetch via getQuotePlansForRequest.
On clicking a plan card, navigate to quote_detail.html with quotePlanId set to the id of the selected QuotePlan.

On browser back/forward navigation that changes URL parameters, re-run the page init logic (re-reading quoteRequestId, sortBy, sortDirection) and re-call getQuotePlansForRequest so the rendered list always matches the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state is driven by URL parameters and the response of getQuotePlansForRequest. When the page loads or when the URL changes:
1. quoteRequestId (required): If present, it is used as the main key to request plans. If missing or empty, the page shows a blocking error state instead of calling getQuotePlansForRequest.
2. sortBy (optional): If provided in the URL, it is passed directly into getQuotePlansForRequest. If omitted, the page passes a null or undefined sortBy, relying on the backend to apply its default sort and return appliedSort reflecting that default. The UI shows appliedSort.sortBy as the active selection.
3. sortDirection (optional): If provided, it is passed into getQuotePlansForRequest. If omitted, the backend applies its default direction, reflected back in appliedSort.sortDirection.
4. The getQuotePlansForRequest response becomes the single source of truth for current sort state (appliedSort) and available sort options (availableSortOptions). The sort UI does not assume or hardcode any sort options; it binds directly to availableSortOptions.
5. Any change to sorting is done by manipulating sortBy and/or sortDirection in the URL while keeping quoteRequestId unchanged. After updating the URL, the page controller re-invokes getQuotePlansForRequest with the new parameters.
6. The list of plans displayed is always the last plans array returned by getQuotePlansForRequest for the currently active URL parameters. No localStorage is used; all state beyond what is in the URL is transient in-memory state scoped to the page instance.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, reads URL parameters, calls getQuotePlansForRequest, and manages global loading, success, empty, and error states for the Quote Results page.
event_handlers: onInit: Read quoteRequestId, sortBy, sortDirection from the URL. If quoteRequestId is missing, switch to error state without calling getQuotePlansForRequest. If present, call getQuotePlansForRequest({ quoteRequestId, sortBy, sortDirection }).

onGetQuotePlansSuccess: When getQuotePlansForRequest resolves, store quoteRequest, plans, availableSortOptions, appliedSort in page state. Based on plans length, set view mode to 'results' or 'empty'. Notify child components (summary, sort bar, list) to render or update.
onGetQuotePlansError: On API error, set view mode to 'error' with an error message and optionally error details; ensure no stale plans are shown.
onRetryFetch: Triggered from the error state component; re-calls getQuotePlansForRequest with the current URL parameters.
onUrlChange: Triggered on browser back/forward or programmatic URL updates; re-reads quoteRequestId, sortBy, sortDirection from the URL and re-runs onInit logic.

components.functionality: Displays a concise summary of the QuoteRequest associated with the current plans to give context about the property and inputs used to generate the quotes.
event_handlers: onQuoteRequestUpdate: When the page controller supplies a new quoteRequest object from getQuotePlansForRequest, update the displayed fields (e.g., zip_code, property_type, roof_area_sqft, average_monthly_bill, desired_system_size_kw, created_at).
onVisibilityChange: Show the summary when a valid quoteRequest is available; hide or show a placeholder when it is not.

components.functionality: Provides controls to select sorting criteria and direction for the quote plans, driven entirely by availableSortOptions and appliedSort from getQuotePlansForRequest.
event_handlers: onOptionsUpdate: When availableSortOptions changes (from the latest getQuotePlansForRequest response), populate the sort dropdown or equivalent control with these options' labels and values.
onAppliedSortUpdate: When appliedSort changes, update the selected sort option and direction indicators in the UI; do not override values returned by the API.

onSortByChange: When the user selects a different sort option value, update the sortBy URL parameter, optionally resetting sortDirection if required by business rules, then trigger page-controller onUrlChange (which re-calls getQuotePlansForRequest).
onSortDirectionToggle: When the user toggles the sort direction, update sortDirection in the URL while preserving quoteRequestId and current sortBy, then trigger page-controller onUrlChange.
onDisableControlsForNoPlans: When notified that plans array is empty, either disable or hide the sort controls to avoid meaningless sort changes.

components.functionality: Renders the list of QuotePlan cards based on the plans array returned by getQuotePlansForRequest, in the order provided by the API and appliedSort.
event_handlers: onPlansUpdate: When a new plans array is provided by the page controller, clear any existing list rendering and render a list item (plan card) for each plan. The rendering order follows the array order exactly.
onPlanClick: When a plan card is clicked, extract the plan.id from the bound QuotePlan and navigate to quote_detail.html with quotePlanId set to that id in the URL.
onEmptyPlans: If plans is an empty array, hide the list and notify the page controller or an empty-state component to show an appropriate message.
onRefreshRequest: If requested by the page controller (for example after a sort change), re-bind to the updated plans array and re-render.

components.functionality: Represents a single QuotePlan entry within the plans list, exposing key metrics and a single click target to open quote details.
event_handlers: onRender: Bind visible fields (e.g., name, description, system_size_kw, total_price, estimated_monthly_payment, loan_term_years, apr, battery_included, battery_capacity_kwh, inverter_type, created_at) directly from the QuotePlan instance without hardcoded example values.
onClick: When the user clicks the card or a primary action within it, call the quote-plans-list onPlanClick handler with the current QuotePlan.id.
onHover: Optionally highlight card state on hover to indicate interactivity (no data changes, purely behavioral).
onAccessibilityFocus: Ensure the card is focusable and supports keyboard activation to trigger the same navigation as onClick.

components.functionality: Shows a loading state while getQuotePlansForRequest is in progress, and hides when results or an error are available.
event_handlers: onFetchStart: When the page controller calls getQuotePlansForRequest, display the loading indicator and hide results, empty, and error states.
onFetchEnd: When getQuotePlansForRequest resolves or rejects, hide the loading indicator and notify other state components (results, empty, error) to render accordingly.

components.functionality: Displays a message or guidance when getQuotePlansForRequest succeeds but returns no plans for the current QuoteRequest.
event_handlers: onPlansEmpty: Triggered when the page controller detects plans is an empty array. Show an informational message referencing the current quoteRequest context so the user understands which request produced no plans.
onHideWhenPlansExist: Hide the empty-state component whenever plans is non-empty or when an error or loading state is active.

components.functionality: Displays error information when quoteRequestId is missing or when getQuotePlansForRequest fails, and provides a retry option when appropriate.
event_handlers: onMissingQuoteRequestId: When the page controller detects that quoteRequestId is absent or invalid in the URL on init, show an error message explaining that the page requires a valid quote request identifier.
onApiError: When getQuotePlansForRequest rejects or returns an error, show a generic error message. Optionally, log or display non-sensitive error details in a controlled way.
onRetryClick: When the user clicks a retry control, invoke page-controller onRetryFetch to call getQuotePlansForRequest again with the current URL parameters.
onHideForSuccess: Hide the error-state component whenever a valid response with quoteRequest and plans is available.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: (a) a full-width top info bar and (b) a full-width white navigation bar remain part of the global site chrome, (c) a full-width hero image section introduces the quote results with a bold, centered headline, and (d) directly below the hero, a two-column main content section appears. In this main section, the left column (roughly two-thirds of the content width) contains all dynamic quote results controls and output (sort bar and plans list), while the right column (roughly one-third) is a single, structured card that summarizes the quote request context. Within the left column, the sort bar appears at the top, followed immediately by the list of quote plan cards, echoing the mockup’s pattern of media/content-first on the left and a single functional card on the right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: (a) a full-width top info bar and (b) a full-width white navigation bar remain part of the global site chrome, (c) a full-width hero image section introduces the quote results with a bold, centered headline, and (d) directly below the hero, a two-column main content section appears. In this main section, the left column (roughly two-thirds of the content width) contains all dynamic quote results controls and output (sort bar and plans list), while the right column (roughly one-third) is a single, structured card that summarizes the quote request context. Within the left column, the sort bar appears at the top, followed immediately by the list of quote plan cards, echoing the mockup’s pattern of media/content-first on the left and a single functional card on the right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The shallow top info bar and slightly taller navigation bar together occupy about 18–22% of the initial viewport height. The hero section consumes around 40–45% of the viewport height, ensuring the 'Quote Results' headline and supporting copy dominate the above-the-fold experience. Below the hero, a main content container with a max-width (around 1140–1200px) is centered on the page. Inside this container, the left column takes about 65–70% of the width and the right column about 30–35%, mirroring the mockup’s image-plus-sidebar layout. Vertical gaps between hero and main content, and between major blocks (hero → main, main → footer) use 48–64px, while internal gaps (such as between sort bar and list, and between cards) are 16–24px in line with the 8px-based spacing scale. Cards like the quote request summary and each quote plan card use 24–32px internal padding on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero remains visually open and spacious with large typography and generous padding over a darkened image overlay. The main content area below becomes moderately dense: the sort bar and quote plan cards introduce more information per vertical slice, but consistent padding, clear grouping, and sufficient whitespace between cards (16–24px) prevent clutter. The right sidebar summary card is compact yet readable, with neatly stacked text and key figures separated by comfortable vertical spacing. Overall, the layout maintains a corporate marketing feel: enough information on screen to be useful and comparison-friendly, while still feeling airy and legible.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the mockup’s Z-pattern. The user’s eye starts at the top left with logo/address in the global chrome, moves right across the contact info and primary header CTA, then drops diagonally into the center of the hero where the bold 'Your Solar Quote Results' headline sits over the image. From there the flow moves down and left to the top of the main content where the sort bar and first plan card appear, then travels right to the elevated quote request summary card in the sidebar. As users scroll, their attention continues in a zig-zag: down through the left-column plan cards, periodically shifting right to cross-reference details in the sticky summary card. During loading, empty, or error states, the left column remains the focus, with the right-hand summary still available as stable context.
