layout.overall_description: Below the familiar full-width top info bar, white navigation bar, and a bold hero image introducing the quote results, the main content settles into a centered, two-column layout on a clean white background. A wide content container spans roughly 1140–1200px and is horizontally centered, with a generous 48–64px gap below the hero. Inside this container, the left column occupies about two-thirds of the width and begins with a horizontal sort bar that stretches edge-to-edge within the column. Directly beneath it, a vertical list of quote plan cards fills the column, each card spanning its full width with comfortable padding and slightly elevated from the background. To the right, taking the remaining one-third of the width, a single, prominent card summarizes the quote request details—location, roof type, usage, and time horizon—stacked in neat rows. This sidebar card aligns near the top of the left column content and remains in view (via a sticky position) while the user scrolls the left-column results, allowing quick cross-checking of plan assumptions. If the system is loading, or if there are no results or an error, the left column’s results region is replaced with a centered state message or indicator, while the right-hand summary card stays pinned as a stable anchor. The overall feel is that of a professional, corporate solar services site: clear hierarchy, a strong hero moment, and a practical two-column comparison layout that is information-rich but still comfortably spaced.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller occupies the entire main content area beneath the hero section, acting as a full-width container that manages what appears in the left-column results zone (list, loading, empty, or error). Visually, it is not a styled element itself but defines the central content region: a max-width container of about 1140–1200px, centered within the viewport, with 48–64px of vertical separation from the bottom of the hero. Within this container, it establishes a two-column grid: approximately 65–70% width for the left column (results and sort bar) and 30–35% for the right column (quote request summary). It also maintains consistent horizontal gutters (around 24–32px) between the two columns to preserve the clean marketing feel. All other components on this page are positioned relative to this controller’s grid and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width wrapper inside the main page body; it controls conditional rendering of child components within the left column but does not itself appear as a visible UI element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote request context summary appears as a prominent card in the right column of the main content, starting near the top edge of the content container so that its top is roughly aligned with the top of the sort bar in the left column. It occupies about 30–35% of the content container’s width and spans vertically based on its content, typically reaching between one-half and two-thirds the height of the initially visible left-column results. The card has 24–32px of internal padding on all sides and 16–24px of vertical spacing between its internal rows (e.g., location, usage, system size assumptions). It is offset from the container’s right edge by standard grid gutters (16–24px) so it doesn’t touch the viewport edge on smaller screens. Against the white page background, the summary card uses a subtle shadow or border and a slightly elevated look to visually echo the mockup’s 'Categories' sidebar card. As the user scrolls, this card remains pinned in place relative to the viewport, staying visible alongside the scrolling list of plans on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned in the right column with position: sticky; it stays fixed within the viewport once scrolled into place, with a top offset (e.g., 80–96px) so it doesn’t overlap the header, while the left column content scrolls independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort bar is located at the very top of the left column, directly below the bottom edge of the hero section and aligned with the left edge of the main content container. It spans the full width of the left column (roughly 65–70% of the content width), forming a horizontal band of controls where sorting and filtering options are clearly laid out. The bar has 16–24px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, maintaining the compact yet readable density of the mockup’s info-heavy top elements. There is a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px between the bottom of the sort bar and the first quote plan card in the list, visually separating controls from content. On larger screens, the sort bar’s height is relatively shallow compared to the hero but visually denser, anchoring the top-left of the main content in the Z-pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column’s normal flow; scrolls out of view as the user moves down the list. It does not overlay other elements and always precedes the list and state components within the left column region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote plans list sits directly beneath the sort bar in the left column, filling the entire available width of that column (about two-thirds of the main content container). It begins approximately 16–24px below the sort bar, with a clear visual break so users perceive it as the primary results area. The list is a vertical stack of quote plan cards, each spanning the full width of the column with consistent horizontal alignment to the sort bar above. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing between each card, maintaining an organized but moderately dense feel suitable for comparing multiple plans. The list extends downward as far as needed, with the right-column summary card visible alongside the upper portion of the list and then remaining sticky as the user continues to scroll. On smaller screens, this list will typically stack above the summary card, but within the desktop layout it remains firmly anchored as the dominant content in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling vertically within the left column. It is conditionally replaced or visually hidden when loading, empty, or error states are active, but otherwise occupies the full vertical span beneath the sort bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each quote plan card is a full-width card within the left-column list, aligned flush to the list’s left and right edges with consistent internal padding of 24–32px on all sides. The cards are rectangular with slightly rounded corners and a subtle shadow or border, visually similar in elevation to the sidebar summary card but slightly less prominent. Internally, key plan details—headline (plan name), estimated monthly savings, system size, payback period, and a call-to-action button—are arranged in a clean, top-to-bottom and left-to-right hierarchy, with 8–16px gaps between text lines and 16–24px between main content blocks within the card. Cards are separated from each other vertically by 16–24px of whitespace, creating clear visual breaks while still allowing several cards to appear within a single viewport. On hover, cards may show a slight elevation or border color change to reinforce clickability without breaking the restrained corporate style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static elements within the scrollable left-column list; they do not stick or float. They expand vertically based on content but always maintain full-width alignment within the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears in the same left-column space normally occupied by the quote plans list, directly beneath the sort bar. When active, it replaces the list content so that the area below the sort bar is dominated by a centered spinner or progress motif, with a short status message beneath it. The indicator is horizontally centered within the left column and surrounded by generous vertical padding—typically at least 64–96px above and below—to create a calm, uncluttered state while results are being fetched. The right-column quote request summary card remains visible and unchanged, providing contextual reassurance and preventing the page from feeling empty.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the quote-plans-list within the left column’s normal flow; static and centered. It scrolls with the page like regular content and disappears once data has loaded and the list is rendered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state occupies the left-column results area when no quote plans match the current filters or request. It is positioned directly below the sort bar and uses a centered layout within the left column: an optional light illustration or icon at the top, a concise headline explaining that no plans are available, and a short paragraph or guidance text below, each separated by 8–16px of vertical spacing. This content block is wrapped in generous vertical whitespace (at least 64px top and bottom) to make the state feel intentional and calm rather than like a broken UI. The empty state width is constrained to roughly 60–70% of the left column and centered to avoid feeling too stretched, while the right-column summary card remains in place for reference, showing the parameters that led to the empty result.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed in place of the quote-plans-list within the left column’s normal flow; static and vertically scrollable as part of the page. It does not overlay the summary card and does not affect the position of the sort bar above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state also sits in the left-column results area under the sort bar, replacing the list when an error occurs. It consists of a prominent alert-style card or message block that spans most of the left column’s width (around 80–90%) and is horizontally centered. Inside this block, an error icon, a short bold headline, and a more detailed explanation or recovery instructions are stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing. The block is surrounded by comfortable margins—24–32px around the card and 48–64px of open space above and below—to keep the page readable even when showing a problem. The right-column summary card stays visible, allowing users (and support staff) to see the original quote context that led to the error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the quote-plans-list within the left column’s flow; static and scrollable. It does not become sticky or overlay other components but clearly occupies the primary attention area beneath the sort bar.

name: Quote Detail
title: Quote Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected solar quote plan, allows saving the quote, and provides actions to print, download, or continue to consultation and financing based on this plan.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific QuotePlan identified by the quotePlanId URL parameter using getQuotePlanDetail.
Show pricing and payment summary including total project cost, estimated monthly payment, current average bill from the originating QuoteRequest, and a calculated monthly difference.
Display equipment and system configuration details such as system size, panel model, panel count, battery inclusion and capacity, and inverter type.
Display financing-related assumptions from the quote plan such as loan term and APR when available.
Show context for how the quote was created (from quote tool, solar package, or system design) using sourceType, sourcePackage, and sourceSystemDesign.
Allow the user to save the current quote plan to their quote list with an optional nickname via saveQuotePlanToList.
Provide actions to print the quote details using the browser’s print capabilities.
Provide actions to download the quote details as a file generated from the currently displayed quote data.
Provide navigation to schedule a consultation for this quote, passing the relevant ZIP and property type via URL parameters when available.
Provide navigation to explore financing options for this quote, passing the total project cost and ZIP via URL parameters when available.
Handle loading, error, and missing-parameter states when retrieving the quote details.
user_workflows:
View a quote’s details:
1) User navigates to quote_detail.html with a quotePlanId URL parameter.
2) On page initialization, the page reads quotePlanId from the URL.
3) The page calls getQuotePlanDetail with quotePlanId.
4) While the request is in progress, a loading state is shown.
5) On success, the plan, quoteRequest, panelModel, batteryOption, sourceType, sourcePackage, and sourceSystemDesign are stored in page state.
6) The quote summary, pricing/payment, equipment details, financing terms, and source context components render using the loaded data.
7) If the request fails, an error message is shown with an option to retry the load.

Save a quote to the quote list:
1) With quote details loaded, the user clicks the "Save quote" action in the actions bar.
2) A modal or inline form opens, showing the quote name (from the plan) and an optional nickname input field.
3) The user optionally enters a nickname and submits the form.
4) The page calls saveQuotePlanToList with the current quotePlanId and the entered nickname value.
5) While saving, the save button in the form is disabled and a submitting state is shown.
6) On success, the returned SavedQuote is stored in page state, the form closes (or shows an inline success state), and the actions bar updates to reflect that the quote is saved.
7) On error, an error message is displayed in the form and the user can retry.

Print quote details:
1) With quote details loaded, the user clicks the "Print" action.
2) The page ensures the latest plan and related data are rendered.
3) The page triggers the browser print dialog so that the currently rendered quote details can be printed.
4) No additional API calls are made; printing uses the existing loaded state.

Download quote details:
1) With quote details loaded, the user clicks the "Download" action.
2) The page generates a downloadable representation of the quote from the current plan, quoteRequest, panelModel, and batteryOption data (for example, a document or file assembled client-side).
3) The browser download flow starts, allowing the user to save the quote file locally.
4) No additional API calls are made; the download uses the existing loaded state.

Schedule a consultation from a quote:
1) With quote details loaded, the user clicks the "Schedule consultation" action.
2) The page checks that the quoteRequest object exists and that zip_code and property_type are available.
3) If the required fields are available, the page constructs a URL to consultation_booking.html including query parameters zip (from quoteRequest.zip_code), propertyType (from quoteRequest.property_type), and source set to "quote_detail".
4) The browser navigates to that URL.
5) If the required fields are not available, the action is disabled or an explanatory message is shown instead of navigating.

Explore financing for this quote:
1) With quote details loaded, the user clicks the "Explore financing for this quote" action.
2) The page checks that the quoteRequest object exists and that quoteRequest.zip_code is available, and that plan.total_price is available.
3) If the required fields are available, the page constructs a URL to financing_overview.html including query parameters projectCost (from plan.total_price) and zip (from quoteRequest.zip_code).
4) The browser navigates to that URL.
5) If the required fields are not available, the action is disabled or an explanatory message is shown instead of navigating.
interactions: On page load (or when quotePlanId in the URL changes), call getQuotePlanDetail with the quotePlanId to populate the page.

If getQuotePlanDetail succeeds, update page state with the returned plan, quoteRequest, panelModel, batteryOption, sourceType, sourcePackage, and sourceSystemDesign and render all dependent components.
If getQuotePlanDetail fails, show an error message and a "Retry" control that re-invokes getQuotePlanDetail with the same quotePlanId.
When the user clicks "Save quote", open a save form (modal or inline) bound to the current quotePlanId and an editable nickname field.
When the user modifies the nickname field, update the local form state used for the saveQuotePlanToList call.
When the user submits the save form, call saveQuotePlanToList with the current quotePlanId and nickname; show a submitting state until the call resolves.
On successful saveQuotePlanToList, store the returned SavedQuote in page state, update the actions bar to reflect that the quote is saved, and show a success message.
On saveQuotePlanToList error, show validation or generic error messages and keep the form open for correction and retry.
When the user clicks the "Print" button, trigger the browser print dialog for the current page view without additional data calls.
When the user clicks the "Download" button, generate a downloadable file representing the current quote details and start the browser download; no new API calls are made.

When the user clicks "Schedule consultation", if quoteRequest.zip_code and quoteRequest.property_type are available, navigate to consultation_booking.html with those values and source=quote_detail as URL parameters.

When the user clicks "Explore financing for this quote", if quoteRequest.zip_code and plan.total_price are available, navigate to financing_overview.html with projectCost and zip URL parameters derived from those values.
If quotePlanId is missing from the URL, display a missing-parameter error state and do not call getQuotePlanDetail until a valid quotePlanId is provided (e.g., via navigation or URL correction).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by the quotePlanId URL parameter. On initialization, the page reads quotePlanId from the query string. If quotePlanId is present, the page calls getQuotePlanDetail(quotePlanId). While the call is in progress, the page is in a loading state; once the call resolves successfully, the state transitions to a loaded state containing plan (QuotePlan), quoteRequest (QuoteRequest, optional), panelModel (optional), batteryOption (optional), sourceType (optional), sourcePackage (optional), and sourceSystemDesign (optional). Components subscribe to this state and render accordingly. If the call fails, the state transitions to an error state with an associated error message, and a retry action can transition back to the loading state and re-invoke getQuotePlanDetail with the same quotePlanId. If quotePlanId is missing or empty, the page enters a missing-parameter state, displaying an appropriate message and not calling getQuotePlanDetail. The save operation maintains its own sub-state (idle, submitting, success, error) tied to the currently loaded plan.id; submitting triggers saveQuotePlanToList(quotePlanId, nickname). On success, the returned SavedQuote is stored in state, and the UI updates (for example, disabling or relabeling the save button). Navigation to consultation_booking.html and financing_overview.html is computed from the loaded state and expressed purely as URL query parameters; no localStorage is used. Refreshing the page with the same quotePlanId simply repeats the getQuotePlanDetail flow and reconstructs state from the interface response.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the quotePlanId URL parameter, calling getQuotePlanDetail, and managing loading, success, and error states. Exposes the loaded plan, quoteRequest, panelModel, batteryOption, sourceType, sourcePackage, and sourceSystemDesign to child components.

Displays a concise summary of the quote including the plan’s name or identifier, system size, property type (if available from quoteRequest), and basic metadata such as created_at. Provides a high-level overview that confirms the user is looking at the correct quote.

Shows core financial information for the quote: total project price (from plan.total_price), estimated monthly payment (from plan.estimated_monthly_payment), and the originating average monthly bill (from quoteRequest.average_monthly_bill when available). Calculates and displays a monthly difference between the current bill and the estimated solar payment as a simple savings-related indicator.

Displays equipment details for the quote, including system size (plan.system_size_kw), panel count (plan.panel_count), panel model information (from panelModel such as name, manufacturer, wattage, and efficiency_percent when available), battery inclusion and capacity (plan.battery_included, plan.battery_capacity_kwh, and batteryOption fields when available), and inverter type (plan.inverter_type).

Shows financing-related assumptions baked into the quote, including plan.loan_term_years and plan.apr when these values are provided. If these fields are absent, the component indicates that financing terms are not specified in this quote.

Displays context on how the quote was generated using sourceType. If the quote is from the quote tool, it highlights that it is based on the associated QuoteRequest. If it is from a solar_package, it shows key information from sourcePackage (such as name, system_type, and system_size_kw). If it is from a system_design, it shows key information from sourceSystemDesign (such as system_type, panel_quantity, and inverter_type). Optionally provides navigation actions to view the originating package or system design using their identifiers in URLs.

Provides primary actions for the current quote: save the quote, print the quote, download the quote, schedule a consultation, and explore financing. Each action uses current page state or navigates with URL parameters constructed from the loaded plan and quoteRequest.

Provides a form to save the currently viewed quote to the quote list. Allows the user to enter an optional nickname and then calls saveQuotePlanToList with the current quotePlanId and nickname. Displays submission progress, success messages, and error messages based on the interface response.

Handles and displays states where quotePlanId is missing from the URL or where getQuotePlanDetail returns an error. Shows appropriate messages and a control to retry loading the quote details when possible.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the mockup’s linear flow: global top info bar and navigation (shared across the site), then a full-width hero section dedicated to the quote summary header, and below that a two-column main content area. In the hero, the quote summary is centered over a subdued background image/gradient. In the main content, the left column (about two-thirds width) stacks the core quote detail panels (pricing & payment, equipment, financing, source context) from top to bottom. The right column (about one-third width) holds a single, structured quote actions card, visually similar to the 'Categories' card in the mockup. Loading, error, and modal states overlay or replace this structure when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the mockup’s linear flow: global top info bar and navigation (shared across the site), then a full-width hero section dedicated to the quote summary header, and below that a two-column main content area. In the hero, the quote summary is centered over a subdued background image/gradient. In the main content, the left column (about two-thirds width) stacks the core quote detail panels (pricing & payment, equipment, financing, source context) from top to bottom. The right column (about one-third width) holds a single, structured quote actions card, visually similar to the 'Categories' card in the mockup. Loading, error, and modal states overlay or replace this structure when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section for the quote summary occupies roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height, visually dominated by a large headline and key quote metadata. The main two-column content area fills the remainder, with the left column taking about 65–70% width and the right sidebar 30–35%. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero → main content, between stacked panels) are in the 48–64px range. Each panel/card (summary, details, actions) uses 24–32px internal padding. The data loader and save-quote modal are full-viewport overlays, centered content, and temporarily override normal space allocation. The error/empty state consolidates content into a central message card, reducing multi-column complexity when the quote data is unavailable.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero section remains open and spacious with large typography and generous padding around the quote summary, matching the marketing feel. The main content is moderately dense: multiple stacked panels in the left column, but each panel has clear padding, distinct headings, and consistent spacing between sections (16–24px inside). The sidebar action card is slightly lighter in content density, with a tight cluster of primary CTAs and secondary actions but ample inner padding so it never feels cramped. Loader and modal states have minimal surrounding clutter, focusing attention on a single task or message.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern: starting at the top left with the logo/info bar, sweeping right across the navigation and any top-right CTAs, then diagonally down into the centered hero quote headline. From there, the eye moves left into the dominant left-column panel (pricing & payment summary as the main anchor), then shifts to the right to the quote actions card. Within the left column, users scroll downward through increasingly detailed content (equipment specifics, financing terms, source context). The right column’s sticky actions card stays present to continuously attract attention to saving, downloading, printing, or continuing to consultation/financing. When active, the loader, modal, or error state temporarily take over the visual flow as the primary focus.

layout.overall_description: Below the site-wide info bar and white navigation, the page opens into a full-width hero area with a subtle solar-themed background image and a semi-transparent dark overlay. Centered within this hero, the quote summary header presents the quote title, location, system size, and key highlights in large, bold white text, vertically centered so it dominates the above-the-fold view. Scrolling down past a comfortable vertical gap, the layout transitions into a two-column content band on a clean white background. On the left, a wide main column holds a prominent pricing and payment summary card at the top, acting as the visual anchor for the quote. Directly beneath, additional cards for equipment details, financing terms, and source context stack with consistent spacing, building detail as the user moves down the page. On the right, a narrower sidebar begins at the same vertical level as the pricing summary and contains a single elevated quote actions card, visually similar to a classic WordPress sidebar widget. This actions card gathers the key controls—save, print, download, and continue to consultation/financing—in an organized, vertically stacked format, and remains visible as the user scrolls via a sticky behavior. When loading, a dim overlay and centered loader sit above all content; when errors occur or data is missing, the main content area is replaced by a centered message card. Triggering 'Save Quote' brings up a centered modal form on a darkened backdrop, keeping the user focused on completing that single task while the underlying two-column layout softly recedes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-detail-loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that sits above the hero and the two-column content area but below the global site header/navigation if desired. It covers the entire content region from the bottom edge of the navigation bar to the bottom of the viewport, with a semi-transparent dark backing that subtly mutes the underlying quote details. At the visual center of the viewport, a compact loading indicator (spinner or progress graphic) is placed, accompanied by a short status message directly beneath it. The vertical spacing around the loader content is generous, allowing that central element to feel isolated and clearly primary. When active, interactions with all underlying components (summary header, panels, sidebar actions) are visually and functionally disabled, but the overall page structure remains faintly visible behind the overlay to reassure users that their quote is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay that covers the full content area; pinned to the viewport so it remains centered regardless of scroll position. It appears and disappears based on loading state and prevents interaction with underlying content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-summary-header sits inside a dedicated hero section immediately below the site’s navigation bar, spanning the full width of the page’s main content container. The hero occupies approximately 40–45% of the viewport height on desktop. A solar-themed background image or gradient runs edge-to-edge, with a dark blue overlay to ensure text contrast. Within this hero, the quote-summary-header content is vertically centered, with its main headline centered horizontally on the page. The headline uses large, bold typography, stretching roughly 60–70% of the page width and is aligned center to reinforce the hero feel. Below the headline, a subheading and a concise row or stack of key quote highlights (e.g., system size, estimated savings, location) are placed with 16–24px vertical spacing between lines. These secondary details can either be center-aligned in a single row or split into two small columns centered beneath the headline, but overall the block remains visually compact and centrally anchored. Generous top and bottom padding (32–48px) within the hero gives this header breathing room and ensures it reads as the primary introduction to the quote detail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal page flow as a hero section; it remains at the top of the content stack and scrolls out of view as the user moves down to the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing-and-payment-summary is the first and most prominent card in the main two-column content area directly below the hero. After a vertical gap of roughly 48–64px from the bottom of the hero, the page transitions to a white background main container where this panel occupies the top of the left column. The left column spans about 65–70% of the total content width (approximately 8 of 12 grid columns), aligned flush with the main content’s left margin. The panel itself is a full-width card within this column, featuring a subtle elevation (drop shadow or outlined card) and 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Inside, a bold section title is placed at the top left of the card, followed by a high-contrast price figure and monthly payment information, arranged either in a two-column internal layout (total cost on the left, monthly payments and key terms on the right) or in a stacked pattern with clear hierarchy. Spacing between inner elements respects a 16–24px rhythm. This card is tall enough to feel substantial but remains above the fold alongside at least the top of the sidebar actions card, reinforcing it as the main informational anchor of the quote.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the left column, participating in the document flow. It pushes subsequent panels down and aligns its top edge horizontally with the top of the sidebar actions card for a clean row alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The equipment-details-panel is placed directly beneath the pricing-and-payment-summary within the left column, aligned to the same left and right edges. A vertical gap of roughly 24–32px separates it from the summary panel, maintaining consistent visual rhythm. The panel is a card-style container with a white background and subtle shadow or border, matching the styling of the pricing card but likely shorter in height. It spans the full width of the left column (about 65–70% of the page width), with 24–32px of internal padding. At the top left of the card, a section title (e.g., 'Equipment Details') is followed by structured content such as a small grid or list describing panels, inverters, and other hardware. Items may be organized in two columns internally for readability, but the outer container remains single-column within the page layout. Vertical spacing between pieces of equipment information is in the 16–24px range, keeping the density moderate but readable. As the user scrolls, this card naturally follows the pricing summary, creating a clear step down into more technical detail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the left column. It scrolls normally with the page and maintains constant margins relative to the pricing-and-payment-summary above and the financing-terms-panel below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing-terms-panel sits below the equipment-details-panel within the same left column, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px. It uses the same card treatment—white background, subtle elevation or border, and 24–32px internal padding—to maintain a cohesive visual system. The panel’s width matches the column width, extending across roughly 65–70% of the page. A clear section heading appears at the top left of the card, with key financing terms laid out in either short paragraphs or a structured list/table. If a table layout is used, it stays within the card’s padding, not reaching the outer edges, preserving whitespace. Internal spacing between headings, terms, and any explanatory notes follows the 16–24px vertical rhythm, making potentially dense information easier to scan. This panel is roughly similar in height to or slightly taller than the equipment details card, depending on content length, forming a steady vertical block of information in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the left column. It scrolls with the rest of the content and leads directly into the source-context-panel below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The source-context-panel is the final content card in the left column, positioned directly beneath the financing-terms-panel with a 24–32px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the page width) and shares the same card styling—white background, subtle shadow or border, and 24–32px padding. This panel is text-heavy, used to explain how the quote was generated (assumptions, data sources, modeling context). A section title at the top left is followed by brief paragraphs or bullet points. Content is aligned left, with a comfortable line length and 16–24px spacing between paragraphs to avoid clutter. As the bottom-most card, it leaves a 48–64px margin below it before the end of the main content area, creating a clean visual conclusion to the left column’s stacked content. The overall height of this panel depends on the explanation length but is visually balanced with the card stack above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element at the bottom of the left column. It terminates the scrollable content region for detailed information, with no special sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-actions-bar occupies the right-hand sidebar column of the main content area. This right column begins at the same vertical level as the top edge of the pricing-and-payment-summary, forming a clean horizontal row alignment between the first left and right elements. The sidebar is approximately 30–35% of the page width (about 4 of 12 grid columns), aligned flush to the main content’s right margin. Within this column, the quote-actions-bar is implemented as a tall, single card with a white or very light background, elevated slightly with a shadow or border. The card uses 24–32px of padding and contains a vertically stacked set of controls: a primary button (e.g., 'Continue to Consultation' or 'Continue to Financing') near the top, followed by prominent secondary actions ('Save Quote', 'Download PDF', 'Print Quote') and possibly smaller tertiary links. Between each button or group of actions is 16–24px of vertical spacing, ensuring clarity and touch-friendly sizing. The card’s content is center-aligned horizontally within the card, while the card itself is aligned to the top of the sidebar column, creating a visual counterpart to the large pricing card on the left. As the user scrolls down the left column panels, this action card remains visible, maintaining easy access to the main CTAs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky sidebar card: within desktop view, it uses CSS position: sticky (or equivalent) with a top offset (e.g., 24–32px below the bottom of the navigation/hero) so that once scrolled into view, it stays pinned within the viewport while the left column scrolls. On smaller screens, this may collapse into a full-width bar placed above or below the main content in normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-quote-modal appears as a centered overlay on top of the existing page content when the user selects 'Save Quote' from the quote actions bar. A semi-transparent dark backdrop covers the entire viewport, subtly dimming both the hero and the two-column content beneath. At the center of the screen, a modal card with a white background and gentle shadow is presented. On desktop, the modal width is about 480–560px (roughly 40–50% of the viewport width), with 32px padding on all sides. The modal’s header (title such as 'Save this Quote') sits at the top, left-aligned, with a close icon in the top-right corner, and is separated from the form fields below by 16–24px of vertical space. The form fields (e.g., name, email, label for the quote) are stacked vertically with 16px spacing, and a primary action button (e.g., 'Save Quote') sits at the bottom, aligned to the right or full-width across the modal. There is at least 24–32px of padding between the lowest button and the modal’s bottom edge. On shorter viewports, the modal content scrolls internally while the header stays visible. The overall composition keeps the user’s focus on this task while still giving a faint sense of the page context in the background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, centered overlay that sits above all other page content, including the loader when active. It is pinned to the viewport center and remains in place regardless of scroll position; underlying content is non-interactive while the modal is open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-and-empty-state panel replaces the standard two-column content layout when no quote data can be shown (e.g., the loader fails or the quote is invalid). The hero with the quote-summary-header may be omitted or reduced in this state; immediately below the navigation, the page presents a single, centered message card within the main content area. This card spans roughly 50–60% of the page width on desktop and is horizontally centered between the margins, leaving wide whitespace on both sides to echo the spacious feel of the hero. It is positioned with a 64px top margin beneath the navigation (or hero if retained), so it feels visually separated and clearly important. The card features 24–32px padding and a simple layout: an icon or status symbol at the top center, a bold heading beneath it (e.g., 'We couldn't load this quote'), and a short explanatory message. If any recovery actions exist (e.g., 'Go back to quotes', 'Try again'), they are placed as buttons or links below the message with 16–24px vertical spacing. The right sidebar and left-column stack are not shown in this state, avoiding unnecessary clutter and concentrating attention on the message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element that conditionally replaces the main content area. It is centered within the content container and scrolls normally if the message content exceeds the viewport height.

name: Solar Packages
title: Solar Packages
description: Allows users to search and filter predefined solar system packages by system type, size, price, warranty, and sorting, then open individual package details.

core_features: Initialize available filter and sort options by calling getSolarPackageFilterOptions and apply any incoming URL parameters (systemType, minSystemSizeKw, maxSystemSizeKw, sortBy) as initial state.
Provide a filter form for system type, system size range (min/max kW), maximum total price, and minimum panel warranty years, mapped to searchSolarPackages parameters.
Provide a sort control bound to the sortOptions returned by getSolarPackageFilterOptions, mapped to searchSolarPackages.sortBy and searchSolarPackages.sortDirection.
Execute package searches using searchSolarPackages whenever filters or sorting change, and display the resulting SolarPackage list and totalCount.
Display each solar package with key attributes (name, system_type, system_size_kw, total_price, panel_warranty_years, and other available fields) using dynamic data from searchSolarPackages.
Allow users to navigate to package_detail.html for a selected package by passing packageId in the URL, supporting both same-tab navigation and opening in a new tab.
Keep the current filter and sort state represented in the page URL query parameters so that the page can be bookmarked, shared, and restored via browser navigation.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing with pre-applied filters from another page
1. User clicks a navigation link or button on index.html that includes a systemType and optional system size range and sortBy as URL parameters.
2. User arrives on solar_packages.html with systemType, minSystemSizeKw, maxSystemSizeKw, and/or sortBy in the URL.
3. The page controller calls getSolarPackageFilterOptions to retrieve available system types, size ranges, price ranges, warranty ranges, and sort options.
4. The page controller parses the URL parameters and merges them with the defaults from getSolarPackageFilterOptions to form the initial filter and sort state.
5. The page controller calls searchSolarPackages with the resolved state (systemType, minSystemSizeKw, maxSystemSizeKw, maxTotalPrice, minPanelWarrantyYears, sortBy, sortDirection).
6. The package list displays the returned packages, and the filter form and sort control show the pre-applied filter values.
7. The user can refine filters or sorting as needed (see Workflow 2).

Workflow 2: Refining filters and sorting on the Solar Packages page
1. User adjusts one or more filter controls in the filter form (e.g., selects a system type, changes min/max system size, sets a maximum total price, or increases the minimum warranty years).
2. On change or on form submission (depending on interaction design), the filter form notifies the page controller of the new filter state.
3. The page controller updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new filter values (systemType, minSystemSizeKw, maxSystemSizeKw, maxTotalPrice, minPanelWarrantyYears) while preserving or updating sortBy and sortDirection.
4. The page controller calls searchSolarPackages with the updated filters and current sort settings.
5. The package list is updated with the new search results, and the result summary shows the updated totalCount from searchSolarPackages.
6. User adjusts the sort control (e.g., selects a different sort option from the options returned by getSolarPackageFilterOptions).
7. The sort control notifies the page controller of the new sortBy (and implied sortDirection, if encoded in the selected option).
8. The page controller updates the URL query parameters for sortBy and sortDirection and calls searchSolarPackages again with the same filters but new sorting.
9. The package list re-renders in the new order.

Workflow 3: Opening a package detail view
1. After reviewing the list, the user locates a specific solar package they are interested in.
2. The user clicks on the package card or a dedicated action within the package list item.
3. The package list component triggers navigation to package_detail.html, appending the selected package's id as the packageId URL parameter.
4. If the user uses a standard click, navigation happens in the same tab; if they use a modifier key (e.g., platform-specific shortcut) or a context menu to open in a new tab, the browser opens package_detail.html in a new tab with the same packageId parameter.
5. The Solar Packages page state remains available in the original tab (or can be restored via the browser back button).

Workflow 4: Restoring state via URL and browser navigation
1. User shares or bookmarks a URL to solar_packages.html that includes filter and sort query parameters.
2. When that URL is later opened, the page controller runs its initialization logic: calls getSolarPackageFilterOptions, then reads the URL query parameters.
3. The controller applies the URL parameters as initial state and calls searchSolarPackages with those values.
4. The filter form and sort control are pre-populated according to the URL parameters, and the displayed package list matches the stored state.
5. When the user changes filters or sorts repeatedly, the controller updates the URL for each change (e.g., via history API), so browser back/forward steps navigate through previous filter/sort states.
6. On a back/forward navigation event, the controller re-reads the URL, updates the filter/sort controls, and re-invokes searchSolarPackages to restore the corresponding results.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load and initialization
- Trigger: Page loads or is reloaded.
- Behavior: The page controller calls getSolarPackageFilterOptions, then parses the URL query parameters (systemType, minSystemSizeKw, maxSystemSizeKw, maxTotalPrice, minPanelWarrantyYears, sortBy, sortDirection). It merges these with the defaults from getSolarPackageFilterOptions to produce the initial state, then calls searchSolarPackages with that state. The resulting packages and totalCount are propagated to the result summary and package list components.

Interaction: Changing system type filter
- Trigger: User selects a system type value from the system type input in the filter form.
- Behavior: The filter form emits an onFilterChange event with the updated systemType. The page controller updates the in-memory filter state and the URL query parameter systemType, then calls searchSolarPackages with the new systemType (and existing other filters and sort settings). The package list re-renders with the updated results.

Interaction: Changing system size range
- Trigger: User adjusts the minimum or maximum system size inputs (e.g., via numeric inputs or a range control) within the bounds defined by sizeRangeKw returned by getSolarPackageFilterOptions.
- Behavior: The filter form validates that minSystemSizeKw is less than or equal to maxSystemSizeKw (if both are set) and updates its internal model. On change or form submit, it emits onFilterChange with updated minSystemSizeKw and maxSystemSizeKw. The page controller updates the corresponding URL parameters and calls searchSolarPackages with these new values.

Interaction: Changing maximum price filter
- Trigger: User sets or adjusts the maximum total price input within the range derived from priceRange returned by getSolarPackageFilterOptions.
- Behavior: The filter form emits onFilterChange with maxTotalPrice. The page controller updates the URL query parameter maxTotalPrice and calls searchSolarPackages using this constraint, refreshing the package list.

Interaction: Changing minimum panel warranty filter
- Trigger: User sets or adjusts the minimum panel warranty years input within the range derived from warrantyRangeYears returned by getSolarPackageFilterOptions.
- Behavior: The filter form emits onFilterChange with minPanelWarrantyYears. The page controller updates the URL query parameter minPanelWarrantyYears and calls searchSolarPackages, updating the displayed packages.

Interaction: Submitting the filter form (if explicit submission is used)
- Trigger: User presses an explicit Submit/Apply button in the filter form or presses Enter while focused inside the form.
- Behavior: The filter form validates all fields (e.g., numeric ranges and required selections if any), then emits onSubmit with the full filter state. The page controller updates all corresponding URL query parameters and calls searchSolarPackages with the full filter set, updating the list and summary.

Interaction: Changing sort option
- Trigger: User selects a sort option from the sort control.
- Behavior: The sort control emits onSortChange with sortBy (and optionally sortDirection derived from the selected option configuration from getSolarPackageFilterOptions). The page controller updates the URL query parameters sortBy and sortDirection, then calls searchSolarPackages with the existing filters and new sorting. The package list is re-rendered in the new order.

Interaction: Clicking a package to view details
- Trigger: User clicks on a package card or a detail link within the package list.
- Behavior: The package list component calls the navigation handler with selectedPackage.id. The page navigates to package_detail.html?packageId=selectedPackage.id in the same tab. If the user uses browser mechanisms to open the link in a new tab, the browser opens package_detail.html with the same packageId parameter in a new tab.

Interaction: Handling no-results state
- Trigger: searchSolarPackages returns totalCount equal to zero.
- Behavior: The package list component renders a functional no-results state, indicating that no packages match the current filters. The filter form remains interactive so the user can broaden filters. The result summary can reflect zero results with the current filter context.

Interaction: Browser back/forward navigation
- Trigger: User uses the browser back or forward buttons.
- Behavior: The page controller listens for URL changes and, upon detection, parses the query parameters again, updates the filter form and sort control state to match the URL, and re-invokes searchSolarPackages to reload results consistent with the navigated state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
State is managed via in-memory objects controlled by the page controller and is always mirrored in the URL query parameters so that it can be reconstructed without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence.

1) URL Parameters used
- systemType: reflects the selected system type filter and maps directly to searchSolarPackages.systemType.
- minSystemSizeKw: reflects the selected minimum system size filter and maps to searchSolarPackages.minSystemSizeKw.
- maxSystemSizeKw: reflects the selected maximum system size filter and maps to searchSolarPackages.maxSystemSizeKw.
- maxTotalPrice: reflects the selected maximum total price filter and maps to searchSolarPackages.maxTotalPrice.
- minPanelWarrantyYears: reflects the selected minimum panel warranty filter and maps to searchSolarPackages.minPanelWarrantyYears.
- sortBy: reflects the selected sort option's value and maps to searchSolarPackages.sortBy. It is initialized from the URL if present; otherwise the controller uses an appropriate default from getSolarPackageFilterOptions.sortOptions.
- sortDirection: reflects ascending/descending direction (if required by searchSolarPackages) and maps to searchSolarPackages.sortDirection. If not explicitly set in the URL, it can be inferred from the default sort option configuration.

2) Initial load logic
- On initial load, the controller calls getSolarPackageFilterOptions to obtain systemTypeOptions, sizeRangeKw, priceRange, warrantyRangeYears, and sortOptions.
- The controller then reads the current URL query parameters. For each supported parameter, if it exists, it is parsed and used as the initial state value. If it does not exist, the controller uses a default derived from the filter options (for example, the default systemType might be left unset or set to a default that is consistent with systemTypeOptions; sortBy may default to one of the sortOptions returned by getSolarPackageFilterOptions).
- After resolving full filter and sort state, the controller calls searchSolarPackages once, passing systemType, minSystemSizeKw, maxSystemSizeKw, maxTotalPrice, minPanelWarrantyYears, sortBy, and sortDirection. The returned packages and totalCount populate the result summary and package list components.

3) State updates and URL synchronization
- Whenever the user changes filter or sort values through the UI, the controller first updates its in-memory state and then synchronizes the URL query parameters to match that state. For example, if a user clears a filter, the corresponding query parameter is removed or set to an empty state according to URL conventions.
- After updating the URL, the controller calls searchSolarPackages with the new state. This ensures that the displayed data is always consistent with the URL and that using the browser's reload or share functions will reproduce the same state.

4) Handling browser navigation events
- When the URL changes due to browser back/forward navigation, the controller re-parses the URL parameters, overwrites its in-memory state with the new values, updates the filter form and sort control to reflect this state, and then calls searchSolarPackages with the parameters derived from the URL.

5) No localStorage usage
- The page does not store any state in localStorage or other persistent browser storage. All restorable state is encoded in the URL query parameters, and any ephemeral UI state (e.g., temporary input while editing before applying filters) is kept only in memory within the corresponding components until it is applied and synchronized to the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, state management, and calls to getSolarPackageFilterOptions and searchSolarPackages. Propagates filter and sort state to child components and handles navigation to package_detail.html.

Displays and manages interactive controls for filtering solar packages by system type, system size range, maximum total price, and minimum panel warranty years. Emits filter changes and submissions to the page controller.
Renders a sort selection control based on sortOptions from getSolarPackageFilterOptions and allows the user to change the sorting of solar packages. Emits sort changes to the page controller.

Displays the total number of matching solar packages and optionally summarizes key active filters (e.g., selected system type or size range) based on current search state and totalCount from searchSolarPackages.

Displays the list of solar packages returned by searchSolarPackages. Each item shows dynamic attributes from the SolarPackage model and supports navigation to package_detail.html for the selected package.
Handles the case where searchSolarPackages returns zero packages by presenting a no-results message and encouraging the user to adjust filters. Only visible when totalCount is zero.

Abstracts reading and writing of filter and sort state to URL query parameters. On initialization and on browser history changes, it parses the URL and notifies the page controller; on filter/sort updates from the controller, it updates the URL accordingly.

Receives package selection events from the package list and performs navigation to the package_detail.html page with the appropriate packageId URL parameter. Supports standard browser behavior for opening in the same or a new tab.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow within the existing site shell: global top info bar, then white navigation bar, then a full-width hero section for the Solar Packages page, and finally the main content area. Inside the main content, a two-column layout is used: a dominant left column for package results and controls related to those results (summary and sorting), and a narrower right column for a single, vertically stacked filter card (the search form), echoing the original ‘Categories’ sidebar card pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow within the existing site shell: global top info bar, then white navigation bar, then a full-width hero section for the Solar Packages page, and finally the main content area. Inside the main content, a two-column layout is used: a dominant left column for package results and controls related to those results (summary and sorting), and a narrower right column for a single, vertically stacked filter card (the search form), echoing the original ‘Categories’ sidebar card pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the hero, the main content container is centered and constrained in width (roughly 1140–1200px). Vertically, there is a generous 48–64px gap between the hero and the start of the Solar Packages content. Horizontally, the main content area is split so the left column occupies about 65–70% of the container width and the right sidebar about 30–35%. Inside each column, cards and sections use 24–32px internal padding. Between stacked elements (summary, sort control, list, etc.) vertical spacing is 16–24px, mirroring the mockup’s internal spacing rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero section above the components remains open and bold, while the Solar Packages area is moderately dense. The result summary and sort control form a fairly compact horizontal bar at the top of the left column. The results list is visually rich but separated into clear cards with adequate whitespace. The filter form in the right sidebar is denser and more utility-focused but sits inside a well-padded card to avoid clutter. The overall effect is professional and business-like: information-rich but not cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention follows the same Z-pattern as the mockup: it starts at the top left with logo and global header, moves right to the main navigation and CTA, then diagonally down to the centered Solar Packages hero headline. From there, the eye moves into the main content: first to the upper-left of the content area where the result summary and sort control live, then down into the left-column list of package cards, and finally across to the right-column filter sidebar card. Within the left column, the summary and sort controls introduce the content; within the right column, the vertical filter card acts as the clear secondary interaction area.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the global blue-accented header and navigation, a full-width hero image with a subtle dark overlay introduces the Solar Packages page, featuring a large white headline centered both horizontally and vertically within the hero. Below this, after a spacious 56px gap, the main content sits in a centered, white background container with a subtle max-width, reinforcing a corporate, WordPress-style marketing layout. This container is divided into a two-column grid: on the left, taking about two-thirds of the width, a compact horizontal strip at the top combines a result summary on the left and a sort control on the right, both sitting flush with the top of the content area. Beneath this strip, a vertically flowing list of solar package cards fills the column, each card wide and image-forward to echo the strong visual anchoring of the original project photo. On the right, a single tall card spans the top of the sidebar, labeled as filtering for solar packages. Inside this sidebar card, the filter fields are stacked vertically with consistent spacing, resembling the structured ‘Categories’ card from the mockup. If no packages match the filters, a centered empty state message occupies the left column where the list would normally appear, preserving the overall layout. Invisible controller components manage URL state and navigation transitions but do not disturb the clean, two-column visual structure. The overall impression is a professional, marketing-driven search and browse experience that clearly mirrors the mockup’s hero + two-column template.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller anchors the Solar Packages content area directly beneath the hero section. It introduces a centered main container with a max-width of roughly 1140–1200px, leaving comfortable side margins on larger screens. There is a vertical gap of about 56px (between 48–64px) from the bottom of the hero to the top of this container. Inside, it establishes a two-column grid: the left primary column spans approximately 70% of the container width, while the right sidebar column spans about 30%. Both columns start at the same vertical baseline, so the top of the result summary/sort strip on the left aligns with the top edge of the filter card on the right. Vertical spacing between sections within this controller (e.g., between the summary/sort row and the result list, or between stacked elements in the sidebar) follows the 16–24px scale, while padding inside any cards follows the 24–32px pattern. On narrower viewports (e.g., mobile), the page controller stacks the columns vertically, placing the primary results column first and the filter sidebar card below it, while maintaining consistent 24px vertical spacing between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container in the normal document flow that defines the two-column grid; it is not sticky or fixed, but it controls responsive behavior (column widths on desktop, stacking on mobile).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter form sits entirely within the right-hand sidebar column, visually implemented as a single, tall card that echoes the ‘Categories’ card from the mockup. The card starts at the very top of the sidebar column, aligned horizontally with the top edge of the result summary/sort strip in the left column. It occupies nearly the full width of the sidebar column, leaving a small 16–24px outer margin from the container edges. Inside the card, there is a uniform internal padding of 24–32px on all sides. A clear sidebar title (e.g., 'Filter Packages') appears at the top of the card, followed by filter fields (system type, size, price, warranty, etc.) stacked vertically with 16px vertical spacing between labels and inputs. Grouped filters (such as price range sliders or checkbox groups) are separated by slightly larger 24px gaps to create distinct sub-sections. Overall, the card extends downward as needed, maintaining clear separation from the page background and visually reading as the primary interactive sidebar element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, scrolling naturally with the page. On smaller screens, this card moves below the results list as a full-width stacked card, maintaining the same internal padding and vertical stacking of fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is placed at the top of the left column, forming the right half of a horizontal control bar that sits just below the top edge of the main content area. This bar spans the full width of the left column and has a height of about 48–64px, visually lighter than a full card but with enough vertical padding (8–12px) to feel clickable and clear. Within this bar, the sort control is right-aligned, occupying roughly the right 30–40% of the left column’s width. It appears as a labeled dropdown or segmented control, with its baseline aligned to the result summary text on the left. A 16–24px gap separates this bar from the results list below. The sort control’s visual hierarchy is subtle yet clear: it uses standard font sizes and consistent blue accents for interactive elements, so it is easy to find without overshadowing the result list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the left column’s top control row. It scrolls away with the content as the user moves down the page; no sticky behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The result summary sits in the same horizontal row as the sort control, occupying the left side of the top control bar in the left column. It is left-aligned and typically spans about 60–70% of that row, providing enough space for a brief sentence like 'Showing 12 solar packages' or '24 results matched your filters.' The summary text uses a slightly bolder weight than body copy but is smaller than section headings, matching the mockup’s approach to information-dense top bars. The baseline of the summary text aligns with that of the sort control on the right, so the bar feels cohesive. There is about 16–24px of vertical spacing between this control bar and the results list below, ensuring the summary is visually associated with the content it describes without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline in the top control bar of the left column, scrolling naturally as part of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the main bulk of the left column, beginning directly beneath the summary/sort control bar, separated by a 16–24px gap. It spans nearly the full width of the left column, with a small 16–24px inner gutter from the column edges. Each solar package appears as a card with a moderate visual emphasis on imagery (e.g., a photo or icon of a solar installation) to mirror the large project photo in the mockup. Cards use 24–32px internal padding and are spaced vertically by 24px. On wider screens, the list may use either a single column of wide cards (mirroring a large central image) or a two-column grid within the left column; in either case, the cards collectively read as the dominant content block, drawing the eye down the page. The list area grows vertically as more packages are shown, pushing the sidebar filter card alongside it; the bottom edges of list and sidebar may not align, but the top alignment and consistent horizontal gap preserve the two-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the primary scrolling content in the left column. On smaller screens, it becomes full-width and appears above the filter form when stacked.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state is displayed in the exact footprint where the results list would normally appear, within the left column. When active (no matching packages), it replaces the list content but retains the same top alignment with the summary/sort control bar. The empty state content is centered within this area both horizontally and slightly more toward the vertical center of the visible portion of the column, using generous top and bottom padding (approximately 48–64px within the left column width) to create an open, reassuring message. A simple icon or illustration and a short, clear message are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing, and an optional 'Clear filters' or 'View all packages' button may sit beneath with standard button padding (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). This maintains the mockup’s clean, marketing feel even when no data is present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in place of the results list; it occupies the same spatial region in the left column and scrolls normally. It is not visible when the results list has items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component is non-visual and conceptually sits behind the main content area, associated with the Solar Packages page container. It aligns logically with the top of the main content area—where filters, sorting, and results first appear—but does not occupy any visible space or alter the layout. Its role is to keep filter selections, sort order, and possibly pagination reflected in the URL, ensuring the layout remains stable while the underlying data state changes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-spatial; it does not influence positioning or dimensions of any visible components. It hooks into the page lifecycle and routing behind the scenes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation handler is also non-visual and is conceptually associated with the entirety of the Solar Packages content region, from the top control bar to the bottom of the list. It facilitates transitions to individual package detail pages when a package card in the results list is clicked. This component does not render UI of its own and therefore does not alter spacing, but it ensures that interactive elements in the list feel seamless within the established two-column marketing layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-spatial; it does not occupy layout space. It attaches behaviors to interactive elements in the result list and operates within the normal navigation flow.

name: Package Detail
title: Solar Package Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected solar package and lets the user add this package to their quote list.

core_features: Read the packageId from the URL and load complete package information using getSolarPackageDetail.
Display full package specifications including system size, total price, panel model details, inverter type, and battery information when available.
Show warranty and common-criteria indicators using the meetsCommonCriteria object from getSolarPackageDetail.
Provide an input to optionally set a nickname for the package before adding it to the user’s quote list.
Handle the Add to quote action by calling addPackageToQuoteList with packageId and nickname, then optionally navigate to quote_detail.html with the generated quotePlanId.
Handle loading, success, and error states for both data retrieval and quote creation without using static or hardcoded data.
user_workflows: View package details: 1) User navigates to package_detail.html with a packageId URL parameter. 2) On initial load, the page reads packageId from the URL. 3) The page calls getSolarPackageDetail with packageId. 4) While waiting, a loading state is shown. 5) On success, the page displays the solar package data, related panel model and battery option (if present), and the criteria indicators. 6) If the interface call fails or packageId is missing/invalid, an error message is shown instead of the detail sections.

Evaluate if the package fits typical criteria: 1) After getSolarPackageDetail returns, the page reads meetsCommonCriteria from the response. 2) Each property (isSixToEightKw, isUnder15000, hasTwentyYearWarranty) is mapped to a visible status indicator. 3) The user can quickly see which criteria this package meets without any manual calculation.

Add package to quote list: 1) On the package detail page, the user locates the quote action panel. 2) The user optionally enters a nickname in the provided input field. 3) The user clicks the Add to quote button. 4) The page validates that packageId is available from the URL and that no add operation is currently in progress. 5) The page calls addPackageToQuoteList with packageId and the nickname (or an empty value if none was entered). 6) While waiting, the button shows a loading state and is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions. 7) On success, the page shows a confirmation message using the message field and updates local page state with generatedPlan and savedQuote. 8) Depending on configuration or user choice (e.g., following a View quote detail action), the page navigates to quote_detail.html with quotePlanId set to generatedPlan.id.

Navigate to generated quote detail: 1) After a successful addPackageToQuoteList call, the page displays a control to view the generated quote. 2) If the user clicks this control, the page constructs a URL for quote_detail.html including the quotePlanId parameter from generatedPlan.id. 3) The browser navigates to quote_detail.html?quotePlanId=[generatedPlan.id].

Change the displayed package via URL: 1) The user (or another page) modifies the browser URL to a different packageId (for example by clicking a link from the package listing). 2) When package_detail.html loads with the new packageId, the page repeats the loading flow for getSolarPackageDetail. 3) All displayed data, criteria indicators, and quote actions now refer to the newly selected package.
interactions: On page load: Read packageId from the URL query string. If present, trigger getSolarPackageDetail. If absent, show an error state indicating that no package is selected.
On getSolarPackageDetail success: Update bound components with package, panelModel, batteryOption, and meetsCommonCriteria. Hide the loading indicator and any previous error for the detail section.
On getSolarPackageDetail failure: Hide the loading indicator, show an error banner, and disable the Add to quote button because required package data is not available.
On nickname input change: Update an in-memory nickname value in the quote action panel state. This value is used when calling addPackageToQuoteList.

On Add to quote button click: Validate that packageId exists and that package data has loaded successfully. If valid, call addPackageToQuoteList with the current packageId and nickname, set an in-progress state, and disable repeated clicks.

On addPackageToQuoteList success: Clear any previous error related to quote creation, store generatedPlan and savedQuote in page state, show a success confirmation area using the returned message, and enable a control to navigate to quote_detail.html using generatedPlan.id.
On addPackageToQuoteList failure: Show an error message specific to the quote creation attempt, reset the in-progress state, and re-enable the Add to quote button so the user can retry.

On View quote detail control click: Construct a URL for quote_detail.html including a quotePlanId query parameter set to the generatedPlan.id from the most recent successful addPackageToQuoteList response, then navigate to that URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is determined by the packageId query parameter in the URL (e.g., package_detail.html?packageId=[id]). On initial load, the page reads packageId; if the parameter is missing, the page enters a 'no package selected' error state and does not call getSolarPackageDetail or enable Add to quote. When a valid packageId is present, getSolarPackageDetail is called and the page shows a loading state until it resolves. The results of getSolarPackageDetail populate the package detail components and the criteria indicators. A separate part of the state tracks the quote creation process: before the user clicks Add to quote, there is no generatedPlan or savedQuote in state. When addPackageToQuoteList is called, an in-progress flag is set and the Add to quote button is disabled. On success, the returned generatedPlan and savedQuote objects are stored in page state and used to enable navigation. Navigation to quote_detail.html uses only URL parameters: if the user chooses to view the generated quote, the page navigates to quote_detail.html?quotePlanId=[generatedPlan.id]. No data is persisted to localStorage; all persistence is handled through the assigned interfaces and downstream pages using URL parameters for identification.

components.functionality: Reads the packageId from the URL, invokes getSolarPackageDetail, and manages loading and error states for the rest of the page.
Shows high-level package information, such as package name, system type, system size, total price, and key panel model attributes from the loaded data.
Displays whether the current package meets common selection criteria using the meetsCommonCriteria object returned by getSolarPackageDetail.
Shows detailed technical information about the package components, including panel model details, inverter type, panel count, and battery specifications if a batteryOption is present.

Displays warranty-related information from the package data and highlights how warranty duration relates to the meetsCommonCriteria flags, along with the total system price and any related notes from the package.

Allows the user to optionally enter a nickname and then add the current solar package to their quote list by calling addPackageToQuoteList. Manages submission, loading, success, and error states for quote creation.

After a successful addPackageToQuoteList call, shows confirmation using the returned message and summarizes key information from generatedPlan and savedQuote, such as the plan name, system size, and total price.
Allows the user to navigate to the generated quote detail page after a successful addPackageToQuoteList call by constructing a URL to quote_detail.html with quotePlanId set to generatedPlan.id.
Shows or hides a loading state for the page while getSolarPackageDetail or addPackageToQuoteList calls are in progress.
Displays error messages when packageId is missing from the URL or when getSolarPackageDetail or addPackageToQuoteList return an error.
Provides navigation back to the previous listing or referring page (such as solar_packages.html or index.html) using browser history or known referrer information, without altering any stored data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s structure: a full-width hero-like package summary at the top, then a main content band using a two-column layout. In the hero area, the package title and key criteria are centered over a darkened background (image or tinted block). Below this, the main content splits into a dominant left column for detailed specs and warranty/pricing, and a narrower right column for the quote-action panel and its related status states. Utility elements (back navigation, error banner) sit just below the hero but above the two-column content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s structure: a full-width hero-like package summary at the top, then a main content band using a two-column layout. In the hero area, the package title and key criteria are centered over a darkened background (image or tinted block). Below this, the main content splits into a dominant left column for detailed specs and warranty/pricing, and a narrower right column for the quote-action panel and its related status states. Utility elements (back navigation, error banner) sit just below the hero but above the two-column content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 40–45% of the initial viewport is dedicated to the hero-style package summary. Directly below, the main content band uses about 65–70% width for the left column and 30–35% for the right sidebar on desktop. Cards and panels (specs, warranty, quote panel) use 24–32px internal padding. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero to main content, between cards) are around 48–64px, while spacing between stacked internal elements is around 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero is visually open: large typography, generous vertical spacing, and minimal text. The main two-column area is moderately dense—detailed text and tabular information in the left column balanced by a single, clearly defined quote-action card in the right column.
