Whitespace around and inside cards keeps the layout from feeling crowded, particularly around calls-to-action and status messages.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The flow mirrors the original Z-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left back navigation and package identification, sweeps right across the hero heading and criteria tags, then diagonally down to the left column’s detailed specs, and finally to the right sidebar quote-action panel. Within the hero, contrast draws attention immediately to the central package title. In the main content, the left column content anchors understanding, while the elevated right sidebar card and its primary CTA guide the user’s action. Error and success states are placed where they are quickly noticed but do not disrupt this primary flow.

layout.overall_description: The Package Detail page presents as a professional, corporate solar service page with a strong hero and a structured two-column body. At the top, a full-width hero section with a subtle image or gradient backdrop and dark overlay centers the package name in large, bold type, flanked by concise criteria indicators just below. Immediately beneath the hero, a slim back-navigation link and any error banner appear within the main content container, stretching horizontally but staying visually light. The content then breaks into a clear two-column layout: on the left, wide cards stack vertically to present component specs followed by warranty and pricing details, each floating on a white background with consistent padding and spacing. On the right, a narrower, elevated quote-action card aligns with the top of the left column, acting as the main sidebar. This card contains the add-to-quote form, transient loading feedback, success confirmation, and a link to view quote details, all tightly grouped and visually prominent. As the user scrolls, the left column provides depth and detail, while the right column keeps the conversion action persistently visible, echoing the marketing-driven, WordPress-style template feel of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-detail-loader appears as a full-page overlay that sits above the hero and the main two-column content area whenever the package data is being fetched. It covers the content from just below the site’s global navigation down to the bottom of the viewport, with a semi-transparent dark layer that dims the underlying hero and cards. At the vertical and horizontal center of this overlay, a spinner or loading graphic is displayed, approximately 40–60px in size, with a short status message directly beneath it. All other page elements remain in their layout positions underneath but are visually and interactively blocked by this overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay relative to the viewport; it stays centered and covers the visible content area while loading is in progress and disappears entirely once loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-summary-header occupies the visual hero area near the top of the page, directly below the global navigation/header bars. It spans the full page width, with content constrained to the central content width (e.g., 70–80% of the viewport, aligned center). Vertically, this hero takes roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height. The package title is centered horizontally, set in large, bold white or very dark text against a darker image or gradient overlay, with ample top and bottom padding (roughly 64px above and below the title within the hero). Any brief package subtitle or capacity summary sits directly below the main title, centered and separated by about 16–24px. The overall effect is a strong, high-contrast focal point that dominates the upper portion of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow; it scrolls away with the rest of the content. On smaller screens, the hero height compresses slightly, but the title remains centered with slightly reduced padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The criteria-indicator-list is positioned within the hero, immediately below the package-summary-header’s main text block. It appears as a horizontal row of pill-shaped or small rectangular badges (e.g., ‘5 kW System’, ‘On-grid’, ‘Residential’, ‘Includes Battery’) centered under the title. Each badge has 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding and small, high-contrast text. The entire group spans at most 60–70% of the content width, leaving breathing room to the left and right. Badges are spaced 8–12px apart horizontally, and the group is separated from the main title by about 16–24px of vertical space. On narrower viewports, the badges wrap into two or more lines while remaining center-aligned, preserving a compact, ribbon-like cluster under the headline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling with the hero content; it reflows responsively, wrapping to additional lines on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The components-specs-section is the first major card in the left column of the main content area, directly below the hero and any intervening back link or error banner. The main content container starts roughly 48–64px beneath the hero, creating a clear break. Within this container, the left column occupies about 65–70% of the available width. The components-specs-section card aligns flush with the left column’s edge and spans its full width. It appears as a white, lightly elevated card with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. A section title (e.g., ‘System Components & Specs’) is placed at the card’s top-left, with a 16–24px gap before the detailed content (bullet lists, spec grid, or tables). Vertical spacing between internal rows of specs follows the 8–16px rhythm, while the bottom of the card has at least 24px padding before the card’s bottom edge. Below this card, there is a 24–32px vertical gap before the next card in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and block-level within the left column; on smaller screens, it expands to 100% width and becomes the first main card stacked above the warranty-and-pricing-section and the quote-action-panel (if the mobile design prioritizes detail first).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The warranty-and-pricing-section sits directly below the components-specs-section within the same left column, aligned to the same left and right column boundaries. It is also presented as a white card with subtle shadow or border and 24–32px padding on all sides, visually matching the specs card. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the components-specs-section and the top of this card, following the consistent stacking rhythm. The top of the card starts with a clear heading (e.g., ‘Warranty & Pricing’) in bold text, followed by pricing details, warranty durations, and any financing information arranged in 1–3 logical blocks or rows. Internal elements are separated by 16–24px vertical spacing. The card’s width matches the left column (about 65–70% of the content area), and its height is determined by the amount of warranty and pricing information, maintaining sufficient whitespace around text blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the left column; on small screens it becomes a full-width card stacked directly below the components-specs-section, with consistent margins to the viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-action-panel is the primary card in the right sidebar column, aligned to the top edge of the components-specs-section in the left column. The right column occupies about 30–35% of the content width and is positioned flush to the right inside the main content container. The quote-action-panel spans the full width of this right column and appears as a slightly more visually prominent card than the left column cards: a white or very light surface with subtle shadow, 24–32px internal padding, and possibly a stronger accent line or header bar in the brand’s blue. At the top of the card, a concise heading (e.g., ‘Add this Package to Your Quote’) is aligned left. Below the heading, the form fields (system size options, extras, quantity, etc.) are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing. The primary CTA button (‘Add to Quote’) is placed near the bottom of the panel, spanning most of the card width (e.g., 80–100%) and given enough vertical padding (10–12px) to feel substantial. There is 16–24px padding below the button to accommodate success or error messaging without crowding. The card maintains a 24–32px margin from the left column content to its left and a similar margin from the right edge of the page container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a right sidebar card. On desktop, it may become sticky relative to the viewport top once the user scrolls past the hero (e.g., sticking 24–32px below the global nav) so that the CTA remains visible while the left column scrolls. On tablets and mobile, the card loses stickiness and stacks either above or below the left-column cards (typically above for conversion emphasis) at full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-success-summary appears inside the same visual area as the quote-action-panel, occupying the top portion of that card when the user has successfully added the package to their quote list. It presents as a highlighted success strip or sub-card within the panel: a full-width band across the card, with a soft green or brand-accent tinted background, rounded corners mirroring the card’s radius, and 16–24px internal padding. It sits directly above the main form fields and CTA or temporarily replaces them, depending on the state logic. The success icon and message are aligned left within the card, with a short, single or double-line confirmation and a small spacing of 8–12px above any follow-up action like the view-quote-detail-link. Vertical spacing separates this success block from the card edges and other form content so it feels clearly distinct but integrated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the quote-action-panel, it appears and disappears without altering the panel’s position. When visible, it pushes the form and CTA downward or replaces them, but the outer card dimensions adjust naturally within the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-quote-detail-link is positioned inside the quote-action-panel, closely associated with the quote-success-summary. When a package is successfully added, this link appears directly beneath the success message, aligned to the left edge of the card’s content. It’s displayed as a small textual link or a subtle tertiary button, occupying no more than 40–50% of the card’s width and separated from the success message by about 8–12px. There is an additional 16–24px padding between this link and the next element below (if any) or the bottom padding of the card. On states where the quote-success-summary is not visible, the link can appear beneath the primary CTA button as a low-emphasis navigation option, maintaining a similar left alignment and spacing pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the quote-action-panel, it scrolls with the panel content and follows its responsive stacking behavior; it becomes a simple line of text link at full width on mobile.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator is a localized status element within the quote-action-panel, shown when the user submits the form or when an inline operation is in progress. It appears either embedded within the primary CTA button (replacing the button label with a spinner and ‘Adding…’ text) or as a small spinner and message immediately below the button. In the latter case, it is left-aligned under the button with about 8–12px vertical spacing, occupying only as much horizontal space as needed (typically less than 30% of the card width). The rest of the card remains visible but subtly de-emphasized (e.g., button disabled) while this indicator is shown.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the quote-action-panel; it does not affect overall page layout and only slightly alters the height of the quote-action-panel card when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-state-banner sits at the top of the main content container, directly below the hero section and above the two-column layout. It stretches the full width of the content area (matching the left and right columns combined) but remains constrained within the central page container rather than edge-to-edge with the viewport. The banner is relatively shallow—roughly 48–64px tall for a single-line message—with a red or warm-tinted background and white or dark text, and 16–24px internal vertical padding to give the message breathing room. It aligns left with an error icon followed by the text, and there is about 16–24px of vertical spacing below the banner before the left and right columns begin. If a back-navigation-link is present, the back link appears above and to the left of the banner, keeping the banner immediately above the core content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the content container; it pushes the main two-column content downward when present and disappears entirely when there’s no error.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-navigation-link is a small, subtle navigational element positioned at the top-left of the main content container, just below the hero and aligned with the left edge of the left column. It appears as a text link with a left arrow icon (e.g., ‘← Back to Packages’), occupying only a narrow horizontal slice at the top. There is approximately 24–32px vertical space between the bottom of the hero and this link. If an error-state-banner is present, the link sits 8–16px above the banner; if not, it sits 24–32px above the start of the two-column area. The link does not disrupt the columns; it feels like a light breadcrumb-style control, visually lighter than headings but clearly visible when the user first scrolls from the hero into the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element; it scrolls with the page content and remains anchored to the left margin of the content container on all screen sizes (on mobile it appears as a simple line of text at the top of the stacked content).

name: Consultation Booking
title: Consultation Booking
description: Page for scheduling a solar consultation by selecting consultation type, location, an available date and time slot, and submitting contact details to create a consultation appointment.

core_features: Load consultation type options, property type options, and time slot help text via getConsultationBookingOptions to dynamically configure the booking form.
Allow users to select consultation type and property type, and enter location details (including ZIP code) with optional prefill from URL parameters.
Display a calendar that queries available consultation slots via getConsultationAvailabilitySlots based on the chosen consultation type and a dynamic date range.

Provide a time-slot picker for a selected date that lists only available ConsultationAvailabilitySlot items and highlights the earliest available weekday 10:00 AM slot as a recommended choice when present.

Automatically determine and preselect a recommended slot (earliest available weekday at 10:00 AM) using the data returned from getConsultationAvailabilitySlots, while allowing users to override this choice.
Collect contact information (name, phone, email) and optional notes in a booking form.
Validate that consultation type, property type, ZIP code, a time slot, and required contact details are present before booking.
Submit the booking using bookConsultationAppointment with the selected slot, location, and contact details to create a ConsultationAppointment.
Display booking confirmation using the ConsultationAppointment returned from bookConsultationAppointment, with an option to navigate back to the home page.
Respect incoming URL parameters (zip, propertyType, consultationType, source) to preconfigure the page and influence initial selections without storing data in localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open the Consultation Booking page and load options
1. User navigates to consultation_booking.html, optionally with URL parameters (zip, propertyType, consultationType, source).
2. On page load, the page parses URL parameters into internal state.
3. The page calls getConsultationBookingOptions with no parameters and stores consultationTypeOptions, propertyTypeOptions, and timeSlotHelpText.
4. The page uses the URL parameters (if present) to preselect consultationType and propertyType from the returned options and prefill the ZIP input; if a URL parameter does not match any returned option, it is ignored.
5. Using the selected consultationType and an initial date range (e.g., from the current date forward over a configurable window), the page calls getConsultationAvailabilitySlots to load slots for the calendar and time-slot picker.
6. Once slots are loaded, the page scans them to identify the earliest available weekday slot whose start_datetime corresponds to 10:00 AM and marks it as the recommended slot.

Workflow 2: Adjust consultation details and view available slots
1. User can change the consultation type using a selector bound to consultationTypeOptions.
2. When the consultation type changes, the page updates its internal selectedConsultationType state, clears any selected slot, and calls getConsultationAvailabilitySlots again with the new consultationType and the current calendar date range.
3. User selects a property type using a selector bound to propertyTypeOptions; the choice is stored in page state.
4. User enters or edits the ZIP code; the value is stored in state and validated for non-empty and basic format rules.
5. The calendar component visualizes which days in the current range have is_available slots; user can navigate the calendar date range (e.g., to future weeks or months), and whenever the visible date range changes, the component triggers getConsultationAvailabilitySlots for that new range.
6. For the active date, the time-slot picker component lists all slots matching that date with is_available set to true; among them, if there is a weekday slot with a 10:00 AM start time, that slot is flagged as the recommended slot and can be selected in one action.

Workflow 3: Select a specific time slot (including earliest weekday 10:00 AM)
1. User either accepts the recommended earliest weekday 10:00 AM slot by clicking a dedicated control, or manually chooses any other available slot from the list for the selected date.
2. On selecting a slot, the page records selectedSlotId and selectedAppointmentDatetime in state, derived from the chosen ConsultationAvailabilitySlot.
3. If the user changes the date in the calendar, the time-slot list updates to show only the slots for that date; any previously selected slot not in the new date is cleared, and the component recomputes the recommended earliest weekday 10:00 AM slot for that date range if applicable.
4. The booking summary component updates to reflect the currently selected consultationType, propertyType, ZIP code, and selected slot details.

Workflow 4: Enter contact details and submit booking
1. User enters contact_name, contact_phone, contact_email, and optional notes in the contact form.
2. On submit, the booking form validates that a consultationType, propertyType, ZIP code, and a selectedSlotId are present and that required contact fields pass basic validation.
3. If validation passes, the form calls bookConsultationAppointment with: slotId (from the selected ConsultationAvailabilitySlot), appointmentDatetime (from the slot’s start_datetime), consultationType (current selection), propertyType (current selection), zipCode (from the ZIP input), contactName, contactPhone, contactEmail, and notes.
4. While the request is in progress, the form shows a loading state and disables repeated submission.
5. On success, the page receives a ConsultationAppointment object and stores it in state; the booking form transitions to a confirmation state.
6. On error, the page displays an error message and allows the user to retry submission without losing entered data.

Workflow 5: View confirmation and navigate away
1. After a successful booking, the confirmation component reads the ConsultationAppointment object from state and displays key details (such as appointment_datetime, consultation_type, property_type, zip_code, and contact_name) for reference.
2. The page may optionally mark the appointment status from the returned ConsultationAppointment for display only; it does not manually update status.
3. User can click a "Done" or "Back to Home" control that navigates to index.html according to the outgoing connection definition.
4. If the user uses browser navigation (back/forward), the page reinitializes from URL parameters and internal state without using localStorage; previously booked appointments are not re-submitted, and the confirmation component is only displayed if the in-memory ConsultationAppointment state is still present.
interactions: Selection of consultation type: The consultation-type selector is populated from consultationTypeOptions returned by getConsultationBookingOptions. When the user selects a type, the page updates selectedConsultationType state, clears any selectedSlotId, and triggers a refresh of availability data by calling getConsultationAvailabilitySlots with the new consultationType and the current calendar date range.

Selection of property type: The property-type selector is populated from propertyTypeOptions. The user’s selection updates selectedPropertyType state; it is required for booking but does not directly affect available slots.

ZIP code entry: The ZIP input is optionally prefilled from the zip URL parameter. When the user edits this field, the value is stored in zipCode state. Basic validation feedback is provided on blur or form submission; slots are not re-queried based on ZIP because the availability interface does not accept ZIP as a parameter.

Calendar navigation: The calendar component shows a current date range (e.g., a month or a configurable window). When the user navigates to previous/next ranges, it fires an onDateRangeChange event that triggers getConsultationAvailabilitySlots with the current consultationType and the new startDate and endDate. The returned slots are merged into or replace the current slot list for that date range.

Date selection: Clicking on a date in the calendar triggers onDateSelect. The page filters all loaded ConsultationAvailabilitySlot records to those whose start_datetime falls on the selected date and is_available is true, and passes these to the time-slot picker. If the selected date has no available slots, the time-slot picker shows no options and may display timeSlotHelpText to guide the user.

Time-slot selection and recommended slot: Within the time-slot picker, each listed slot corresponds to a ConsultationAvailabilitySlot. When the user clicks a slot, an onSlotSelect handler sets selectedSlotId and selectedAppointmentDatetime based on that slot. The component also identifies the earliest slot among is_available weekday slots with a 10:00 AM start time; this slot is marked as recommended (e.g., via a distinguishable state) and can be selected by clicking a dedicated control (onRecommendedSlotClick), which programmatically sets selectedSlotId to that slot’s id.

Contact form input: The contact form fields (name, phone, email, notes) update corresponding state properties on each onChange event. Basic validation (e.g., non-empty name and phone, structurally valid email) is triggered on blur and onSubmit. Validation errors are stored in state and displayed next to the fields.

Booking form submission: When the user submits the booking form, an onSubmit handler validates required inputs (consultationType, propertyType, zipCode, selectedSlotId, contactName, contactPhone, contactEmail). If validation fails, the handler prevents submission and updates validation error state. If validation passes, it calls bookConsultationAppointment with the selected slot and form data. While awaiting the response, submission controls are disabled and a loading state is shown.

Handling booking response: On a successful response from bookConsultationAppointment, an onBookSuccess handler stores the returned ConsultationAppointment in state and switches the view from the edit form to the confirmation panel. On failure, an onBookError handler records error details in state and shows them to the user, allowing another submission attempt.

Confirmation and navigation: In the confirmation state, clicking a "Back to Home" or equivalent control triggers an onClickDone event that navigates the browser to index.html, in line with the configured outgoing connection. No additional parameters are required for this navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page maintains its state entirely in memory and via URL parameters, without using localStorage. On initial load, the page reads the following URL parameters: zip, propertyType, consultationType, and source. The zip parameter initializes the ZIP input field if present. The propertyType parameter, if present and matching a value from propertyTypeOptions returned by getConsultationBookingOptions, determines the initial selectedPropertyType; otherwise the initial selection is left unset or derived from defaults defined by the options data (for example, by using the first available option). The consultationType parameter, if present and matching a value from consultationTypeOptions, sets the initial selectedConsultationType and drives the first call to getConsultationAvailabilitySlots; if it does not match any available option, the consultation type is chosen from the options data instead. The source parameter is stored in a separate tracking state and may be used to conditionally adjust inline contextual text or for analytics, but it is not passed to any of the assigned interfaces and is not persisted in any data model on this page. After parsing URL parameters, the page calls getConsultationBookingOptions. Once the options are loaded and initial selections resolved, the page computes an initial calendar date range (e.g., starting at the current date for a configurable number of days) and calls getConsultationAvailabilitySlots with consultationType (from state), startDate, and endDate. The response slots are stored in a collection keyed by date and/or date range. For each date, the page filters and identifies available slots (is_available true) and checks is_weekday to drive weekday/weekend behavior. To support the requirement of choosing the earliest available weekday appointment at 10:00 AM, a derived state value recommendedSlotId is maintained: when slots are loaded or when consultationType or the visible date range changes, the page computes recommendedSlotId by scanning all in-range slots to find the one with is_weekday true, is_available true, and a start_datetime corresponding to 10:00 AM, preferring the earliest such start_datetime. This recommendedSlotId is used by the time-slot picker for highlighting and quick selection; the user’s explicit selection is stored separately in selectedSlotId so that they can override the recommendation. Changing consultationType clears selectedSlotId and recommendedSlotId and triggers a fresh getConsultationAvailabilitySlots call. Changing the calendar’s visible date range merges or replaces the slot data for that range and recalculates recommendedSlotId if necessary. Form data (zipCode, selectedPropertyType, contactName, contactPhone, contactEmail, notes) is stored in state as the user types. Validation errors are stored alongside their respective fields. On a successful bookConsultationAppointment call, the returned ConsultationAppointment is stored in appointmentState and used to render the confirmation view; the existence of a non-null appointmentState controls whether the page is in editing or confirmation mode. If the user reloads the page or navigates away and back via a new request, appointmentState is lost (since it is not persisted), and the page reinitializes based solely on current URL parameters and fresh calls to getConsultationBookingOptions and getConsultationAvailabilitySlots.

components.functionality: On page load, parse URL parameters, initialize local state, fetch consultation booking options, and trigger initial loading of available slots for the selected consultation type and date range. Also computes the recommended earliest weekday 10:00 AM slot after slots are retrieved.

Collects core consultation details: consultation type, property type, and ZIP code. Uses options returned by getConsultationBookingOptions and URL parameters to set initial selections. Changing consultation type triggers reloading of availability slots.

Displays a selectable calendar view that indicates which days have available consultation slots. Handles navigation between date ranges and triggers getConsultationAvailabilitySlots when the visible date range changes. When a date is selected, it filters the loaded ConsultationAvailabilitySlot records to those on that date.

For the selected date, lists all available time slots based on ConsultationAvailabilitySlot data. Highlights and exposes a quick action for the recommended earliest weekday 10:00 AM slot when present. Allows the user to choose any available slot and stores the selectedSlotId and selectedAppointmentDatetime in state.

Shows a real-time summary of the current booking configuration, including selected consultationType, propertyType, ZIP code, and details of the currently selected ConsultationAvailabilitySlot (if any). This helps users verify their choices before filling contact details and submitting.

Collects contact_name, contact_phone, contact_email, and optional notes for the consultation. Performs field-level validation and stores errors in state. Provides its data to the booking submission component when the user submits the booking.

Coordinates validation of all booking inputs and triggers the bookConsultationAppointment interface. It aggregates state from the consultation-details-form, time-slot-picker, and booking-contact-form, validates required fields, and calls bookConsultationAppointment with slotId, appointmentDatetime, consultationType, propertyType, zipCode, contactName, contactPhone, contactEmail, and notes. Manages loading, success, and error states.
data_binding: DerivedState(selectedConsultationType, selectedPropertyType, zipCode, contactName, contactPhone, contactEmail, notes)

components.functionality: Displayed after a successful booking. Renders details from the ConsultationAppointment returned by bookConsultationAppointment, including appointment_datetime, consultation_type, property_type, zip_code, contact_name, and status for informational purposes. Provides a control to navigate back to the home page (index.html).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear structure: a full-width top info bar and navigation (global shell), then a full-width hero section introducing Solar Consultation Booking with a bold centered headline. Directly beneath the hero sits the primary functional area: a two-column layout where the left column (about 65–70% width) contains the booking flow (details form, calendar, time slots, contact form, submit), and the right column (30–35% width) contains a single, vertically stacked booking summary card. After submission, a confirmation panel appears below the two-column area as a full-width section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear structure: a full-width top info bar and navigation (global shell), then a full-width hero section introducing Solar Consultation Booking with a bold centered headline. Directly beneath the hero sits the primary functional area: a two-column layout where the left column (about 65–70% width) contains the booking flow (details form, calendar, time slots, contact form, submit), and the right column (30–35% width) contains a single, vertically stacked booking summary card. After submission, a confirmation panel appears below the two-column area as a full-width section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top info bar is shallow, the navigation slightly taller, and the hero consumes roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height with generous vertical padding and a strong headline. The main two-column booking section fills the remainder of the viewport and can extend downward as needed. The left booking column uses most of the horizontal space, with comfortable gaps (24–32px) between its stacked components. The right summary column is narrower and visually card-like, with 24–32px internal padding and clear separation from the background. The confirmation panel below spans the full content width with ample top margin (~48px) to feel like a distinct section.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero is spacious with large typography and minimal elements. The main booking area is moderately dense: multiple components are stacked in the left column, but each has clear separation using the 16–24px vertical rhythm and card padding. Fields and interactive elements use 8–12px internal padding. The summary card in the right column is compact yet readable, with distinct groups separated by 16–24px. The confirmation panel is visually open and celebratory, with slightly more whitespace and fewer elements to reduce cognitive load after completion.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern. The user starts at the top left (logo/address), moves right across contact info and the main CTA (e.g., 'Get a Free Quote'), then diagonally down to the centered hero headline and subtext. From there, the eye moves down and slightly left to the main booking column, first encountering the Consultation Details form at the top. The flow then continues downward through the calendar and time slot picker, then the contact form, and finally the submit controls. Parallel to this, the eye occasionally jumps right to the lighter, elevated Booking Summary panel which mirrors the role of the 'Categories' sidebar in the mockup. After submission, the flow continues straight down to the full-width confirmation panel as the final focal point.

layout.overall_description: The Consultation Booking page sits inside a corporate-style solar services shell: a slim dark info bar at the very top, a clean white navigation bar beneath it, and then a wide hero image of solar panels under a blue gradient overlay. Centered in the hero is a bold white headline like 'Book Your Solar Consultation' with a short supporting line and ample top and bottom padding, occupying roughly the upper half of the viewport. Below this hero, the content snaps into a two-column layout constrained inside a centered max-width container. The left column, about two-thirds of the width, forms a clear booking flow: at the top is the Consultation Details form, followed by the availability calendar, then the time slot picker, the contact details form, and finally the submit controls, all stacked with consistent vertical spacing and card-like sections that echo the theme’s marketing feel. On the right, a taller, narrower card holds the Booking Summary, visually floating on the white background with a subtle shadow and generous internal padding, updating dynamically as selections change. Once the user submits, a distinct full-width confirmation panel appears below the two-column block, framed with larger vertical margins and a confident heading, visually signaling completion while still harmonizing with the same grid, spacing, and color language as the rest of the site.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This initializer component is non-visual and effectively behaves as an invisible wrapper for the booking experience. It sits logically just below the hero section and above all functional booking components within the document hierarchy, initializing data for the left booking column and the right summary card. It does not consume visible space or alter the layout; instead, it ensures that when the two-column booking section comes into view, all other components (forms, calendar, summary, confirmation) can render with the correct state and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, structural; participates only in logical load order without affecting spatial layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation details form is the first visible component in the main content area below the hero, positioned at the top of the left column in the two-column layout. It spans roughly 65–70% of the total content width, aligned flush to the left, with the right edge ending at the gutter separating it from the summary sidebar. It appears as a card-like block with 24–32px internal padding on all sides and a subtle card elevation or border to distinguish it from the background. At the top of this form is a medium-weight section title (e.g., 'Consultation Details') with 16–24px space below, followed by stacked fields for consultation type, property type, and location. Each field row maintains an 8–12px vertical gap from the next. There is about 32px of vertical margin below this form before the next component (availability calendar) begins, maintaining the consistent section rhythm observed in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored to the top of the left main content column, participating in the standard scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability calendar sits directly beneath the consultation details form in the left column, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It occupies the same column width (about two-thirds of the overall content width) and aligns left with the form above it, creating a clean vertical edge. The calendar is presented within its own card container with similar 24–32px internal padding to the details form, so the calendar grid does not touch the card edges. A small section heading like 'Select a Date' is aligned to the left at the top of the card, with about 16px spacing above the calendar controls. The calendar grid itself fills most of the card’s width but maintains 16–24px side padding, giving the dates room to breathe and matching the moderately spacious density of the mockup. About 24px of vertical margin separates the bottom of the calendar card from the time-slot picker below, maintaining consistent stack spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the left column, expanding vertically as needed with the month grid while preserving top and bottom spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time slot picker is positioned immediately under the calendar in the left column, with a 24–32px vertical gap. It shares the same left alignment and width as the components above, continuing the strong vertical column. This picker appears as a low-to-medium height card with 24px internal padding; at the top is a left-aligned label such as 'Available Time Slots' with 16px spacing below. Available time slots are presented in one or more rows of pill-shaped buttons or list items, each with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, arranged to wrap within the card’s width. There is a comfortable 16–24px gap between rows to prevent crowding. The entire picker card leaves approximately 24–32px of margin below it before the booking contact form begins. The visual weight is balanced so it feels like a continuation of the booking flow, not an isolated widget.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that may grow vertically if there are many slots, but always contained within the left column and standard scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking summary panel occupies the right sidebar column of the main content area, beginning vertically approximately aligned with the top of the consultation details form in the left column. It takes about 30–35% of the total content width and is offset from the left column by a consistent gutter (around 32px) to reinforce the two-column structure. The panel is designed as a prominent card with a white or very light background, subtle shadow, and rounded corners in line with modern WordPress-like themes. Internally, it uses 24–32px padding on all sides. At the top sits a concise, bold title such as 'Your Consultation Summary' with 16–24px spacing below, followed by stacked summary sections (selected consultation type, location, chosen date, chosen time, estimated duration) separated by 12–16px. Each piece of information is aligned left within the panel, using a label–value pattern that remains easily scannable. The card extends downward roughly in parallel with the left column’s combined content, but it doesn’t need to match heights exactly; it simply grows as summary details require. On taller pages, the card scrolls with the content rather than staying fixed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar card within the right column; scrolls normally with the page and is not sticky by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking contact form follows the time slot picker in the left column, separated by another 24–32px vertical gap. It spans the same left column width (about two-thirds of the page content width) and aligns cleanly with the forms and calendar above. This form is contained in its own card with 24–32px internal padding, visually consistent with the other booking cards. A section heading such as 'Your Contact Details' appears at the top left of the card, with approximately 16–24px space beneath it. Below the heading, fields for name, email, phone number, and any optional notes are stacked vertically, with 12–16px of space between each row. The bottom of this contact form card has a slightly larger bottom padding (around 32px) to separate the last field from the edge, after which there is about 24px of external margin before the booking submit controller area. The overall density is slightly tighter than the hero but comfortable for reading and completing multiple fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the left column, contributing to the vertical flow of the booking process.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking submit controller is positioned at the bottom of the left column, directly beneath the contact form with around 24px of vertical spacing. It spans the same column width and aligns left with all previous components, but it reads as a slimmer bar-like section rather than a full card. It may either be a simple row within the contact form card’s lower region or a discrete, low-height card with 16–24px internal padding. Within this area, the primary 'Confirm Booking' button is aligned to the left, using generous padding (10–12px vertical, 24px horizontal) and a prominent brand color (strong blue) that echoes the main CTA from the top navigation and hero. To the right of the button (or just below it on smaller widths), there may be supporting text such as terms acknowledgment or a subtle link to edit selections, spaced with about 8–12px. There is 32–48px of bottom margin between this control section and the start of the confirmation panel below, creating a clear visual break once the booking is submitted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored at the end of the left column’s flow; in standard desktop layout it is not sticky, though it naturally stays near the fold as the main action area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking confirmation panel appears below the entire two-column content section, occupying the full content width within the central page container once a submission is successful. It is separated from the main booking area by a generous vertical gap of roughly 48–64px to emphasize a transition from process to completion. The panel is a wide, visually open card with 32px internal padding on all sides and a subtle shadow or border to distinguish it from the background. Centered near the top of the panel is a bold confirmation headline (e.g., 'Your Solar Consultation is Booked') with 16–24px space below for a short explanatory paragraph summarizing the appointment details. Key details (date, time, location, and next steps) may be presented in a simple two-column text grid or a short vertical list, centered or slightly left-aligned, with 12–16px spacing between items. At the bottom, there may be a secondary button (e.g., 'Add to Calendar') aligned centrally or to the left with 24px spacing from the last text. The panel’s overall spacing is slightly more generous than the booking forms, echoing the open, impactful feel of the hero and giving users a sense of completion and ease.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width section that appears after the main booking flow; it scrolls into view naturally and does not overlay other content.

name: Blog / Resources
title: Blog & Resources
description: Page for browsing, searching, filtering, and sorting solar energy articles, with navigation to individual article detail pages.

core_features: Initialize search, filter, and sort controls using dynamic options from getArticleSearchFilterOptions.
Parse incoming URL parameters (query, dateRange, category, sortBy) to initialize page state and pre-populate search/filter controls.
Search and filter Article records using searchArticles based on the current search query, date range, category, and sort options.
Display a list of articles (title, summary, published date, category/tags as available) returned by searchArticles.
Display total article count returned by searchArticles for the current search and filter criteria.
Allow users to update the search query, filters, and sorting, updating the URL parameters and reloading results via searchArticles.
Allow users to clear search and filters to return to a default state based on getArticleSearchFilterOptions.
Allow users to navigate to an article’s detail page (article_detail.html) by clicking on an article title or card.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial page load
1. User navigates to blog_list.html (optionally with URL parameters: query, dateRange, category, sortBy).
2. Page calls getArticleSearchFilterOptions with no parameters.
3. Page waits for getArticleSearchFilterOptions to resolve and stores the returned dateRangeOptions, categoryOptions, and sortOptions.
4. Page reads URL parameters and initializes internal state:
   - If query is present, set search query state to that value; otherwise use an empty string.
   - If dateRange is present and matches one of the dateRangeOptions.values, select that option; otherwise select a default option (e.g., the first in dateRangeOptions).
   - If category is present and matches one of the categoryOptions, select that option; otherwise select a default category (e.g., none selected).
   - If sortBy is present and matches one of sortOptions.values, select that option; otherwise select a default option (e.g., the first in sortOptions).
5. Page derives sortDirection from the selected sortBy value when possible (e.g., if the value includes a suffix indicating ascending or descending) or leaves it undefined if not determinable.
6. Page calls searchArticles with the current state values (query, derived dateRange, no custom startDate/endDate, category, sortBy, sortDirection).
7. When searchArticles returns, page updates article list, totalCount, and view state (list vs empty state).
8. User sees articles matching the initialized state and can scroll the list.

Workflow 2: Searching by keyword
1. User focuses the search input field and types one or more keywords (e.g., related to incentives or tax credits).
2. User submits the search via an explicit action (e.g., clicking a Search button or pressing Enter).
3. Search form component updates the internal query state to the current input value.
4. Page updates the URL, setting or updating the query parameter with the new value while preserving any existing dateRange, category, and sortBy parameters.
5. Page calls searchArticles using the updated query and existing filter/sort state.
6. When results return, article list and totalCount are updated.
7. If no articles are found, an empty state message is displayed instead of the list.

Workflow 3: Filtering by date range
1. User opens the date range selector (populated from getArticleSearchFilterOptions.dateRangeOptions).
2. User selects a new date range option.
3. Filter component updates the internal dateRange state to the selected option’s value and clears any custom date range values that might be in use (if applicable).
4. Page updates the URL dateRange parameter to the selected value, preserving query, category, and sortBy.
5. Page calls searchArticles with the new dateRange, current query, category, sortBy, and derived sortDirection.
6. When results return, article list and totalCount are refreshed.
7. The date range selector reflects the newly selected option as active.

Workflow 4: Filtering by category
1. User opens the category selector (populated from getArticleSearchFilterOptions.categoryOptions).
2. User chooses a category or clears the selection (depending on UI design, e.g., a 'All categories' option or empty selection).
3. Filter component updates internal category state.
4. Page updates the URL category parameter (or removes it if no category is selected), preserving query, dateRange, and sortBy.
5. Page calls searchArticles with current query, dateRange, updated category, sortBy, and sortDirection.
6. When results return, article list and totalCount update to reflect the chosen category filter.

Workflow 5: Sorting results
1. User opens the sort control (populated from getArticleSearchFilterOptions.sortOptions).
2. User selects a new sort option (e.g., sort by published date, newest or oldest depending on options returned by the interface).
3. Sort component updates internal sortBy state to the new option’s value.
4. Page derives sortDirection from the chosen sortBy value where applicable (for example, by checking if the value indicates ascending or descending order) or leaves it unset.
5. Page updates the URL sortBy parameter to the selected value, preserving query, dateRange, and category.
6. Page calls searchArticles with the updated sortBy (and sortDirection if available) and the current query, dateRange, and category.
7. When results return, article list is re-rendered in the new order.

Workflow 6: Clearing search and filters
1. User clicks a 'Clear filters' or similar control.
2. Page resets internal state to defaults determined from getArticleSearchFilterOptions (e.g., empty query, default date range option, default category selection, default sort option).
3. Page updates the URL to remove query, dateRange, category, and sortBy parameters or reset them to default values as appropriate.
4. Page calls searchArticles with the reset state.
5. When results return, article list and totalCount reflect the default view.

Workflow 7: Navigating to article detail
1. User clicks on an article item in the article list (e.g., the article title or a designated clickable area on the card).
2. Click handler reads the selected Article’s id.
3. Browser navigates to article_detail.html with the articleId URL parameter set to the selected article’s id.
4. The Blog / Resources list page does not need to maintain any additional state beyond what is already in the URL; when the user returns via browser back, the page can reinitialize using the URL parameters and re-call searchArticles as needed.
interactions: Typing text into the search input updates the local query input value; submitting the form triggers a call to searchArticles and URL parameter update.
Changing the date range selector updates the internal dateRange state, updates the dateRange URL parameter, and triggers searchArticles.
Changing the category selector updates the internal category state, updates the category URL parameter (or removes it), and triggers searchArticles.
Changing the sort selector updates the internal sortBy state (and derived sortDirection), updates the sortBy URL parameter, and triggers searchArticles.
Clicking the clear/reset control resets all search and filter states to defaults based on getArticleSearchFilterOptions, updates URL parameters accordingly, and triggers searchArticles.
Clicking an article item navigates to article_detail.html with articleId set based on the clicked article’s id.

Whenever a new searchArticles request is initiated, a loading state is activated; while loading, the article list can show a loading indicator instead of or on top of previous results; when resolved, loading state is cleared and results or empty state are shown.

If searchArticles returns zero articles, the article list component exposes an empty state interaction (e.g., message and possibly a button to clear filters), which when triggered resets filters as in the clear filters workflow.
page_functionality.state_logic:
Page state is fully represented and synchronized using URL parameters (no localStorage). On initial load and on every URL change (including browser back/forward), the page reads URL parameters and re-evaluates state:
- query: If present, used as the search query. If absent, the search query defaults to an empty string. When the user submits a new search, the query parameter is set/updated, and other parameters are preserved.
- dateRange: Used to select a specific date range filter. After getArticleSearchFilterOptions resolves, the component tries to match dateRange against the returned dateRangeOptions values. If a match is found, that option is selected; if not, a default option (such as the first in the list) is selected and the URL may be updated to reflect this default if necessary.
- category: After getArticleSearchFilterOptions resolves, the component attempts to match the category parameter’s value to an item in categoryOptions (string match). If matched, internal category state is set accordingly. If not matched or absent, default category state is applied (e.g., no category filter), and the URL category parameter may be removed or normalized.
- sortBy: After getArticleSearchFilterOptions resolves, the component attempts to match the sortBy URL parameter’s value to one of sortOptions.values. If matched, that sort option is selected. If not present or unmatched, a default sort option is applied (such as the first in sortOptions), and the URL may be updated to reflect the applied default.
- sortDirection: Not represented directly as a URL parameter by default. Instead, it is computed whenever sortBy changes (e.g., by inspecting the sortBy value if it encodes direction). The computed sortDirection is passed to searchArticles. If the backend requires an explicit sortDirection independent of sortBy, additional logic could map URL parameters to both, but this page assumes sortBy is authoritative and sortDirection is auxiliary.

Every time state changes via user interaction (search submit, filter change, sort change, clear filters), the page updates the browser URL parameters using navigation APIs (e.g., pushState/replaceState or a full navigation, depending on implementation). After updating the URL, the page calls searchArticles with the current state values derived from URL and internal state: query, dateRange, (optionally unset startDate and endDate), category, sortBy, and sortDirection. The totalCount returned from searchArticles is used purely for display and does not alter URL state.

Returning to the page (e.g., via browser back from article_detail.html) rehydrates the view from URL parameters using the same logic, ensuring the user sees the same search, filter, and sort configuration they had before navigating away.

components.functionality: Provides a text input for users to enter a keyword search for articles and submit it, updating the query state, URL parameter, and triggering a new search via searchArticles.
event_handlers: onInputChange: Updates internal search query input value without immediately calling searchArticles.

onSubmit: Prevents default form submission, updates the query URL parameter with the current input value (preserving dateRange, category, sortBy), derives sortDirection from current sortBy if needed, then calls searchArticles with current state.
onClearQuery: Clears the search input, removes the query parameter from the URL (or sets it to an empty value), and calls searchArticles with the cleared query and existing filters/sort.

components.functionality: Displays and manages filter controls for date range and category based on dynamically loaded options from getArticleSearchFilterOptions.
event_handlers: onInit: After page load, calls getArticleSearchFilterOptions, stores dateRangeOptions and categoryOptions, and initializes filter selections based on URL parameters (dateRange, category). Triggers an initial call to searchArticles once options and initial state are ready.

onDateRangeChange: When the user selects a new date range option, updates internal dateRange state, updates the dateRange URL parameter to the selected option value, clears any conflicting custom date state if present, and calls searchArticles with updated dateRange and existing query, category, and sort state.

onCategoryChange: When the user selects or clears a category, updates internal category state, sets or removes the category URL parameter accordingly, and calls searchArticles with updated category and existing query, dateRange, and sort state.

onResetFilters: Resets dateRange and category to defaults based on getArticleSearchFilterOptions (e.g., first options or neutral values), updates URL by removing or normalizing dateRange and category parameters, and calls searchArticles.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose how the article list is ordered using dynamic sort options from getArticleSearchFilterOptions.sortOptions.
event_handlers: onInit: After getArticleSearchFilterOptions resolves, initializes the currently selected sort option based on the sortBy URL parameter or defaults to a suitable option (e.g., the first sortOptions item).

onSortChange: When a new sort option is selected, updates internal sortBy state to the option’s value, optionally computes sortDirection if the value encodes direction, updates the sortBy URL parameter, and calls searchArticles with existing query, dateRange, category, and updated sortBy/sortDirection.
onURLSync: Listens for URL changes (e.g., via browser navigation) and updates the selected sort option if sortBy in the URL changes, then triggers searchArticles if needed to keep results in sync.

components.functionality: Displays the list of Article records returned from searchArticles, including title, summary, published date, and any available category/tags, and exposes click interactions to navigate to article detail pages.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When searchArticles returns, receives the articles array and totalCount, replaces the current list with the new results, and triggers rendering of each article item.
onArticleClick: When an article item is clicked, reads the selected Article’s id and triggers navigation to article_detail.html with articleId set to that id.

onEmptyStateCheck: After each results update, checks if the articles array is empty. If empty, hides the list view and emits a signal to show the empty-state component; otherwise, ensures the empty-state component is hidden.
onRetryFromError: If an error state is exposed (e.g., network error when calling searchArticles), allows the user to retry the last searchArticles call with the current state.

components.functionality: Shows a textual summary of the current result set using totalCount from searchArticles and current filters (e.g., indicating how many articles match the current search).
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When searchArticles returns, reads totalCount and the length of the articles array to display how many articles are currently shown out of the total available for the active filters.
onFilterOrSearchChange: When query, dateRange, category, or sortBy change, updates its displayed description to reflect the active filters and search terms.

components.functionality: Provides feedback when searchArticles returns no articles and optionally offers a shortcut to clear filters or adjust search criteria.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When searchArticles returns an empty articles array, displays an informative empty state message; hides itself when there are results.

onClearFiltersClick: When the user chooses to reset from empty state, triggers the same logic as filter and search reset: clears query, resets dateRange and category to defaults, resets sortBy to default, updates URL accordingly, and calls searchArticles.

components.functionality: Indicates when article data is being fetched from searchArticles or filter options from getArticleSearchFilterOptions.
event_handlers: onSearchStart: Activated whenever searchArticles is called, showing a loading indicator and optionally disabling interaction controls to avoid duplicate requests.
onSearchEnd: Deactivated when searchArticles resolves or fails, hiding the loading indicator.
onFilterOptionsLoadStart: Activated when getArticleSearchFilterOptions is called on initialization.
onFilterOptionsLoadEnd: Deactivated when getArticleSearchFilterOptions resolves or fails.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL parameters (query, dateRange, category, sortBy) and coordinates calls to searchArticles when URL-derived state changes.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Reads current URL parameters (query, dateRange, category, sortBy) and stores them as initial state. Waits for getArticleSearchFilterOptions to resolve, normalizes these values against available options, then triggers an initial searchArticles call.

onStateChangeFromUI: When the search form, filter bar, or sort control emit state changes, updates URL parameters accordingly using navigation APIs (without full page reload if possible) and triggers searchArticles with the new state.

onPopState: Listens to browser navigation events (e.g., back/forward). When detected, re-parses URL parameters, updates internal state and component props (search input, filters, sort), and re-calls searchArticles as needed to ensure the article list reflects the URL.

onDefaultStateEnforce: If URL parameters are missing or invalid with respect to options from getArticleSearchFilterOptions, applies default values and may update the URL to reflect these defaults before calling searchArticles.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: Keep the existing site frame from the mockup: a full-width top info bar, a full-width white navigation bar, and a full-width hero image with a centered 'Blog & Resources' headline. Directly beneath the hero, use a two-column content layout on a 12-column grid: main content on the left (≈8/12 columns) and a sidebar on the right (≈4/12 columns). All blog interaction components (search, sorting, results, list, loading, empty state) sit in the left main column, while the filter bar becomes the structured sidebar card on the right, echoing the 'Categories' card from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep the existing site frame from the mockup: a full-width top info bar, a full-width white navigation bar, and a full-width hero image with a centered 'Blog & Resources' headline. Directly beneath the hero, use a two-column content layout on a 12-column grid: main content on the left (≈8/12 columns) and a sidebar on the right (≈4/12 columns). All blog interaction components (search, sorting, results, list, loading, empty state) sit in the left main column, while the filter bar becomes the structured sidebar card on the right, echoing the 'Categories' card from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top ~60–65% of the initial viewport for the existing header + nav + hero stack (info bar ~8–10% height, nav ~10–12%, hero ~40–45%). The blog components start in the main content section immediately under the hero. In that section, allocate ≈65–70% of the horizontal width to the left main column and ≈30–35% to the right sidebar. Apply 48–64px vertical margin between hero and main content, 24–32px vertical spacing between stacked elements inside the main content, and 32px horizontal gutter between the two columns. Cards (like the filter sidebar) have 24–32px internal padding on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the hero area very open and spacious, then slightly increase density in the main content area. The search bar and toolbar row (summary + sort) are compact but not cramped, with clear separation between controls. The article list presents relatively dense information (multiple cards), balanced by generous vertical spacing (16–24px) between cards and sufficient padding inside each card. The sidebar filter card is visually lighter and more open, using padding and clear section headers to avoid a cluttered feel even if it contains several filters.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Maintain a Z-pattern flow: users start top-left at logo/address, move right to contact and CTA, then diagonally down to the centered hero headline. From the hero, the eye naturally drops to the top-left of the main content, landing on the search form as the first interactive blog element. From there, the gaze moves horizontally across the results summary to the sort control on the right of the main column, then diagonally down to scan through the vertical article list. It finally moves rightward to the elevated sidebar filter card. Loading and empty states occupy the same main left column area as the list so they fit into this Z-flow without disrupting the overall pattern.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing corporate header, white navigation bar, and bold hero image with a centered 'Blog & Resources' headline, the page transitions into a clean two-column content section on a white background. A generous 48–64px gap separates the hero from this section, reinforcing the hierarchy. The left main column spans about two-thirds of the width and opens with a full-width search bar aligned to the left, framed by comfortable whitespace. Directly below, a slim toolbar row stretches across the column, with a brief results summary on the left and a sort dropdown aligned to the right, creating a tidy horizontal band of controls. Beneath this, the article list flows downward as a series of evenly spaced cards, each surrounded by consistent padding and breathing room. On the right, the remaining third of the layout is occupied by a single, prominent filter card that mimics the 'Categories' sidebar from the mockup: a white card with subtle elevation and 24–32px padding, containing vertically stacked filter controls. As users scroll, their focus stays anchored on the main column’s content, while the sidebar filter panel remains visually distinct and easily reachable. Loading and empty states appear in the same spot as the article list, keeping the structure intact. The whole experience feels like a modern WordPress-style marketing blog section: corporate, structured, and easy to scan, with a strong left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article search form sits at the very top of the main content section, directly below the hero and aligned with the left main column. After a 48–64px vertical gap from the bottom edge of the hero, the search form begins flush with the left margin of the main column and extends almost its full width (≈95–100% of the 8/12 grid columns), leaving only the standard inner padding from the column edges. Visually, it reads as a single horizontal search bar with an input field and a search button, approximately 48px tall. There is 24–32px of clear vertical space between the search form and the toolbar row (results summary + sort control) below it. This positioning establishes the search form as the first interactive element in the content area and a natural continuation of the hero’s focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the page as part of the left main column; it does not stick. On narrower viewports, it continues to appear at the top of the content stack, spanning 100% of the available width and retaining its 16–24px vertical spacing above and below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article filter bar occupies the right sidebar column, taking on the visual role of the 'Categories' card from the mockup. It begins horizontally aligned with the top of the search form in the left column: the top edge of the filter card is level with the top edge of the search form, creating a clean horizontal baseline between columns. The filter bar is contained within a white card that spans nearly the full width of the sidebar (≈90–100% of the 4/12 grid columns), leaving a 16–24px gutter between the card and the page edge. Inside the card, there is 24–32px padding on all sides, and the filter controls (e.g., checkboxes, dropdowns, tag chips) are stacked vertically with 12–16px of spacing between them. The card height grows with content but maintains at least 32px of space beneath it before any subsequent sidebar content or the footer, preserving the airy feel of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a sidebar panel. On desktop and tablet widths, it can be configured as a sticky element that remains visible within the viewport while the user scrolls the article list in the left column, as long as there is enough vertical space (sticky within the bounds of the main content section). On mobile, it drops below the main content and becomes a full-width card that scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article sort control is part of a slim horizontal toolbar just below the search form within the left main column. This toolbar is positioned 24–32px beneath the search form and sits flush with the main column’s left and right internal edges. The toolbar is divided into two zones: the results summary on the left and the sort control on the right. The sort control itself (for example, a labeled dropdown like 'Sort by: Newest') is right-aligned, occupying roughly 20–25% of the toolbar width, with its right edge lining up with the main column’s right edge. The vertical height of this toolbar is modest, around 40–48px, keeping it visually compact but giving enough room for comfortable click/tap targets. This establishes a clear control band that users immediately see after searching.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the left main column, scrolling away with the content. On smaller screens, it stacks above or below the results summary (depending on implementation), but remains directly above the article list with 16–24px spacing separating it from the first article card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article list occupies the majority of the left main column, anchored directly beneath the toolbar row (results summary + sort control). It starts 24–32px below that toolbar and spans the full usable width of the main column (≈100% of its 8/12 grid width), with consistent left and right padding that matches the rest of the layout’s grid gutters. Each article appears as an individual card or row, with 24–32px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between cards. The cards line up with the column edges, forming a strong vertical alignment that contrasts with the more compact sidebar on the right. The list extends downward and becomes the primary content the user scrolls through, while the top of the list always starts high enough in the viewport to feel connected to the controls above. The right edge of the article list is separated from the sidebar by a fixed horizontal gutter of about 32px, ensuring no visual crowding between the two columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically as the main content stream. It expands to fill available vertical space with no fixed height, and on smaller screens it becomes a full-width list that sits directly below the search, summary, and sort controls, with the sidebar content stacking underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article results summary is placed within the same toolbar row as the sort control, in the left portion of the left main column just below the search form. It is left-aligned and starts at the same x-position as the search form input, occupying roughly 50–60% of the toolbar’s width. The text (e.g., 'Showing 12 of 85 articles') sits on a single line when possible, vertically centered within the 40–48px tall toolbar, with 16–24px of space above and below the text relative to the toolbar edges. This summary acts as a subtle but immediate feedback area, visually linked to both the search form above and the sort control to the right, forming a cohesive band of query-related information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the toolbar row and scrolls with the page. On narrow viewports, it may stack above the sort control within the same vertical block, maintaining 8–16px vertical spacing between the summary text and the sort control while staying directly above the article list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article empty state occupies the same footprint as the article list in the left main column. When there are no results, it appears directly below the toolbar row (results summary + sort control), starting at the same vertical position where the first article card would normally appear. The empty state content is centered horizontally within the main column; any illustration or icon appears at the center with a headline beneath it, followed by descriptive text and an optional CTA (like 'Clear filters' or 'View all articles'). There is a generous 32–48px vertical gap between the toolbar and the top of the empty state’s main content to create a distinct separation from the controls, and 24–32px spacing between the empty state’s internal elements. The layout does not push or alter the sidebar filter card, which remains visible to the right, encouraging users to adjust filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the article list region without changing the height or position of the search form, toolbar, or sidebar. It scrolls with the page and collapses back to the standard article list view (same position and width) when results are available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article loading indicator is visually anchored to the article list area in the left main column. When active, it appears centered over the list region, either as an inline loader before cards render or as a semi-transparent overlay. In an overlay configuration, a subtle dimmed layer covers the width and current height of the article list area (or the empty area where it will render), with a spinner or progress element centered horizontally and vertically within that region. The indicator respects the boundaries of the left main column, not spilling into the sidebar. There is no additional permanent spacing reserved for it; when inactive, the space is fully reclaimed by the article list or empty state content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or occupies the article list’s content area without shifting surrounding layout elements. It appears and disappears dynamically, but does not alter the position of the search form, toolbar, or sidebar. On mobile, it still centers within the full-width main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog URL state manager has no visible UI representation. It operates behind the scenes, keeping the current search term, selected filters, sort option, and pagination state synchronized with the URL. It does not occupy any space in the layout and does not introduce visual components on the page. Its impact is only observable through behaviors like updated query parameters and state restoration on page reload.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint and no spatial behavior in the rendered layout. It functions logically in the background, tying together the behaviors of the search form, filter bar, sort control, and article list without affecting their physical placement.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays the full content of a selected article, allows saving it to a reading list with tags and notes, and shows related articles for further navigation.

core_features: Load and display full article details (title, publication date, category, tags, content, and article URL if available) using getArticleDetail(articleId).
Allow users to save/bookmark the current article to their reading list with optional tags and notes using saveArticleToReadingList(articleId, tags, notes).
Display a list of related or recent articles in a sidebar or secondary section using getRelatedArticles(articleId).
Provide navigation back to the main articles list or filtered by category (blog_list.html?category=...) using the current article’s category.
Enable navigation to other articles by clicking items in the related articles list, updating the articleId URL parameter.
Provide basic sharing controls that use the article’s URL (from the Article model or the current page URL) so users can copy or share the link.
user_workflows:
View article from blog list or homepage:
1. User clicks an article link on blog_list.html or index.html that navigates to article_detail.html?articleId={id}.
2. On page load, the articleId URL parameter is read.
3. The page calls getArticleDetail(articleId) to fetch the article data.
4. Once the article is loaded, the main article content, metadata, and category/tags are displayed.
5. In parallel or after article load, the page calls getRelatedArticles(articleId) to populate the related articles section.
6. User reads the article, can click the back or category link to return to blog_list.html with an optional category filter.

Save article to reading list with tags and notes:
1. While viewing an article, the user opens or sees the reading list save form.
2. User optionally enters one or more tags (e.g., separated by commas) and/or a short note.
3. User clicks the save button.
4. The page calls saveArticleToReadingList(articleId, tagsArray, notesText).
5. On success, the page can show a confirmation state and may clear or preserve the form fields depending on the design; the SavedArticle response can be stored in component state for display.
6. On error, the page displays an error message and offers a retry action.

Browse and open related articles:
1. User scrolls to the related articles section.
2. Related articles are loaded via getRelatedArticles(articleId) and displayed as a list.
3. User clicks a related article item.
4. The browser navigates to article_detail.html?articleId={relatedArticleId}.
5. The page detects the new articleId in the URL, re-runs getArticleDetail and getRelatedArticles for the new articleId, and updates all components accordingly.

Return to all articles or filter by category:
1. Within the article header or navigation, user clicks a "Back to all articles" link.
2. The page navigates to blog_list.html without additional parameters, or with category if desired.
3. Alternatively, user clicks on the article’s category label.
