4. The page navigates to blog_list.html?category={currentArticle.category} using the category from the loaded Article.

Access article via shared or direct link:
1. User or another person opens article_detail.html with an articleId or slug-based parameter in the URL.
2. The page reads articleId from URL parameters.
3. The page loads article data with getArticleDetail(articleId) and related articles with getRelatedArticles(articleId).
4. All functionality (reading, saving, navigating) is available without needing to come from blog_list.html.
interactions: Initial data load on page open based on the articleId URL parameter; triggers getArticleDetail(articleId) and getRelatedArticles(articleId).
Retry loading article details if the initial getArticleDetail call fails (e.g., a retry button re-calls getArticleDetail with the same articleId).
Clicking the save button in the reading list form submits tags and notes via saveArticleToReadingList and updates the component state when the SavedArticle is returned.
Editing tags in the reading list form: user types or edits text, which is parsed into an array of tags upon form submission (e.g., split by separators).
Adding notes in the reading list form: user types a free-form note, stored in local component state and sent as the notes parameter on submission.
Clicking a related article title or item navigates to article_detail.html with the related article’s ID as articleId, causing a full re-load for that article.
Clicking the category label in the article details navigates to blog_list.html?category={article.category}, using the category value from the loaded Article.
Clicking a generic back link navigates to blog_list.html (optionally without parameters) to show all articles.

Using sharing controls (e.g., a copy-link button) retrieves the article URL from the Article model (article.url) if available, or falls back to the current window URL, and copies it to the clipboard or opens the user’s default share mechanism.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state is driven by the URL parameter articleId. On each load or navigation to article_detail.html, the page reads articleId from the query string. If articleId is present, getArticleDetail(articleId) is called to obtain the Article. After the Article is successfully loaded, getRelatedArticles(articleId) is called to obtain related Article objects. All child components that display the article content, metadata, or category bind to the Article returned by getArticleDetail. The related articles list binds to the array returned by getRelatedArticles. When a user clicks a related article, the browser navigates to article_detail.html?articleId={relatedId}, which changes the URL and triggers a full re-evaluation of articleId and fresh API calls; no localStorage is used to persist or infer state. The reading list save form maintains transient input state for tags and notes in component-level state (not URL or localStorage) and, upon submission, calls saveArticleToReadingList with the current articleId and the parsed inputs. The SavedArticle returned from saveArticleToReadingList is stored in memory for the current page view to drive confirmation messages or saved-state indicators. Navigation to blog_list.html optionally includes a category URL parameter taken from article.category to filter the list on that page, but article_detail.html itself does not depend on that parameter.

components.functionality: Reads articleId from the URL on page load or when the URL changes, calls getArticleDetail(articleId) to fetch the article, and then calls getRelatedArticles(articleId) to fetch related articles. Manages loading, success, and error states and exposes the loaded data and statuses to other components.
event_handlers: onLoad: Triggered when article_detail.html is first opened; reads articleId from URL and initiates getArticleDetail(articleId) and getRelatedArticles(articleId).
onUrlParamChange: Triggered when the articleId query parameter changes (e.g., navigating to a related article); re-executes getArticleDetail and getRelatedArticles using the new articleId.
onArticleLoadSuccess: Called when getArticleDetail returns successfully; stores the Article in page-level state so other components can render it.
onArticleLoadError: Called when getArticleDetail fails; sets an error state that can be displayed with a retry option.
onRelatedArticlesLoadSuccess: Called when getRelatedArticles returns successfully; stores the related Article array in page-level state.
onRetryLoad: Triggered by a user action (e.g., clicking a retry button) to re-call getArticleDetail(articleId) and getRelatedArticles(articleId) using the current URL parameter.

components.functionality: Displays the main article metadata from the loaded Article, such as title, publication date, category, and tags. Provides navigation links to go back to all articles or to filter by the article’s category.
event_handlers: onClickBackToArticles: Navigates to blog_list.html without additional parameters, returning the user to the general articles list.
onClickCategory: Uses article.category from the bound Article to navigate to blog_list.html?category={article.category}.
onRender: Renders updated metadata whenever the Article binding changes due to a new getArticleDetail response.

components.functionality: Renders the full body content of the article, including text and any embedded markup provided in Article.content. It should update whenever a different Article is loaded.
event_handlers: onArticleChange: Triggered when the Article from article-data-loader changes; updates the displayed content accordingly.

onContentLinkClick: Handles clicks on links inside the article body. If a link points to another article_detail.html with an articleId query parameter, the browser navigates there, which in turn triggers article-data-loader.

components.functionality: Provides inputs for users to save the current article to their reading list by entering optional tags and notes. On submission, calls saveArticleToReadingList with the current articleId, parsed tags, and notes, then reflects the SavedArticle response.
event_handlers: onTagsInputChange: Updates local form state when the user types or edits tags in the tags input field.
onNotesInputChange: Updates local form state when the user types or edits notes in the notes field.

onSubmitSaveArticle: Validates input (e.g., trims whitespace), converts the tags string into an array, then calls saveArticleToReadingList(articleId, tagsArray, notesText) using the currently loaded Article.id.

onSaveSuccess: Triggered when saveArticleToReadingList returns a SavedArticle; stores the SavedArticle in component state (e.g., to show a saved indicator or timestamp) and may display a success message.
onSaveError: Triggered if saveArticleToReadingList fails; sets an error message and allows the user to retry submission.
onArticleChange: Clears or adjusts the form state when the current Article changes (e.g., user navigates to a different article), ensuring the form reflects the new article.

components.functionality: Displays a list of related or recent articles based on data returned from getRelatedArticles(articleId). Each list item represents an Article and allows navigation to that article’s detail page.
event_handlers: onRelatedDataChange: Triggered when the related articles array from article-data-loader updates; re-renders the list.

onClickRelatedArticle: When the user clicks a related article item, navigates the browser to article_detail.html?articleId={relatedArticle.id}, which triggers article-data-loader to fetch the new article.
onClickRelatedArticleCategory: Optionally navigates to blog_list.html?category={relatedArticle.category} when a related article’s category label is clicked.

components.functionality: Provides controls to share or copy a link to the current article. Uses Article.url if available, otherwise uses the current page URL as the share link. Does not use additional APIs beyond the browser environment.
event_handlers: onShareLinkClick: Determines the share URL (Article.url if defined, otherwise the current page URL) and triggers the browser’s sharing behavior (e.g., opening a mailto link or native share dialog where available).
onCopyLinkClick: Copies the share URL to the clipboard and displays a transient confirmation state.
onArticleChange: Updates the share URL used for controls whenever the Article changes.

components.functionality: Shows loading indicators while getArticleDetail or getRelatedArticles are in progress and displays error messages with retry options if loading fails.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange: Updates the panel when article-data-loader or related articles loading states change (e.g., showing or hiding loading indicators).
onErrorStateChange: Displays error messages when article-data-loader reports an error for getArticleDetail or getRelatedArticles.
onClickRetry: Calls article-data-loader.onRetryLoad to re-attempt fetching the article and related articles using the current articleId from the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The article detail page follows the mockup’s structure: global top info bar and navigation (outside this spec), then a full-width hero image dedicated to the article header, followed by a main two-column content band where the article body occupies the left ~2/3 and a single, structured card-style sidebar (the reading list save form) occupies the right ~1/3. Below this two-column block, a full-width related articles section continues the scroll. The data loader and status/error panel sit near the top of the article area, controlling and annotating the whole article experience, while the share controls are visually tied to the article header/hero.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The article detail page follows the mockup’s structure: global top info bar and navigation (outside this spec), then a full-width hero image dedicated to the article header, followed by a main two-column content band where the article body occupies the left ~2/3 and a single, structured card-style sidebar (the reading list save form) occupies the right ~1/3. Below this two-column block, a full-width related articles section continues the scroll. The data loader and status/error panel sit near the top of the article area, controlling and annotating the whole article experience, while the share controls are visually tied to the article header/hero.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section consumes roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height, with the article title and metadata vertically centered over a darkened solar-themed image. Below the hero, the content is constrained to a centered max-width container. Inside that, the left main column takes approximately 65–70% of the width for the article body, and the right sidebar takes 30–35% for the reading list card. Vertical spacing between the hero and the main content band, and between the main content and the related articles section, is generous (about 48–64px). Card-like elements (sidebar form, related article cards) use 24–32px internal padding on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero and article header area are visually open with a large title, ample top/bottom padding, and minimal competing elements. The main content band is moderately dense: the article text and images are comfortably spaced with 16–24px between paragraphs and media, while the sidebar form is compact but not cramped thanks to card padding and clear grouping of fields. The related articles section is visually lighter, with distinct cards and consistent spacing to avoid clutter despite multiple items.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the observed Z-pattern. The eye is drawn from the upper-left logo/nav (global) to the right side of the hero where share controls echo a CTA position, then diagonally down to the centered article title. From there, the flow continues down into the main content: first to the large article body on the left, then across to the right to the prominent reading list save card. After finishing the article, the eye naturally moves down to the related articles section, which offers clear, card-like next steps. Status/error feedback appears just below the hero so it is noticed early without overpowering the title.

layout.overall_description: Below the global info bar and navigation, the page opens with a full-width hero image featuring a subtle dark overlay, showing a solar-panel-related photograph. Centered within this hero, and constrained to a comfortable max-width, the article title appears in large, bold white text, with metadata directly beneath. To the lower-right area of this same hero band, a compact share toolbar floats aligned with the right edge of the content, echoing the mockup’s right-side CTA position. Immediately below the hero, within a centered content container, a slim status/error panel appears when needed, spanning the full content width. Beneath that, the main article region is split into two columns: the left, wider column houses the full article body—text and images flowing top to bottom—while the right, narrower column holds a single card-like sidebar containing the reading list save form, visually elevated with a subtle shadow. Generous vertical spacing separates this main band from a full-width related articles section at the bottom, where a row of article cards invites the user to continue exploring. Throughout, spacing and alignment follow a familiar Bootstrap-style 12-column grid, producing a professional, corporate marketing feel that remains clean and easy to scan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is implemented as a full-width, semi-transparent overlay that sits on top of the hero and the main article content area, but beneath the global navigation. When active, it covers from the top edge of the hero image down to just below the bottom edge of the two-column article band (excluding the related articles section), dimming the article area while keeping the page frame visible. A spinner or loading indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically within this overlay. In its inactive state, the loader has no visible footprint and does not affect spacing; it only appears as an absolute/overlay layer anchored to the article container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute overlay anchored to the article content region: it stretches to match the width of the main content container and the height of the hero plus primary content band, covering interactions in that region while loading but not altering the underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header occupies the center of the hero section. The hero itself spans the full viewport width and roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height, with a solar-themed background image and a dark gradient overlay. Within this hero, a centered content column (aligned with the main page grid) holds the header elements. The article title is placed slightly above the vertical center of the hero, aligned center horizontally, and set in large, bold white type that stretches to about 60–70% of the content width for comfortable line lengths. Directly beneath the title, article metadata (publish date, author, reading time, tags) is arranged on one or two lines, also centered, with 8–16px vertical spacing from the title. The header block as a whole has generous top and bottom padding (approximately 48–64px) relative to the hero edges, ensuring ample breathing room around the text and preserving the strong, headline-focused hero aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal document flow within the hero section; it scales and reflows responsively but remains vertically centered within the hero’s height on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article content sits in the left main column of the two-column band immediately below the hero (and status panel, if present). The overall content area is centered on the page and constrained to a typical theme width; within this, the article body column occupies roughly 65–70% of the width and is aligned to the left. The top edge of the article body starts about 32–48px below the hero’s bottom edge (or below the status panel if it’s visible), maintaining a clear separation from the hero. The text is set in a comfortable line length, with paragraphs stacked vertically and separated by 16–24px. Inline images and media break out to span the full width of this left column, with 24px of vertical margin above and below each. Headings, lists, and blockquotes follow similar vertical spacing, ensuring a moderately dense but still breathable reading experience. The article content extends downward until it ends naturally, and the two-column layout continues alongside it until the content finishes, after which there is a 48–64px gap before the related articles section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal document flow as the dominant left column of a responsive two-column layout; on smaller screens, it stacks above the sidebar and expands to nearly full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading list save form is placed in the right-hand sidebar column of the main two-column band, aligned with the top of the article content. This sidebar column occupies approximately 30–35% of the main content container’s width. The form itself appears as a single, cohesive card with a white background, subtle drop shadow or border, and rounded corners, visually echoing the mockup’s categories card. It is inset from the column edges with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. A concise card title (e.g., “Save to Reading List”) sits at the top, with a 16–24px gap below before form fields begin. Input fields (such as tags, notes, and list selection) are stacked vertically with 12–16px between them, and the primary save button occupies the full card width or aligns to the right near the bottom, with 16–24px of padding below it to the card’s edge. The top of the card sits roughly 32–48px below the hero’s bottom edge, mirroring the vertical offset of the article body, and the bottom of the card ends well above the fold in most cases, maintaining comfortable whitespace between it and the related articles section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard sidebar card in the two-column layout. On smaller screens, it drops below the article content and becomes a full-width card with the same internal padding, separated from the article by about 32px of vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related articles list forms its own full-width section beneath the main two-column article band. It is aligned to the same centered content container as the article above. A section heading (e.g., “Related Articles”) is placed at the top left of this area, starting roughly 48–64px below the bottom of the article content/reading list band, providing a clear visual break. Below the heading, related articles are displayed as individual cards in a horizontal row on desktop—typically 3 cards across within the 12-column grid, each taking about 3–4 columns with consistent gaps of 24px horizontally and 24–32px vertically if wrapping occurs. Each card includes a thumbnail image at the top, a short title, and a brief excerpt, all with internal padding of 24px. The section extends only as far down as the related items require, with about 48px of padding at the bottom before the page footer. This section feels visually lighter than the main content, acting as a clear pathway to continue browsing without overwhelming the user.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow as a full-width section beneath the primary article band; on smaller screens, cards stack vertically with maintained padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article share controls are visually associated with the hero and header area. Within the hero’s centered content container, they are positioned towards the lower-right portion of the header block, aligning with the right edge of the article title’s text column. On desktop, the share toolbar appears as a compact horizontal row of icons (or buttons) sitting 16–24px below the metadata line and 16–24px above the bottom edge of the hero content area, creating a right-anchored band of controls that mirrors the mockup’s top-right CTA positioning. The toolbar itself occupies roughly 20–25% of the hero’s content width, leaving ample open space to the left so it does not compete with the headline. On scroll, the toolbar scrolls away along with the hero, but its initial placement ensures it’s seen early in the Z-pattern sweep from top-left nav to top-right actions to central header. On narrower screens, the share controls can move to a centered row directly below the metadata to preserve legibility.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits within the hero content’s normal flow, aligned to the right within the header block; it is not sticky and scrolls with the hero, but its right alignment and positioning near the bottom of the hero give it CTA-like emphasis.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status and error panel is a slim horizontal band placed immediately below the hero section and above the main two-column content band. It spans the full width of the centered content container, aligning with the article body and sidebar columns beneath. When visible, it appears as a colored strip (e.g., pale red for errors, yellow for warnings, green for success) with a subtle border radius and 16–24px of internal vertical padding, making it about 48–64px tall depending on content. An icon and short message are aligned left within the panel, with optional actions (e.g., retry) aligned right. There is a 16–24px gap between the bottom of this panel and the top of the article content/reading list columns, preserving the sense of separate functional layers. When there are no messages, the panel collapses entirely, and the two-column band shifts up to sit directly beneath the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies a fixed position in the layout just below the hero when active, pushing the two-column content down; when inactive, it is removed from the flow, creating no extra spacing. It does not float or stick; it scrolls with the rest of the page.

name: Savings Calculator
title: Savings Calculator
description: Interactive calculator where users enter their location, energy costs, and roof details to estimate solar energy production and annual savings, adjust system size, and save configurations as scenarios for later comparison or to request a quote.

core_features: Prefill calculator inputs from URL parameters (zip, monthlyBill, systemSizeKw) and load default options using getSavingsCalculatorDefaults.
Collect user inputs: ZIP code, average monthly electric bill, roof orientation, shading level, and system size (within the range returned by getSavingsCalculatorDefaults).
Trigger calculateSavingsEstimate to compute estimated annual savings, estimated system cost, annual production, and payback period based on current inputs.
Allow manual adjustment of system size using the range and step provided by getSavingsCalculatorDefaults.systemSizeRangeKw, with instant recalculation using calculateSavingsEstimate.

Provide an optional "adjust to target savings" interaction where users specify a desired annual savings amount and the system iteratively adjusts system size (within the allowed range) by the configured step, invoking calculateSavingsEstimate until the target is reached or the range limit is hit.
Enable saving the current configuration as a named SavingsScenario via saveSavingsScenario and display an updated list of saved scenarios from getSavedSavingsScenarios on the same page.
Allow users to reapply a saved SavingsScenario to the calculator (restore all corresponding inputs) and recalculate using calculateSavingsEstimate.

Offer navigation to get_quote.html with current calculator inputs (zip, averageBill, systemSizeKw) appended as URL parameters when a user clicks "Get a quote for this system" after generating an estimate.
Keep key calculator state (zip, monthlyBill, systemSizeKw, and optionally selected scenario) synchronized with URL parameters so the page can be reloaded or shared with the same configuration.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial load and prefilled inputs
1. User navigates to savings_calculator.html, optionally with URL parameters zip, monthlyBill, and systemSizeKw.
2. The page parses the URL parameters and sets initial calculator state for ZIP code, monthly bill, and system size, if present.
3. The page invokes getSavingsCalculatorDefaults to retrieve roofOrientationOptions, shadingLevelOptions, and systemSizeRangeKw (min, max, step) for the system size control.
4. The page validates the incoming systemSizeKw against systemSizeRangeKw; if it is outside the range or not provided, the component initializes system size based on the allowed range from getSavingsCalculatorDefaults.
5. The page invokes getSavedSavingsScenarios to populate the saved scenarios list component.
6. If URL parameters exist for the base inputs, the page may automatically invoke calculateSavingsEstimate once all required inputs are valid, populating the results panel without additional user action.

Workflow 2: Run a new savings estimate
1. User fills in or edits the ZIP code field.
2. User fills in or edits the average monthly electric bill field.
3. User selects roof orientation from the options provided by getSavingsCalculatorDefaults.roofOrientationOptions.
4. User selects shading level from getSavingsCalculatorDefaults.shadingLevelOptions.
5. User adjusts system size using the system size control, constrained by systemSizeRangeKw (min, max, step).
6. User clicks the primary "Calculate savings" action on the calculator form.
7. The page validates that all required inputs are present and within allowed ranges; if not, it shows inline validation messages and aborts the call.
8. On success, the page calls calculateSavingsEstimate with the current inputs.
9. When the interface returns the estimate, the page updates the results panel with estimatedAnnualSavings, estimatedSystemCost, estimatedAnnualProductionKwh, and paybackYears, and stores the response for later use.
10. The URL is updated to reflect the current zip, monthlyBill, and systemSizeKw so the state is shareable.

Workflow 3: Adjust system size toward a savings target
1. After viewing an initial estimate, the user enters a desired annual savings amount in a dedicated target input within the system size control area.
2. The user clicks an "Adjust system size" action linked to this target.
3. The page checks that a target value and current base inputs (ZIP code, monthly bill, roof orientation, and shading) are valid.
4. The component iteratively adjusts the systemSizeKw value within systemSizeRangeKw, using the step value returned by getSavingsCalculatorDefaults, and calls calculateSavingsEstimate after each adjustment.
5. After each response, the component compares estimatedAnnualSavings to the user-provided target.
6. If the target is met or exceeded while staying within systemSizeRangeKw, the component stops adjusting, leaves the system size at the last value, and shows the corresponding estimate in the results panel.
7. If the allowed range is exhausted without reaching the target, the component stops adjusting, keeps the nearest system size tested, and displays a non-blocking notice that the target was not fully reached within the available range.
8. During this process, the component keeps the visible system size and the URL systemSizeKw parameter aligned with the last used value.

Workflow 4: Save a scenario and compare later
1. After obtaining an estimate using calculateSavingsEstimate, the user enters a scenario name in the scenario save form.
2. The user clicks a "Save scenario" action.
3. The component validates that required calculator inputs are present and that a scenario name has been entered.
4. The component calls saveSavingsScenario, passing the scenario name and current calculator inputs (zipCode, monthlyElectricBill, roofOrientation, shadingLevel, systemSizeKw).
5. The backend recomputes the estimated savings and cost and persists a new SavingsScenario.
6. On successful response, the page optionally highlights the new scenario and then refreshes the scenarios list either by appending the returned SavingsScenario or by re-invoking getSavedSavingsScenarios.
7. The user can repeat this process with different system sizes or inputs to create multiple named scenarios for comparison, all listed on the same page.

Workflow 5: Reapply a saved scenario
1. From the saved scenarios list, the user clicks on a scenario entry to reuse it.
2. The scenario-list component reads the selected SavingsScenario object from its data set (populated by getSavedSavingsScenarios).
3. The component updates the calculator state fields (zipCode, monthlyElectricBill, roofOrientation, shadingLevel, systemSizeKw) to match the chosen SavingsScenario.
4. The component optionally scrolls or focuses the calculator form to indicate that inputs have changed.
5. The component invokes calculateSavingsEstimate with the restored inputs to ensure results reflect current backend logic.
6. The results panel updates with the new estimate data, and the URL parameters (zip, monthlyBill, systemSizeKw) are updated to reflect the applied scenario.
7. The user can now modify the restored scenario’s inputs to explore variations or save a new scenario with a different name.

Workflow 6: Proceed to get a quote
1. After viewing a satisfactory estimate from calculateSavingsEstimate, the user clicks the "Get a quote for this system" action in the results panel.
2. The quote-cta component checks that required inputs (zipCode, monthlyElectricBill, systemSizeKw) are currently valid.
3. The component constructs a URL to get_quote.html, appending query parameters zip, averageBill, and systemSizeKw using the current calculator state values.
4. The browser navigates to get_quote.html with these parameters, where the quote page can prefill its form based on the incoming values.
interactions:
Form input interactions
- ZIP code field: onChange updates internal calculator state and immediately updates the zip URL parameter. Optional onBlur validation can mark the field if empty or invalid.
- Monthly electric bill field: onChange updates internal state and the monthlyBill URL parameter. Validation ensures it is a positive numeric value.
- Roof orientation dropdown: populated with roofOrientationOptions from getSavingsCalculatorDefaults; onChange updates internal state.
- Shading level dropdown: populated from shadingLevelOptions; onChange updates internal state.
- System size control (slider and/or numeric input): configured using systemSizeRangeKw.min, systemSizeRangeKw.max, and systemSizeRangeKw.step; onChange clamps values within the range and updates systemSizeKw state and URL parameter.

Estimate calculation interaction
- "Calculate savings" button: onClick/onSubmit validates current inputs and, if valid, calls calculateSavingsEstimate.
- While awaiting a response, the button is disabled and a loading state is shown in the results panel.
- On success, the results panel binds to the returned estimatedAnnualSavings, estimatedSystemCost, estimatedAnnualProductionKwh, and paybackYears.
- On error, the component shows a generic error message and keeps the previous successful estimate (if any) visible.

Target-based adjustment interaction
- Target savings input: onChange updates a local targetSavings value; optional format validation ensures it is numeric.
- "Adjust system size" button: onClick triggers the iterative logic to modify systemSizeKw by systemSizeRangeKw.step within the allowed min/max, calling calculateSavingsEstimate after each change.
- During adjustment, system size controls and relevant buttons can be temporarily disabled to prevent conflicting user actions.
- The interaction ends when a suitable system size is found or when the range limits are reached; a status message communicates the outcome.

Scenario saving interaction
- Scenario name field: onChange updates local scenarioName; validation ensures it is non-empty when saving.
- "Save scenario" button: onClick validates name and required inputs; on success calls saveSavingsScenario with the current state.
- After a successful response, the new SavingsScenario is added to the scenarios list (or the list is reloaded via getSavedSavingsScenarios), and the form clears the scenarioName field.
- Any errors in saveSavingsScenario prompt a message, and the existing list remains unchanged.

Scenario list interaction
- On initial page load, the scenario-list component calls getSavedSavingsScenarios and binds the returned array to a list view.
- Each scenario row exposes a primary action (e.g., onClick) that re-applies the scenario to the calculator: it updates calculator state and triggers calculateSavingsEstimate.
- Optional secondary actions (such as simple selection highlighting) do not modify data but update local UI state to indicate the active scenario.
- The currently active scenario can be reflected in the URL using a custom parameter (for example, scenarioId) to support reloading into the same selection, resolved client-side from the list returned by getSavedSavingsScenarios.

Quote navigation interaction
- "Get a quote for this system" button in the results panel: onClick checks that a valid estimate exists or that minimum required inputs are populated.
- The component then constructs a navigation URL: get_quote.html with query parameters zip, averageBill, and systemSizeKw populated from the current calculator state.
- The browser navigates to the constructed URL; no additional interfaces are invoked from this page for this action.

URL synchronization interaction
- On each relevant state change (ZIP code, monthly bill, system size, and optionally selected scenario), a URL-state-sync component updates the browser URL query parameters using the history API without reloading the page.
- On page load, the same component reads existing URL parameters to initialize calculator state before any interface calls that depend on these values.
- If a parameter is removed or cleared (e.g., user clears the ZIP field), the corresponding URL parameter is removed or set to an empty representation, keeping the URL consistent with the visible state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the primary mechanism for persisting and restoring calculator state, avoiding localStorage. On initial load, the page parses the query string for the supported incoming parameters: zip, monthlyBill, and systemSizeKw. If present and valid, these values are used to initialize the calculator inputs. After initialization, the page calls getSavingsCalculatorDefaults to retrieve dynamic ranges and options; the systemSizeKw parameter is then validated against systemSizeRangeKw. If systemSizeKw is outside the allowed range or not provided, the system size control is initialized to a valid value within the range defined by the defaults. The page then calls getSavedSavingsScenarios to load all existing SavingsScenario records, which are held in memory for the current session; no scenario data is stored in URL parameters by default, but an optional URL key such as scenarioId can be used to indicate which scenario should be automatically applied. In that case, after getSavedSavingsScenarios resolves, the component locates the matching scenario from the returned list and updates the calculator state before optionally calling calculateSavingsEstimate. Throughout the session, whenever the user changes ZIP code, monthly bill, or system size, the URL-state-sync logic updates the URL's zip, monthlyBill, and systemSizeKw parameters to reflect the current state, using the history API to avoid page reloads. This enables deep-linking: anyone opening the URL arrives at the same base configuration, and the page can re-trigger calculateSavingsEstimate once all required inputs are valid. When a user navigates to get_quote.html via the quote CTA, the page constructs a URL including zip, averageBill, and systemSizeKw derived from the current calculator state so that get_quote.html can prefill its own form based on these values. No calculator or scenario data is persisted using localStorage; all persisted scenario data comes from and is refreshed via getSavedSavingsScenarios, and all calculations rely exclusively on calculateSavingsEstimate or saveSavingsScenario as defined by the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: On page initialization, loads default options and ranges for the calculator via getSavingsCalculatorDefaults, merges them with any incoming URL parameters, and initializes shared calculator state.

Collects core calculator inputs: ZIP code, average monthly electric bill, roof orientation, and shading level. It validates inputs, updates shared state, and keeps ZIP and bill values synchronized with URL parameters.

Provides a system size selector (such as a slider and numeric input) constrained by systemSizeRangeKw from getSavingsCalculatorDefaults, along with an optional target-savings adjustment feature that iteratively modifies system size and triggers new estimates.
Central action to call calculateSavingsEstimate with the current calculator inputs, handle loading and error states, and store the latest estimate in shared state for downstream components.

Displays the latest estimate returned by calculateSavingsEstimate, including annual savings, system cost, production, and payback period. It reacts to new estimates and provides contextual messaging when no estimate is available or when errors occur.

Allows the user to assign a name to the current calculator configuration and save it as a SavingsScenario via saveSavingsScenario, using the current inputs. On success, it updates the scenarios list and clears the name field.
Loads all saved SavingsScenario records using getSavedSavingsScenarios, displays them in a list, and lets users reapply a scenario to the calculator by updating inputs and recalculating the estimate.

Uses the current calculator inputs (ZIP code, monthly bill, and system size) to construct a navigation URL to get_quote.html with the corresponding query parameters when the user wants a quote for the current system.

Synchronizes key calculator state fields (zip, monthlyBill, systemSizeKw and optionally selected scenario) with URL parameters on change, and reads them on initialization to restore state before invoking interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a shallow full-width top info bar with contact details, followed by a slightly taller white navigation bar, then a full-width hero image with a dark blue overlay and a centered 'Solar Savings Calculator' headline and short supporting copy. Below the hero, the main calculator area is a two-column layout on desktop: a dominant left column (~65–70% width) containing the results panel and input controls stacked vertically, and a right sidebar (~30–35% width) with a single vertical stack of cards for saving scenarios, viewing the scenario list, and a quote call-to-action. Non-visual controller/state components are anchored to this main calculator section without visible UI of their own.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a shallow full-width top info bar with contact details, followed by a slightly taller white navigation bar, then a full-width hero image with a dark blue overlay and a centered 'Solar Savings Calculator' headline and short supporting copy. Below the hero, the main calculator area is a two-column layout on desktop: a dominant left column (~65–70% width) containing the results panel and input controls stacked vertically, and a right sidebar (~30–35% width) with a single vertical stack of cards for saving scenarios, viewing the scenario list, and a quote call-to-action. Non-visual controller/state components are anchored to this main calculator section without visible UI of their own.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top info bar occupies roughly 8–10% of the initial viewport height, the navigation bar about 10–12%, and the hero image section about 40–45%, ensuring the hero headline and short description are clearly above the fold. A vertical gap of roughly 48–64px separates the hero from the main calculator section. Within the main calculator section, the left column spans about 65–70% of the content width and vertically hosts a tall, visually prominent results panel card at the top (taking roughly the upper half of the visible calculator area) with the input form and system size control below. The right sidebar spans about 30–35% of the content width and stacks two medium-height cards (scenario save form and scenario list) and a compact but visually strong quote CTA card. Cards have 24–32px internal padding; vertical gaps between stacked cards and elements are 16–24px, aligned to the 8px base spacing system.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero area is visually open with large typography, generous top and bottom padding, and minimal elements. The main calculator section is moderately dense: the results panel emphasizes a clean, bold summary with charts or key metrics, while the input form packs several fields together but maintains comfortable 16–24px spacing between field groups. The sidebar is slightly denser, with lists and CTAs stacked in cards, but each card still has clear internal padding and separation so it never feels cluttered. Overall, density increases slightly as the user moves from the airy hero into the functional calculator, mirroring a typical corporate WordPress template.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern: starting at the top left with the company logo and address in the info/nav bars, moving right toward the top-right contact info and global 'Get a Free Quote' CTA, then diagonally down to the centered hero headline and supporting text. From there, the eye drops down to the left to the large, prominent savings results panel (acting like the project image in the mockup), then moves to the right to the elevated sidebar cards where users can save and browse scenarios and follow the quote CTA. Inside the left column, the flow is top-to-bottom: high-impact results at the top, followed by input fields and the system size slider, ending on the main 'Calculate Savings' action button. The sidebar flow is also top-to-bottom: save current scenario, view existing scenarios, then a strong quote CTA at the bottom.

layout.overall_description:
Below the corporate-style top info bar and clean white navigation bar, a full-width hero image with a subtle dark blue overlay fills nearly half the initial viewport height. Centered within this hero, a large white headline reads 'Solar Savings Calculator,' accompanied by a short explanatory subtitle and a simple primary call-to-action that scrolls the user down to the calculator. The hero has generous vertical padding, with the headline and text vertically centered in the image area, maintaining the open, marketing-focused feel. Beneath the hero, a 56–64px vertical gap introduces the main calculator section, which sits on a white background within a constrained content container aligned to an implicit 12-column grid.

The main section splits into a two-column layout: on the left, occupying roughly two-thirds of the width, a tall, prominent card immediately shows the estimated annual savings and solar production—this is the primary visual anchor, echoing the large project image from the mockup. Directly below it, separated by about 24px, lies a structured input form card containing location, energy cost, and roof parameters, with a horizontal system size slider near the bottom and a bold 'Calculate Savings' button aligned to the right. On the right, occupying the remaining third of the width, a vertical sidebar stacks three cards: a compact scenario save form at the top, a slightly taller card listing saved scenarios in the middle, and a standout quote CTA card at the bottom. The sidebar column appears slightly elevated with consistent card padding and 24px gaps between cards, mirroring the mockup’s categories card. As users interact, a subtle loading overlay occasionally appears over the left column while default values or recalculated results load, but otherwise all elements scroll naturally. Overall, the layout feels like a polished, modern corporate template: structured, predictable, and easy to scan, with the main savings numbers visually leading and supportive actions organized cleanly to the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calculator-defaults-loader is a non-visual controller that manifests only when defaults are being fetched or calculations are in progress. Spatially, it occupies the entire left calculator column as an overlay that sits above both the savings-results-panel and the savings-input-form. When active, a semi-transparent dark blue-tinted veil covers the full width of the left column from just below the hero’s bottom edge down through the full height of the calculator cards currently in view, with a centrally aligned spinner and a brief 'Loading your default savings estimate…' label. Horizontally, the overlay spans the same width as the left column (roughly 65–70% of the content width); vertically it extends from the top edge of the results panel card to slightly past the bottom of the form card, preserving a consistent 16–24px inset margin so the rounded card edges remain subtly visible underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay within the main calculator container, covering the full left column when active while allowing the page header, hero, and right sidebar to remain visible and scrollable; hidden entirely when inactive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The savings-input-form sits in the left column directly below the savings-results-panel, aligned to the same left and right edges to form a clean vertical stack. It occupies the full width of the left column (about 65–70% of the total content width) and appears as a white, elevated card with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. A 24px vertical gap separates it from the bottom of the results panel above. Inside the card, the form fields are arranged in a two-column grid on desktop where appropriate (e.g., location and utility fields side by side), with 16–24px vertical spacing between rows and 16px between columns, reflecting the moderate density of the mockup’s main content. At the bottom of the card, the system-size-control row and the calculate-action button sit, with the system size slider occupying the left 70–80% of the card width and the button aligned to the bottom-right edge. The card’s overall height is flexible, expanding as needed with fields, but on initial load it typically fills the visible space beneath the results panel without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal document flow within the left column; it scrolls naturally with the page, maintaining its position below the results panel and above the bottom of the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The system-size-control is presented as a prominent horizontal slider row located in the lower third of the savings-input-form card, visually grouped with the other form inputs but treated as a distinct control band. Within the card’s width, the control spans nearly full width, leaving 24–32px horizontal padding on each side. On desktop, a label and current kW/system size value align to the left, the slider track runs centrally across approximately 60–70% of the card width, and minimum/maximum markers sit near the track ends. Vertically, the slider row occupies about 72–96px of height, with 16px of top margin separating it from the preceding field group and 16–24px of bottom margin before the calculate-action button. This placement makes the system size feel like the last key adjustment before triggering the calculation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded within the savings-input-form card’s layout, following the form’s internal grid; it does not have independent scrolling or sticky behavior and resizes responsively with the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calculate-action is a bold, primary button placed at the very bottom-right of the savings-input-form card, visually anchoring the form’s interaction. It is horizontally aligned with the right inside edge of the card, spanning roughly 30–40% of the card’s width on desktop, with 12px vertical and 24px horizontal padding to give it a substantial, tappable feel. A 16–24px vertical gap separates it from the system-size-control above, creating a clear end point to the form. When the viewport is narrower, the button can expand to full card width and center-align to maintain prominence. Its color contrasts strongly with the white card (e.g., solid brand blue) and matches the CTA style seen in the top bar, reinforcing its importance. The button’s top edge aligns visually with the bottom of the slider row, making the form’s final interaction sequence easy to scan from top to bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains within the savings-input-form card and participates in normal flow; it stays just above the bottom padding of the card and scrolls with the rest of the form content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The savings-results-panel is the dominant element in the main calculator section, occupying the top of the left column directly beneath the hero’s lower margin. It spans the full width of the left column (about 65–70% of the content width) and appears as a large, elevated white or very light card with strong emphasis on the key savings metrics. Vertically, it takes up approximately 40–50% of the height of the visible main content area on initial load, making it the first element users’ eyes land on after leaving the hero. Inside, the top portion features a bold headline numeric (e.g., annual savings) across the left two-thirds, possibly accompanied by a small visual like an icon or badge on the right; below that, a simple bar or line chart and a few key breakdown stats are arranged in a two-column layout with 16–24px spacing. A 32–40px margin separates the panel from the hero above, and a 24px margin separates it from the savings-input-form card below, maintaining the mockup’s generous vertical rhythm. This panel acts like the main project image in the original design, anchoring the left column visually.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow at the top of the left column; it scrolls with the page and does not stick, though its position near the top after the hero ensures visibility above the fold on standard laptop viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scenario-save-form is the first card in the right sidebar column, aligned to the top of the main calculator section so that its top edge roughly lines up with the top edge of the savings-results-panel on the left. It occupies the full width of the sidebar (about 30–35% of the content width) and appears as a compact white card with 24px internal padding. Inside, a simple label or heading (e.g., 'Save this Scenario') sits at the top, with a single-line text field for naming the scenario and a small secondary-style save button aligned to the right or below the field. The card is relatively shallow—about 120–160px tall—ensuring it doesn’t overpower the sidebar but is clearly visible. A 24px vertical gap separates this card from the scenario-list card below, echoing the separation seen between sections in the mockup’s sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Placed within the right sidebar column and participates in normal flow. On larger screens, the sidebar column (containing this card, the scenario-list, and quote-cta) may be within a sticky container that keeps the top of the sidebar visible once the user scrolls past the hero, but the form card itself does not have independent sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scenario-list sits directly below the scenario-save-form in the right sidebar, separated by a consistent 24px vertical gap. It spans the same full width of the sidebar column and appears as a taller white card with 24–32px internal padding, mirroring the dimensions and visual style of the 'Categories' card in the mockup. At the top, a card header (e.g., 'Saved Scenarios') is left-aligned; below it, individual scenarios are listed in a single column, each row separated by 8–12px vertical spacing and possibly a subtle divider line. The list’s height is flexible: on initial load it might show 3–5 items before scrolling, with the card itself extending to fit the available sidebar height but not exceeding the height of the left column’s cards by a large margin. If the list grows longer, internal scrolling or pagination can be used while the card remains visually a single block. This card acts as the right-hand visual anchor balancing the results panel on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives within the right sidebar column’s normal flow. If the sidebar container uses sticky positioning, this card’s top will stay within the viewport as the user scrolls through the left column content, but it does not independently float; it remains stacked between the scenario-save-form above and the quote-cta below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-cta is the final card in the right sidebar stack, positioned directly beneath the scenario-list card with a 24px vertical gap between them. It spans the full sidebar width and appears as a compact but visually strong CTA block—either a solid brand-blue background card with white text or a white card with a large, high-contrast button. Internally, it has about 24px padding, with a concise heading (e.g., 'Ready to Go Solar?') at the top and a single, wide button below that stretches across roughly 80–100% of the card width. The card’s height is modest (around 120–160px), ensuring it is immediately visible once the user glances at the bottom of the sidebar. Its placement as the last element in the Z-pattern (after viewing results and exploring scenarios) reinforces a clear next step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides at the bottom of the right sidebar column within normal flow. On desktop, if the sidebar is made sticky, this CTA will remain visible near the lower part of the viewport as users scroll through the left column content, but it does not detach from the sidebar column or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is entirely non-visual and does not present any UI on the page. Conceptually, it is attached to the main calculator section, listening to changes in the savings-input-form, system-size-control, and currently selected scenario from the scenario-list. It synchronizes these states with the browser’s URL and restores them when the page loads. It has no width, height, or visible position; instead, it operates behind the scenes, ensuring that the content and layout state of the left column and sidebar remain consistent when users share or revisit the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual spatial behavior; functions entirely in the background without affecting layout or flow of visible components.

name: Installers Search
title: Installers Search
description: Search for local solar installers by ZIP code, distance, rating, and review count, and open detailed installer profiles.

core_features: Initialize search filters and sort options by calling getInstallerSearchFilterOptions, then binding the returned distance, rating, review count, and sort option lists to the page controls.

Allow users to enter a ZIP code and select a search radius, minimum rating, minimum review count, and sort option, with all active criteria kept in sync with URL parameters (zip, distanceMiles, minRating, minReviews, sortBy).
Trigger installer search requests via searchInstallers using the current criteria and display the returned installers list and totalCount.
Enable sorting of results using sort options provided by getInstallerSearchFilterOptions and mapped to searchInstallers parameters (sortBy and sortDirection).
Provide clickable installer result cards that navigate to installer_profile.html with the selected installerId in the URL.
Handle loading, error, and empty-result states for the search results without using any static or hardcoded data.
user_workflows: Landing with prefilled ZIP from another page: (1) User opens installers_search.html, potentially with zip and other criteria in the URL. (2) Page reads URL parameters (zip, distanceMiles, minRating, minReviews, sortBy). (3) Page calls getInstallerSearchFilterOptions to populate all filter and sort controls. (4) Page applies URL parameters to the controls where matching options exist. (5) If a ZIP code (and optionally distanceMiles) is present, page calls searchInstallers using the resolved criteria. (6) When results return, the page shows the installers list and totalCount. (7) User can click any installer card to open installer_profile.html?installerId={id}.

Starting a new search without prefilled criteria: (1) User opens installers_search.html without search-related URL parameters. (2) Page calls getInstallerSearchFilterOptions and binds the returned options to the controls. (3) Page sets initial filter control values based on the returned options (e.g., choosing the first available option where required) but does not call searchInstallers until a ZIP code is entered. (4) User enters a ZIP code and optionally adjusts distance, rating, review count, and sort option. (5) User submits the search form. (6) Page updates the URL with the chosen criteria (zip, distanceMiles, minRating, minReviews, sortBy) and calls searchInstallers. (7) Page renders the returned installers and totalCount. (8) User clicks an installer card to navigate to installer_profile.html with installerId set to that installer’s id.

Refining results with filters and sort: (1) User views existing search results. (2) User changes distance, minimum rating, minimum review count, or sort option in the controls. (3) On each change (or when the user applies the changes), the page updates the URL parameters to reflect the new criteria. (4) The page calls searchInstallers again with the updated parameters. (5) Installers list updates according to the new filters/sort and displays the updated totalCount.

Handling no-results and retry: (1) User runs a search and searchInstallers returns totalCount equal to zero. (2) Page displays an empty-results message component and suggestions to adjust filters (without hardcoded thresholds or text content, relying on generic messages). (3) User modifies filters (e.g., increasing distance or lowering minimum rating) and the page updates URL parameters and calls searchInstallers again. (4) When results become available, the installers list replaces the empty-results message.

Deep linking and page refresh: (1) User copies the URL from the Installers Search page (including zip, distanceMiles, minRating, minReviews, sortBy). (2) Later, the user or another user opens that URL. (3) Page reads the URL parameters on load, populates the controls to match, and immediately calls searchInstallers with those parameters. (4) The user sees consistent results that correspond to the shared URL state.
interactions: On initial page load, the page triggers getInstallerSearchFilterOptions. When the response returns, the filter and sort controls are populated with the dynamic options. If URL parameters are present, the controls are set to match them.

When the ZIP code input changes and the user submits the search form (via a button click or pressing Enter), the page composes the current criteria (zip, distanceMiles, minRating, minReviews, sortBy), updates the URL query string with them, and then calls searchInstallers using those values.

When a distance option is selected or changed, the page updates the distanceMiles URL parameter and, if a ZIP code has already been entered, immediately re-runs searchInstallers with the updated criteria.
When a minimum rating threshold is selected or changed, the page updates the minRating URL parameter and re-runs searchInstallers with all current criteria if a ZIP code has been set.
When a minimum review count threshold is selected or changed, the page updates the minReviews URL parameter and re-runs searchInstallers with the updated filters if the ZIP code is set.

When a sort option is selected or changed, the page updates the sortBy URL parameter, derives the sortDirection from the selected option value when possible, and calls searchInstallers to refresh results.
While searchInstallers is in progress, the page shows a loading state and disables repeated submissions until a response or error is received.

If searchInstallers returns an error, the page shows an error message component and allows the user to retry by resubmitting the search form or adjusting filters, which triggers a new searchInstallers call.
When searchInstallers returns successfully, the page binds the installers array to the results list component and shows totalCount in a results summary component.
When the user clicks an installer card in the results list, the page navigates to installer_profile.html with installerId set to the clicked installer’s id in the query string.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL parameters and interface responses. On load, the page reads zip, distanceMiles, minRating, minReviews, and sortBy from the current URL query string. It first calls getInstallerSearchFilterOptions to obtain dynamic lists: distanceOptionsMiles, ratingThresholdOptions, reviewCountThresholdOptions, and sortOptions. The page then reconciles URL parameters with these option lists: if a URL value exists and matches one of the returned options, that option is selected in the corresponding control; if no URL value exists for a required control, the page may select a default based solely on the dynamic options (for example, the first item in a list), without introducing any static or hardcoded values. For sort state, the selected sortOptions item determines the sortBy parameter; sortDirection for searchInstallers is derived algorithmically from the sort option value when the value syntax includes a direction indicator (for example, parsing a suffix such as 'asc' or 'desc'), otherwise sortDirection is left undefined so that backend defaults can apply. Whenever the user changes any search control or submits the form, the page serializes the current control values back into the URL query string (overwriting zip, distanceMiles, minRating, minReviews, sortBy as appropriate) using in-page navigation (e.g., updating window.location.search or using history APIs) while keeping the path installers_search.html unchanged. The URL thus becomes the single source of truth for the search criteria. After URL updates, the page calls searchInstallers using zipCode from the ZIP control, distanceMiles from the distance control, minOverallRating from the rating control, minReviewCount from the review count control, and sortBy/sortDirection derived from the selected sort option. The page maintains transient UI state flags (such as 'isLoading', 'hasError', and 'hasResults') based on the lifecycle of the searchInstallers call but does not persist these flags via URL or any local storage. Reloading the page or navigating back to the page always reconstructs state solely from URL parameters and fresh interface calls.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and search execution. On page load, it parses zip, distanceMiles, minRating, minReviews, and sortBy from the URL, invokes getInstallerSearchFilterOptions, applies URL-driven defaults to controls, and conditionally triggers searchInstallers. It also listens for events from child components when criteria change, updates the URL accordingly, and re-runs searchInstallers with the updated criteria.

Provides the main search inputs: ZIP code and search button. It also displays, but does not manage, the filter controls that are provided by sibling components. When the user submits the form, it emits the combined criteria to the state controller to update the URL and call searchInstallers.

Displays and manages the filter inputs for distance, minimum rating, and minimum review count. It consumes distanceOptionsMiles, ratingThresholdOptions, and reviewCountThresholdOptions from the getInstallerSearchFilterOptions response. It selects values based on URL parameters or dynamic defaults and emits filter change events so the state controller can update the URL and trigger searchInstallers.

Displays sort options retrieved from getInstallerSearchFilterOptions.sortOptions and lets the user choose how to order results. The selected sort option’s value is stored in the sortBy URL parameter. The state controller uses this value to determine sortBy and sortDirection for searchInstallers.

Shows a dynamic summary of the current search results, such as the total number of installers found for the given criteria. It binds to the totalCount returned by searchInstallers and to the currently active criteria so that messaging remains consistent with the URL state.

Renders the list of installers returned by searchInstallers. It receives the installers array and produces a list of installer cards. Each card is interactive and navigates to installer_profile.html with the matching installerId when clicked.

Represents a single installer in the search results. It binds to one Installer object from the installers array (including fields such as id, name, overall_rating, review_count, distance_miles, and any other available metadata). When the card is clicked, it triggers navigation to installer_profile.html?installerId={installer.id}.

Displays when searchInstallers returns totalCount equal to zero. It provides a generic message indicating that no installers were found for the current criteria and prompts the user to adjust filters or distance, without relying on any static example values. It listens for filter changes or a new search request to hide itself when new results arrive.

Shows and hides a loading state while searchInstallers requests are in progress. It is controlled by the state controller’s isLoading flag and is displayed during each call to searchInstallers until a success or error event occurs.

Displays a generic error message and optional retry action when searchInstallers fails (for example, due to network or server issues). When the user activates the retry action, it asks the state controller to repeat the last search using the current URL-driven criteria.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s structure: a full-width top info bar (site-wide), a full-width white navigation bar beneath it, then a full-width hero section for the Installers Search page with a large, centered headline over a darkened solar-themed image. Below the hero, the main content is a two-column layout: the left main column (about two-thirds width) holds the functional search experience (search form, summary, sort, results, states), while the right sidebar column (about one-third width) holds a single prominent filters card, visually analogous to the 'Categories' card in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s structure: a full-width top info bar (site-wide), a full-width white navigation bar beneath it, then a full-width hero section for the Installers Search page with a large, centered headline over a darkened solar-themed image. Below the hero, the main content is a two-column layout: the left main column (about two-thirds width) holds the functional search experience (search form, summary, sort, results, states), while the right sidebar column (about one-third width) holds a single prominent filters card, visually analogous to the 'Categories' card in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top info bar occupies about 8–10% of viewport height, the navigation bar about 10–12%, and the hero section roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height, matching the mockup’s emphasis on a bold hero area. The main content sits below the hero with a 48–64px gap. Inside the main section, the left column spans roughly 8 of 12 grid columns (~65–70% width), and the right sidebar spans about 4 of 12 columns (~30–35% width). Cards (search form, filters, result cards) use 24–32px internal padding and 16–24px gaps between stacked elements. Vertical spacing between main blocks (form vs summary/sort vs list) follows the 16–24px rhythm, while larger section breaks (hero to main content) follow the 48–64px spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero area is visually open with a large headline and generous vertical padding. The main content area is moderately dense: the search form and filters are clearly separated using card containers and white space, while the results list uses a tighter but still breathable vertical rhythm between cards. Controls (summary/sort) form a compact toolbar-like row to keep scanning efficient, while the sidebar filter card is comfortably spaced, echoing the lighter density of the mockup’s 'Categories' card.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern inspired by the mockup. Users first see the top-left logo/address area, then scan right to contact info and any header CTA. Their eye then drops diagonally to the center of the hero image, landing on the bold, centered 'Installers Search' headline. From there, the eye moves down to the left main column, where the ZIP search form and results summary anchor attention. The user then scans down through the installer results. In parallel, the right sidebar filters card sits visually elevated, catching attention as a secondary but important control zone, much like the mockup’s categories card. Within the left column, horizontal flow on the summary/sort row moves from left (summary) to right (sort control).

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a corporate-style solar services template: a slim colored info bar runs full-width at the very top, followed by a clean white navigation bar with the logo on the left and a 'Get a Free Quote'–style CTA on the right. Beneath this, a full-width hero section features a darkened photographic image of rooftop solar panels stretching edge-to-edge. Centered within this hero, vertically and horizontally, is a large white headline such as 'Find Local Solar Installers', with a short supporting line underneath; the typography is bold and spacious, matching the mockup’s marketing feel. Below the hero, after a generous 56px gap, the main content area appears on a light background, constrained to a 12-column grid. The left two-thirds of this grid form the main column: a white, card-like search form sits at the top, followed by a compact horizontal bar that shows the results summary on the left and a sort dropdown aligned to the right. Below this bar, a vertical list of installer result cards fills the column, each card slightly elevated against the background. When loading, empty, or error states occur, they appear in this same left column where the list would be, maintaining continuity. On the right, the remaining third of the grid hosts a single, tall filters card that aligns with the top of the search form and extends downward, containing neatly stacked filter controls. This sidebar card has ample internal padding and subtle shadow, echoing the mockup’s 'Categories' card. As users scroll, the left column flows with the results, while the filters card stays in view (within reasonable limits), reinforcing a professional, business-like layout that feels familiar to modern WordPress or Bootstrap-based marketing sites.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy any explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it is associated with the main content container that begins below the hero section, but it renders no UI. It sits logically at the top of the main content hierarchy, just under the hero, orchestrating which of the visible left-column components (loading indicator, error message, empty state, results list) is shown in the same designated results region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visible rendering; purely logical controller with no presence in the spatial layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is the first visible element in the main content area, positioned in the left main column directly below the hero section. It aligns flush with the left edge of the content grid and spans the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the central content width, about 8 of 12 grid columns). The top of the form sits about 48–64px below the hero’s bottom edge, creating a clear break between the hero and the functional search area. The form is contained within a white, card-like panel with softly rounded corners and a subtle shadow, using 24–32px padding on all sides. Internally, the fields (ZIP code, distance selector, etc.) are arranged in a horizontal row on larger screens: the ZIP input is left-most and visually dominant, taking around 40–50% of the card’s width; additional inputs (distance, maybe state or country, a 'Search' button) line up to the right, each with 16–24px horizontal gaps between them. On narrower viewports, these inputs stack vertically with 16px vertical spacing, maintaining the card’s internal padding. Below the fields, a small line of hint text or validation feedback aligns with the left edge of the ZIP field, separated by 8–12px. The bottom of the form card sits about 24px above the next row containing the results summary and sort control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow, anchored at the top of the left main column under the hero section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter controls occupy the dedicated right sidebar column, visually mirroring the mockup’s 'Categories' card. The sidebar begins at the same vertical level as the top of the search form, so the top edges of the search form (left) and filter card (right) align horizontally. The sidebar column spans roughly 30–35% of the content width (about 4 of 12 grid columns) and sits to the right of the main column with a consistent 24–32px horizontal gutter separating them. Within this column, the filter controls are grouped into a single tall white card with rounded corners and a subtle shadow, visually elevated from the background. The card spans almost the full width of the sidebar column, leaving a small 16–24px margin from the right edge of the page content. Inside the card, a section title (e.g., 'Filters') appears at the top, left-aligned, with 24px padding between the card’s top edge and the title. Filter sections (rating sliders, review count toggles, installer types, certifications, etc.) are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between them. Each subsection has enough internal spacing (16px around controls) to avoid visual clutter, matching the moderately spacious density of the mockup. The bottom of the card extends at least as far as the initial set of result cards in the left column; on pages with many results, the card may not reach the full height of the column content but remains visually anchored to the top and right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the viewport on desktop and tablet: after the user scrolls past its initial position, the filter card stays pinned near the top of the viewport within the right column, stopping before overlapping the footer. On smaller screens, it behaves as a normal block element, stacking above or below the results according to responsive behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control appears in the left main column as part of a horizontal bar directly below the search form card. This bar spans the full width of the left column. The results summary text is aligned to the left within this bar, while the sort control is aligned to the right, creating a left-right tension similar to toolbar layouts in corporate sites. The sort dropdown (or segmented control) itself is compact and sits flush with the right edge of the left column, with 8–12px internal padding and 16–24px horizontal padding around its label/value. Vertically, the center of the sort control aligns with the baseline or vertical center of the results summary text. There is roughly 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the search form and the top of this summary/sort row, and another 16–24px between the bottom of this row and the top of the first result card or state component. On smaller screens, the summary and sort control stack vertically, with the sort control appearing below the summary, left-aligned, and a 8–12px gap between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column’s flow, forming a consistent bar between the search form and the dynamic results/empty/error area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary is positioned in the left main column within the same horizontal row as the sort control, directly beneath the search form. It occupies the left side of this row, starting aligned with the left edge of the search form card above. The text (e.g., '24 installers found near 94103') runs on a single line on larger screens and takes up about 50–60% of the row’s width, leaving space on the right for the sort control. The vertical height of this row is determined by the size of the summary text and the sort control, with 8–12px of padding above and below to give the row breathing room. The summary text is slightly smaller than the hero headline but bold enough to be easily scanned. It sits about 16–24px below the bottom of the search form card, anchoring the user’s understanding of the results before they scroll down into the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow, horizontally paired with the sort control and vertically anchored between the search form and the results region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list sits in the left main column beneath the summary/sort row, occupying the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the content area). There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of the summary/sort row and the top of the first result card. The list is a vertical stack of installer result cards, each card extending nearly the full width of the column with a small 8–12px margin on the left and right to keep separation from the column edges. The vertical spacing between cards is consistent—around 16–24px—creating a clear visual rhythm as users scroll. As the user scrolls down, this list drives the main vertical flow of the page, while the right filter card remains visible.
