In loading, empty, or error states, this list area acts as the anchor region: the list of cards is replaced in this same zone by either the loading indicator, the empty state, or the error message, so the user’s focus remains in this left-column content block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the left column; the list grows vertically as more results are loaded and drives page height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each installer result card is a white, elevated panel inside the results list in the left main column. It spans nearly the full width of the column, with a small 8–12px gap from the column’s left and right boundaries, giving the impression of cards floating above the background. The card uses 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Layout-wise, the card can be divided into a left information block and a right action block on larger screens: on the left, the installer name appears at the top in bold, with the rating, review count, and location stacked underneath with 8–12px spacing between lines. On the right, aligned to the top, are key metrics or badges (e.g., overall rating emphasized) and a primary action button such as 'View Profile'. The right section typically consumes about 25–30% of the card’s width, while the left information section takes the remaining 70–75%. A subtle divider or faint vertical separation can be used between these sections if desired. On smaller screens, the right action block collapses beneath the main information, left-aligned, with 16px vertical spacing. The bottom of each card ends 16–24px above the next card, maintaining a tidy, scannable list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical list; cards expand vertically to fit their content but keep consistent padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no installers match the current search and filters, the empty state message appears in the left main column in the exact region normally occupied by the results list. It is centered horizontally within the left column, but still aligned with the column’s grid, not the full page width. The empty state is presented as a light, unobtrusive card or panel: a simple icon or illustration at the top, a headline (e.g., 'No installers found') below it, and a short explanatory paragraph and optional suggestion (e.g., 'Try expanding your search radius') beneath that. The panel uses 24–32px padding and sits approximately 32–40px below the summary/sort row, so it feels clearly associated with the search results. If suggestions or actions (like a 'Clear Filters' button) are present, they are center-aligned within the panel. The surrounding space is slightly more generous than between normal result cards, creating a calm, non-error feeling. There is still a visible right filter card, reinforcing that users can adjust filters to change the state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the results list region within the left column; static block that scrolls with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: During a search or filter update, the loading indicator appears in the left main column over the area where results are shown. On desktop, it is centered horizontally within the left column and roughly centered within the visible portion of the results area, just below the summary/sort row. It can appear either as a standalone inline spinner with a short 'Searching installers…' label or as a semi-transparent overlay on top of the existing list, dimming the result cards while keeping the structure visible. In both cases, the indicator does not extend beyond the left column and respects its width. Vertical space above and below the indicator remains consistent with the base spacing system, avoiding abrupt layout shifts; if the list is empty when the search starts, a 48–64px gap beneath the summary/sort row provides a comfortable buffer before the indicator. The right filter card remains fully visible and unaffected, emphasizing that filters are part of the ongoing operation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or occupies the results region within the left column; it does not affect the header, hero, or sidebar positioning and scrolls with the page if content extends beyond the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: If an error occurs (e.g., network issue), the error message appears in the left main column, directly beneath the search form and above the summary/sort row, ensuring it is clearly associated with the search action. It spans the full width of the left column and is styled as a prominent alert bar or card with a light red background tint and a red accent border on the left edge. The message text is left-aligned, with a short, bold error title and a concise explanation on the same or subsequent line. Internal padding of 16–24px on all sides keeps the message readable without feeling cramped. There is a 16px gap between the bottom of the error card and the summary/sort row. If the error is fatal (no results to show), the results list area below can either be empty or replaced by additional guidance, but the error component itself remains anchored above the summary/sort row in the vertical flow. The right filter card remains unchanged, indicating that the error pertains to data retrieval rather than page structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static alert within the left column; appears in the normal flow, pushing down the summary/sort row and results region while visible.

name: Installer Profile
title: Installer Profile
description: Displays detailed information about a selected solar installer, including ratings and reviews, and allows users to send a contact or quote request to that installer.

core_features: Load installer details based on the installerId URL parameter using getInstallerProfile and display name, rating, review count, certifications, service area and contact information.
Display a list of customer reviews associated with the installer returned from getInstallerProfile.
Provide a contact / quote request form bound to contactInstaller that lets users send project details and preferred contact method to the installer.
Offer direct contact actions such as calling the installer phone number or visiting their website when those fields are available from the installer data.
Provide a navigation link to the consultation booking page, passing the installer’s primary ZIP and a source identifier via URL parameters.
Handle loading, empty-state, and error states when installerId is missing, invalid, or when getInstallerProfile or contactInstaller fail.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View installer details
1. User arrives on installer_profile.html with an installerId query parameter.
2. On page initialization, the page logic reads installerId from the URL.
3. The page calls getInstallerProfile(installerId).
4. While waiting for the response, a loading state is shown in the installer information and reviews sections.
5. On successful response, installer details are bound to the installer summary components, and reviews are bound to the reviews list component.
6. If the installer is inactive or the interface returns an error, an error or unavailable state is displayed instead of the installer details.

Workflow 2: Send a contact / quote request to the installer
1. With installer details loaded, the user scrolls to the contact form section.
2. The user fills in required fields: name, phone, preferred contact method, and message (email is optional unless the user selects email as the preferred contact method).
3. The user submits the form.
4. Client-side validation runs; if validation fails (e.g., missing required fields, invalid email format when email is provided or required), errors are shown and submission is blocked.
5. If validation passes, the page calls contactInstaller(installerId, name, phone, email, preferredContactMethod, message).
6. While waiting for the response, the submit button is disabled and a submitting state is shown.
7. On success, a confirmation state is displayed (e.g., inline success message) using returned InstallerContactRequest data if needed, and the form may be cleared.
8. On error, an error message is displayed, and the user can correct data and resubmit.

Workflow 3: Navigate to schedule a consultation
1. With installer details loaded, the user finds the "Schedule consultation" action.
2. The action reads installer.primary_zip from the installer object.
3. When clicked, the user is navigated to consultation_booking.html with URL parameters zip set to installer.primary_zip and source set to installer_profile.
4. The consultation booking page uses the passed URL parameters to pre-fill or contextualize its own functionality.

Workflow 4: Use direct contact options (phone or website)
1. After installer details are loaded, the user sees phone and website fields if provided in the installer object.
2. If a phone number is present, clicking the phone action triggers a tel: navigation using the installer.phone value (outside of APIs).
3. If a website URL is present, clicking the website action opens installer.website_url in a new browser tab or the current tab, depending on implementation.
4. These interactions do not call any interfaces and do not modify page state except for standard navigation behavior.
interactions: On page load (onInit), read installerId from the URL query string and call getInstallerProfile(installerId).
On getInstallerProfile success (onLoadSuccess), update page state with installer and reviews, hide loading indicators, and render all bound components.
On getInstallerProfile error (onLoadError), display an error state and optionally provide a navigation hint back to the installers search page.
If installerId is missing or empty onInit, skip calling getInstallerProfile and immediately show a validation error that an installer could not be found.
In the contact form, onChange handlers update local form state for each field (name, phone, email, preferredContactMethod, message).
On contact form submit (onSubmit), run client-side validation against required fields and field formats before calling contactInstaller.
If contact form validation fails (onValidationError), mark invalid fields and show inline error messages without calling contactInstaller.
On successful contactInstaller response (onSubmitSuccess), show a confirmation message using the returned InstallerContactRequest, optionally reset the form, and keep the user on the same page.
On contactInstaller error (onSubmitError), display a submission error message and keep the form data so the user can retry.
On click of "Schedule consultation" (onClick), navigate to consultation_booking.html with zip and source URL parameters derived from the current installer data.
On click of the phone action (onClick), trigger a tel: link using installer.phone if present.
On click of the website action (onClick), navigate to installer.website_url if present.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the installerId URL query parameter (e.g., installer_profile.html?installerId={id}). On initialization, the page reads installerId from the URL. If installerId is present and non-empty, getInstallerProfile(installerId) is called. Until the response returns, the page is in a loading state for all installer-dependent components. On successful response, the installer object and associated reviews array become the active page state and are passed down to components as props or context. If installerId is missing, invalid, or getInstallerProfile fails, the page shows an error or 'installer not found' state and does not attempt to render components that require installer data. The outbound navigation to consultation_booking.html constructs its URL from the current installer.primary_zip and a fixed source=installer_profile value each time the consultation link is clicked; no state is stored in localStorage or other client persistence. Form state for contactInstaller is kept only in memory while the user is on the page. Optionally, implementations may encode a post-submit status (e.g., contactStatus=success or error) in the URL if a full page reload is desired after submission, but this is not required; all essential behavior can be handled without additional URL parameters beyond installerId.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading installerId from the URL query string, calling getInstallerProfile, and managing loading and error states for the entire profile.
event_handlers: onInit: Read installerId from window.location.search. If present, call getInstallerProfile(installerId); otherwise set an 'invalidInstaller' error state.
onLoadSuccess: When getInstallerProfile returns, store installer and reviews in page state and signal dependent components to render.
onLoadError: If getInstallerProfile fails, set an error state (e.g., installerLoadError) that dependent components use to display fallback messaging.

components.functionality: Displays key installer information such as name, overall rating, review count, distance, years in business, service radius, primary ZIP, certifications and high-level contact information.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When installer data in page state updates, re-render the summary fields using the latest Installer object.
onClickPhone: If installer.phone exists, trigger browser navigation to a tel: URL using that phone number.
onClickWebsite: If installer.website_url exists, navigate to that URL in a new tab or window.

components.functionality: Renders the list of customer reviews tied to the installer, including author, rating, date, and comment, and handles empty or loading states.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When the reviews array is updated by the loader component, render each review item with its provided fields.
onShowMore: Optional event if pagination or lazy loading is implemented, updating visible review count based on the current reviews array without fetching static data.

components.functionality: Captures user input to contact the installer or request a quote, then calls contactInstaller with the installerId and user-provided details.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize local form state with empty values for name, phone, email, preferredContactMethod, and message.
onChangeName: Update local state.name when the name input changes.
onChangePhone: Update local state.phone when the phone input changes.
onChangeEmail: Update local state.email when the email input changes.
onChangePreferredContactMethod: Update local state.preferredContactMethod (phone or email) when the user selects a contact method.
onChangeMessage: Update local state.message when the message textarea changes.
onValidate: Before submission, ensure name, phone, preferredContactMethod, and message are present; if preferredContactMethod is email, also ensure email is present and formatted correctly.
onSubmit: If validation passes, call contactInstaller(installer.id, state.name, state.phone, state.email, state.preferredContactMethod, state.message) and set a submitting state.

onSubmitSuccess: When contactInstaller returns an InstallerContactRequest, clear the submitting state, optionally reset form inputs, and expose confirmation details (e.g., request id or created_at) for display in a confirmation area.
onSubmitError: If contactInstaller fails, clear the submitting state and show an error message without clearing form fields so the user can retry.

components.functionality: Shows success or error messages related to contactInstaller submissions based on the latest submission result.
event_handlers: onSubmitSuccess: Receive the successful InstallerContactRequest object, display a success message, and optionally show fields like status or created_at if exposed.
onSubmitError: Display a generic or detailed error message derived from the error state set by the form component.
onDismiss: Allow the user to dismiss the success or error message, clearing the local feedback state.

components.functionality: Provides navigation to the consultation booking page with the installer’s primary ZIP code and a fixed source context encoded as URL parameters.
event_handlers: onClick: Construct a URL to consultation_booking.html with query parameters zip set to installer.primary_zip and source set to 'installer_profile', then navigate to that URL.
onDisable: If installer.primary_zip is not available, disable the link or show a tooltip indicating that scheduling is unavailable for this installer.

components.functionality: Displays an error or not-found message when installerId is missing or getInstallerProfile fails, and may provide a navigation hint to return to the search page.
event_handlers: onInit: If installerId is not present in the URL, set invalidInstaller to true and display a corresponding message.
onLoadError: When the loader component reports an error, set installerLoadError and display an error message.
onClickBackToSearch: Optional; navigate to the installers_search.html page without additional state when the user chooses to return.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a full-width top info bar (site-level, not part of these components), a full-width white navigation bar, then a hero section dedicated to the selected installer, and finally a two-column content section. The hero visually corresponds to the 'project hero' in the mockup and is primarily occupied by the installer-summary-header, centered over a subtle solar-themed or blue gradient background. Below the hero, the layout switches to the 12-column grid: the left 2/3 (about 8 columns) is the main content column containing the installer-reviews-list, while the right 1/3 (about 4 columns) is a sidebar card containing the installer-contact-form (and the schedule-consultation-link inside it). The installer-contact-confirmation and installer-error-state replace or overlay their respective primary areas when needed, without changing the global structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a full-width top info bar (site-level, not part of these components), a full-width white navigation bar, then a hero section dedicated to the selected installer, and finally a two-column content section. The hero visually corresponds to the 'project hero' in the mockup and is primarily occupied by the installer-summary-header, centered over a subtle solar-themed or blue gradient background. Below the hero, the layout switches to the 12-column grid: the left 2/3 (about 8 columns) is the main content column containing the installer-reviews-list, while the right 1/3 (about 4 columns) is a sidebar card containing the installer-contact-form (and the schedule-consultation-link inside it). The installer-contact-confirmation and installer-error-state replace or overlay their respective primary areas when needed, without changing the global structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertical space mirrors the mockup: the top info bar is shallow (~8–10% viewport height), the navigation bar slightly taller (~10–12%), and the hero with the installer-summary-header takes roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height so that the installer name and rating dominate above the fold. Below the hero, the main two-column content section occupies the remaining viewport height and scrolls. The left main column (reviews) is 65–70% width, the right sidebar (contact card) 30–35%. Cards like the contact form and confirmation use consistent internal padding of about 24–32px on all sides, with section gaps (hero to main content, main content to footer) around 48–64px. Within sections, elements (titles, controls, list items) use 16–24px vertical spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero/summary area is visually open and spacious: large installer name, prominent rating, and key stats with generous padding and whitespace, echoing the mockup’s open hero. The main two-column area is denser but still clean: the reviews list stacks multiple items with moderate spacing and clear dividers, while the sidebar contact card is compact yet readable, with fields laid out in a single column and consistent vertical rhythm. Status panels (confirmation and error) remain uncluttered, using concise messaging and icons with ample padding in line with the marketing feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the Z-pattern: users start at the top left with the logo/site identity, then scan right across contact or quote prompts in the top bar/nav, then are pulled diagonally down to the centered installer-summary-header in the hero. From there, the eye moves down and left into the main column where the installer-reviews-list anchors the detailed content, and then shifts right to the elevated contact card containing the installer-contact-form and schedule-consultation-link. When shown, the installer-contact-confirmation becomes the key focus on the right, and the installer-error-state, when present, temporarily interrupts and replaces the hero/main area as the primary focal card. The loader uses a centered overlay so attention is focused in the middle of the hero/main region while data is being retrieved.

layout.overall_description: The Installer Profile page appears as a professional, corporate-style marketing detail page with a familiar WordPress/Bootstrap structure. At the very top sit a slim info bar and a slightly taller white navigation bar stretching full width. Beneath them, a broad hero band fills roughly the upper half of the initial viewport, with a darkened solar-themed or blue gradient background and the installer-summary-header vertically centered: the installer’s name, rating, location, and key badges are large and bold, with plenty of breathing room, aligned centrally to create a strong focal point. Below this hero, the content transitions into a two-column grid: on the left, a wide main column runs down the page showing the installer-reviews-list as a series of stacked review cards; on the right, a narrower sidebar column hosts a prominent white card with soft shadow containing the installer-contact-form and a secondary schedule-consultation-link near its bottom. As users scroll through reviews, the sidebar card feels like a steady, accessible CTA column. When a message is sent, the same sidebar card area flips to show the installer-contact-confirmation, maintaining spatial continuity. If there is a loading state, a centered overlay loader covers the hero/main content area; if an error occurs, an elevated error panel replaces the hero/main content zone, centered in the viewport. The overall effect mirrors a modern service-profile template: strong hero, clear Z-pattern from top right CTA down to left main content and back to right contact card, generous but not minimalist spacing, and a trusted corporate aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full-viewport overlay centered over the hero and main content area, while leaving the very top info bar and navigation visually dimmed but still slightly visible behind a translucent backdrop. It spans 100% of the page width and covers from just below the navigation bar down to the bottom of the viewport. In the visual center of this overlay, a spinner or progress indicator is placed, with a short loading message directly below it. The central loader content is contained within an invisible square or circular area roughly 15–20% of the viewport width, with at least 48px of padding around the indicator and text to maintain the clean marketing look. The overlay uses a subtle dark or blue-tinted scrim so that the underlying hero and column layout are perceptible but clearly inactive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay covering the content area beneath the navigation bar; it sits above all other components, disables interaction with underlying elements, and is vertically and horizontally centered within the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The installer-summary-header is the main hero content and is positioned centrally within a full-width hero band directly below the navigation bar. The hero band runs edge-to-edge horizontally and occupies approximately 40–45% of the viewport height on desktop. Within this band, the summary header content is constrained to the main page max-width (e.g., 1080–1200px) and horizontally centered. The content block itself spans roughly 60–70% of that max width, aligned to the center, leaving generous whitespace on both left and right. Vertically, the block is centered within the hero band using ample top and bottom padding (around 64px each as a minimum, more on large screens if space allows). The installer’s name appears as a large, bold heading near the top of this block, with the rating, review count, location, and key service tags stacked below it with 16–24px vertical spacing. The text is left-aligned within the block for legibility but the overall block is centered, creating a balanced corporate hero. The background behind this header is a darkened image or gradient, ensuring high contrast so the summary header text becomes the dominant focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, occupying the hero section; on scroll it moves off-screen as the user reaches the main two-column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The installer-reviews-list sits directly below the hero section in the main content area, anchored to the left within the 12-column grid. It occupies approximately 8 of 12 columns (about 65–70% of the content width) and extends vertically down as reviews accumulate. There is a clear section break between the hero and the reviews list, with a vertical gap of about 48–64px. The left edge of the reviews column aligns with the left edge of the hero’s centered content container, so the transition from hero text to reviews feels continuous. Within this column, an optional section heading (e.g., 'Customer Reviews') is positioned at the top, followed by filter/sort controls if any, then the list of individual review cards. Each review card spans the full width of this column, with 24–32px internal padding and 16–24px gaps between cards, creating a moderately dense but orderly stack. The bottom of the reviews list flows naturally into the page footer, with at least 48px of margin below the final card to maintain breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling within the main left column; it defines most of the page’s vertical length, while the right sidebar remains visually parallel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The installer-contact-form is presented as a prominent sidebar card in the right column of the main content area, mirroring the 'Categories' card positioning in the mockup. It begins at nearly the same vertical position as the top of the reviews list, with a slight 16–24px offset down if needed, creating a balanced top alignment between columns. The form card occupies about 4 of 12 columns (30–35% width), aligned to the right edge of the content container. The card uses a white background, subtle drop shadow, and rounded corners, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Inside the card, a concise title such as 'Contact this installer' or 'Request a quote' sits at the top, followed by a brief descriptive line; below this, form fields are stacked vertically in a single column, each spanning 100% of the card’s inner width with 16–20px vertical gaps. The primary submit button is placed near the bottom of the card, spanning either full width or about 70–80% aligned left, with 16–24px of vertical space above and below relative to other elements. There is at least 32px padding between the last interactive element (e.g., submit button and schedule link) and the bottom edge of the card. The card height adjusts to content; on tall screens with fewer reviews, it may end above the bottom of the viewport, while on long review lists it scrolls with the page but maintains its sidebar role.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column by default; optionally configured as sticky on taller screens so that the top of the card remains visible while the user scrolls through the left-column reviews, without overlapping the page footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The installer-contact-confirmation occupies the exact spatial footprint of the installer-contact-form’s sidebar card. When active, the form fields are replaced by a confirmation panel within the same card container in the right column. The outer card retains its width (about 4 of 12 columns) and alignment to the right, with the same white background, shadow, and 24–32px internal padding. Inside, a success icon or badge is positioned near the top-left or centered horizontally, followed by a bold confirmation message (e.g., 'Your request has been sent') and a brief explanatory paragraph, all stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing. The content is slightly more open than the form: fewer elements with more breathing room, the message vertically centered relative to the card’s height on larger screens where possible. A secondary action (such as 'Back to profile' or 'View more installers') may be placed toward the bottom, aligned left, with at least 24px of padding between it and the card edges. The net effect is a clean, optimistic status screen that clearly replaces the form while preserving the user’s sense of location in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column, directly swapping in for the installer-contact-form in the same card container without altering surrounding layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-consultation-link is a secondary CTA placed inside the same sidebar card as the installer-contact-form. Within that card, it appears near the bottom, immediately below the primary submit button. There is typically 12–16px of vertical space between the button and the link, providing clear separation while keeping them visually associated. The link is left-aligned with the button’s label text, and may use a text-link or subtle ghost-button treatment in the brand’s accent blue, contrasting against the white card background. It does not expand the full width of the card; instead, it occupies only as much horizontal space as its label text requires, preserving a lighter, secondary feel. On narrower screens (e.g., mobile where the layout collapses to a single column), the link remains directly under the primary button, maintaining the same vertical spacing and alignment but now spanning the full content width visually. If the contact-confirmation is shown, this link may be hidden or replaced by a similarly placed link within the confirmation card, maintaining the same spatial position near the card’s lower area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the contact form card, anchored immediately below the primary submit button; it scrolls with the page as part of the sidebar content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The installer-error-state appears when the profile cannot be loaded and replaces the hero plus main content area with a single, focused status panel. It is positioned directly below the navigation bar, occupying the central region of the viewport. Horizontally, the error panel is centered within the main content max-width and spans roughly 50–60% of that width, leaving generous whitespace on either side. Vertically, it uses generous top and bottom padding (at least 64px above and below), and on larger screens it is visually centered within the viewport between the nav and the fold. The panel itself is a card with a white or very light background, subtle border or shadow, and 24–32px internal padding. At the top of the card, an error icon is displayed, followed by a clear error headline (e.g., 'We couldn’t load this installer’s profile') and a short explanatory text, each separated by 16–24px. Below this, any recovery actions (like 'Retry' or 'Back to search results') are grouped as buttons or links aligned to the left or center within the card, with 16–24px spacing. No hero or two-column layout is shown beneath; the error card is the main focal area, with a minimum of 48px of blank space below it before the footer or page edge, reinforcing its importance while keeping the layout clean and corporate.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the standard content flow, but when present it effectively takes over the space where the hero and main two-column content would appear, ensuring the user’s attention is focused on the error and recovery options.

name: System Builder
title: System Builder
description: Interactive step-by-step tool to configure a custom solar system by specifying system type, roof details, panel model and quantity, battery storage, inverter type, and notes, then saving the configuration as a SystemDesign to review on the System Summary page.

core_features: Load available builder options (system types, roof types, roof pitches, panel models, battery options, inverter types) using getSystemBuilderOptions, optionally filtered by the systemType URL parameter.
Guide users through a multi-step wizard: system type & roof details, panel selection, battery storage, inverter selection, and final review.
Create a new SystemDesign or update an existing one on each step submission using createOrUpdateSystemDesign, capturing the returned designId for subsequent updates.
Support editing an existing SystemDesign when designId is present in the URL by pre-populating steps with server-provided design data and then updating it through createOrUpdateSystemDesign.
Validate that, when battery storage is included, the selected battery option meets or exceeds the minimum required battery_capacity_kwh as defined by business rules.
Maintain the current SystemDesign state in memory during the session and keep the designId in the URL for re-entry and navigation from/to system_summary.html.
Provide a final action to navigate to system_summary.html with the current designId when the configuration steps are complete.
user_workflows:
Create a new system design:
1) User navigates to system_builder.html without a designId.
2) On page load, the builder reads any systemType URL parameter and calls getSystemBuilderOptions(systemType).
3) The user completes Step 1 (system type & roof details) and submits; the page calls createOrUpdateSystemDesign without a designId, receives a new SystemDesign (including id), and updates the URL to include ?designId=... .
4) The user progresses through panel selection, battery selection, and inverter selection; each step submission calls createOrUpdateSystemDesign with the current designId and updated fields.
5) After the final step, the user clicks "Review design" which navigates to system_summary.html?designId=currentDesign.id.

Edit an existing system design:
1) User arrives at system_builder.html?designId=... from system_summary.html.
2) The server renders the page with the existing SystemDesign data for that designId embedded in the initial page state, and the client calls getSystemBuilderOptions(systemType) using the design's system_type.
3) The wizard steps are pre-populated from the existing SystemDesign.
4) As the user changes any step and submits, the page calls createOrUpdateSystemDesign with the existing designId and updated fields.
5) When done, the user clicks "Review design" to go back to system_summary.html?designId=... with the same id.

Start the builder with a preselected system type:
1) User navigates from elsewhere via a link such as system_builder.html?systemType=residential (no designId).
2) On load, getSystemBuilderOptions is called with systemType from the URL; the System Type field is preselected accordingly.
3) The rest of the workflow follows the new system design flow, with the preselected system type treated as the initial value; subsequent step submissions create and update a SystemDesign as usual.
interactions: On page load, the builder parses URL parameters (designId, systemType), triggers loading of options via getSystemBuilderOptions, and initializes the wizard state (including current step and initial form values).

When the user changes the System Type field, the form updates its local state; on step submission, createOrUpdateSystemDesign is called and the returned SystemDesign.system_type is treated as the source of truth.
When the user selects a Roof Type or Roof Pitch option, the values are stored in the step state and validated as required fields before allowing progression to the next step.

When the user selects a Panel Model from the list, the selection is bound to SystemDesign.panel_model_id; changing the panel quantity updates the bound panel_quantity and may be used to compute a transient display of total_dc_capacity_kw using the selected panel’s wattage.

When the user toggles the "Include battery" option, the battery selection UI is enabled or disabled; if enabled, selecting a battery option binds its id and capacity to the SystemDesign, and validation checks that battery_capacity_kwh is at or above the configured minimum.

When the user selects an Inverter Type, the value is bound to SystemDesign.inverter_type using the inverterTypeOptions from getSystemBuilderOptions; the step cannot be submitted until a valid option is chosen.

Each step’s primary action (e.g., "Next" or "Save & Continue") triggers validation; if valid, it calls createOrUpdateSystemDesign with the accumulated SystemDesign fields; while the request is in progress, navigation controls are disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.
If createOrUpdateSystemDesign returns an error, the step displays a generic error message and keeps the user on the current step without updating the URL; the user may correct inputs and resubmit.

After the first successful createOrUpdateSystemDesign call that returns a new SystemDesign.id for a new design, the page updates the current URL (using history.replaceState or equivalent) to include designId, ensuring that reloading or returning to the page preserves the design.

Clicking the "Review design" button on the review step navigates to system_summary.html with the current designId passed as a URL parameter; if designId is not available due to a failed save, the navigation is blocked and an error message is shown.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The builder reads and writes state using URL parameters and in-memory state only; no localStorage or similar persistence is used.
- Incoming designId parameter: If present, the server is expected to provide the corresponding SystemDesign as initial state (e.g., via server-side rendering or a bootstrapped JSON object). The client treats this SystemDesign as currentDesign and uses its system_type when calling getSystemBuilderOptions. The designId value in the URL remains unchanged for the session and is sent on every createOrUpdateSystemDesign call.
- Incoming systemType parameter: If designId is absent, systemType is passed directly to getSystemBuilderOptions(systemType) and used to preselect the System Type field. If both designId and systemType are present and conflict, the SystemDesign.system_type associated with designId is authoritative; systemType in the URL is ignored for initialization. The systemType parameter itself is not modified by the page after load.
- Current design state: The client maintains an in-memory object currentDesign that mirrors the last successful SystemDesign returned by createOrUpdateSystemDesign (or server bootstrap when editing). Each step binds to and updates this object before calls.
- Synchronizing designId with URL: When createOrUpdateSystemDesign is called without a designId and returns a new SystemDesign.id, the page updates the browser’s current URL (e.g., via history.replaceState) to append or update the designId query parameter. Subsequent reloads of system_builder.html with this designId re-enter the edit workflow.
- Step progression state: The current step index is held in memory during the session. Reloading the page resets the user to the first step, but all fields associated with an existing designId are repopulated from the server-provided SystemDesign.
- Navigation to system_summary.html: The "Review design" action generates a navigation URL of the form system_summary.html?designId=currentDesign.id. No other parameters are required for system_summary.html to load the design.

components.functionality: Initializes the System Builder by parsing URL parameters (designId, systemType), loading selectable options via getSystemBuilderOptions, and seeding the wizard state with any existing SystemDesign provided by the server.

Controls the multi-step flow of the System Builder (system & roof, panels, battery, inverter, review), managing the active step, enabling/disabling navigation controls based on validation status and save progress, and coordinating with the persistence controller to save at each step.

Collects and edits the SystemDesign fields for system_type, roof_type, and roof_pitch for the first step of the builder, using options from getSystemBuilderOptions and preparing a payload for createOrUpdateSystemDesign when the user proceeds.

Allows the user to choose a panel model from the panelModels provided by getSystemBuilderOptions and specify the panel_quantity, binding these to SystemDesign.panel_model_id and SystemDesign.panel_quantity and passing them to the persistence controller on step submission.

Handles inclusion of battery storage by letting the user toggle include_battery and select a battery option from the batteryOptions list, binding SystemDesign.include_battery, SystemDesign.battery_option_id, and SystemDesign.battery_capacity_kwh, and enforcing that any selected battery has a capacity_kwh at or above the required minimum before allowing progression.

Presents inverterTypeOptions from getSystemBuilderOptions and lets the user select an inverter type, binding the selection to SystemDesign.inverter_type and triggering a save via the persistence controller when the step is submitted.

Provides an optional free-text notes field bound to SystemDesign.notes that can be edited on any step (commonly on the review step) and saved either when the user leaves the field or submits the current step.

Centralizes calls to createOrUpdateSystemDesign for all steps by accepting partial field updates from forms, merging them into the currentDesign object, invoking createOrUpdateSystemDesign with the current designId (if any) and all required fields, then updating currentDesign with the API response and exposing save status to the stepper.

Displays a summary of the current SystemDesign values (as available from currentDesign) and provides the "Review design" button that navigates to system_summary.html with the current designId once all required fields are valid and the last save has succeeded.

Aggregates and displays validation and API error messages for the current step (e.g., missing required fields, invalid battery capacity, or save failures) based on validation results and responses from the persistence controller.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the mockup’s pattern: full-width top info bar and navigation (global, outside this tool), followed by a full-width hero section introducing the System Builder. Below the hero, use a two-column layout: the left ~65–70% of the width is the interactive builder (stepper, validation, active step form(s)), and the right ~30–35% is a tall, card-style sidebar containing the review-and-navigation panel and any persistent, high-level context. Functional, non-visual components (loader, persistence controller) sit logically in relation to these visual chunks, with the loader overlaying the entire hero + main content area when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the mockup’s pattern: full-width top info bar and navigation (global, outside this tool), followed by a full-width hero section introducing the System Builder. Below the hero, use a two-column layout: the left ~65–70% of the width is the interactive builder (stepper, validation, active step form(s)), and the right ~30–35% is a tall, card-style sidebar containing the review-and-navigation panel and any persistent, high-level context. Functional, non-visual components (loader, persistence controller) sit logically in relation to these visual chunks, with the loader overlaying the entire hero + main content area when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Top info bar is shallow (~8–10% viewport height), nav slightly taller (~10–12%), hero occupies ~40–45% of initial viewport height with a large title and brief description. The main System Builder section fills the remaining vertical space, with the left column taking about two-thirds of the horizontal width and the right column one-third. Inside each column, key containers use 24–32px internal padding; vertical gaps between major blocks (hero to main, stepper to forms, forms to sidebar, etc.) are 48–64px, while internal element spacing uses 16–24px steps.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero is visually open and spacious, focused on a bold title and a short supporting line. The main builder area is denser but still breathable: the stepper, validation messages, and active form(s) are grouped with clear separation, using card-like containers and consistent padding. The sidebar review-and-navigation panel is compact but not cramped, resembling the ‘Categories’ card from the mockup: clearly segmented sections, comfortable padding, and enough whitespace around each control to remain scannable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a Z-pattern: from the top-left logo/info across to the top-right CTA in the global bars, then diagonally down to the centered System Builder hero title. From there, the eye moves left to the stepper and active form in the main content, then sweeps right to the elevated sidebar review-and-navigation panel. Within the left column, the stepper leads into validation messages (if any), then down to the active step form; on the right, the sidebar card remains visually stable and slightly elevated, acting as a consistent anchor for navigation and quick review.

layout.overall_description: The System Builder page sits inside the same corporate solar website shell as the marketing mockup: a slim blue-tinted info bar runs full-width at the very top, followed by a slightly taller white navigation bar with the logo on the left and main links and a primary CTA on the right. Below this, a full-width hero image—muted with a dark blue overlay—introduces the System Builder with a bold white headline centered both horizontally and vertically, plus a short line of supporting text beneath it. Generous top and bottom padding in the hero creates an open, confident feel. Scrolling just below the hero, the layout shifts into a two-column builder interface. A centered content container uses an implicit 12-column grid; the left 8 columns (about 65–70% width) contain the System Builder stepper, validation feedback, and whichever configuration step is currently active, all arranged in a linear top-to-bottom flow with 24–32px gaps. The right 4 columns (about 30–35% width) hold a tall, card-like review-and-navigation panel that visually mirrors the ‘Categories’ card from the mockup: a white card with subtle shadow, 24–32px internal padding, and clearly separated sections for progress, summary, and actions. The sidebar feels slightly elevated and stays visible as the user scrolls through longer forms, reinforcing the Z-pattern from hero to left-column content and back to the right sidebar. When the builder is loading or saving, a semi-transparent overlay dims the hero and main content, centering a loader indicator without breaking the overall two-column structure. Overall, the page feels like a professional, structured tool embedded in a polished marketing site, with familiar WordPress-style proportions, consistent spacing, and a clear, guided flow from start to finish.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full-screen overlay that covers the hero section and the entire main two-column builder area whenever data is being loaded or a configuration is being persisted. It sits above all other content, stretching 100% of the viewport width and height from just below the global navigation bar. A semi-transparent dark blue or charcoal layer softly dims the underlying hero image and the builder columns, preserving a hint of the layout beneath. At the visual center of the viewport, a compact loader module (spinner and short status text) is displayed, roughly 280–320px wide, with 24px internal padding and rounded corners, mimicking a card. The overlay respects the page’s central content alignment, so the loader feels anchored to the same grid even while it floats above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned fixed relative to the viewport, covering the entire interactive area beneath the global header; z-index elevated above all other components; appears and disappears dynamically without shifting the underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stepper is placed at the top of the main content area, aligned to the left column directly beneath the hero section. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the overall content width inside the central container), leaving a consistent margin to the left and right edges. Vertically, there is a generous 48–64px gap between the bottom of the hero and the top of the stepper, echoing the mockup’s separation between hero and main content. The stepper itself is a horizontal strip, visually similar to a flat card: 16–24px padding top and bottom, with step labels arranged from left to right and equal spacing between them. It sits about 24px above the next element (the validation-message-list), forming a clear top anchor for the sequence of forms below. On narrower screens, the stepper collapses into a more compressed horizontal or stacked layout but remains at the top of the left column content block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column’s content flow; scrolls with the page, always remaining the first element in the builder section under the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The System Type & Roof form occupies the top-most card position in the left column’s form stack, directly beneath any validation messages when this step is active. It uses a card-like container that spans the full width of the left column with 24–32px internal padding on all sides and slightly rounded corners, visually mirroring the main project image block from the mockup but filled with form fields instead of a photo. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of the stepper (or validation list) and the top of this form card. Fields are arranged in a vertical flow with 16–24px spacing between form rows, maintaining a moderately dense but organized layout. When this is the active step, it appears fully expanded; inactive steps either collapse into slim preview bars below or are hidden to keep the vertical density manageable, but in all cases this form’s logical position is the first major form block in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; appears as the top form card when its step is active and may be collapsed or hidden when the user moves to later steps, without changing its relative placement in the sequence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The panel selection form sits in the same left-column flow as the System Type & Roof form, positioned immediately below whichever step precedes it. It uses the same full-width card treatment: a white or very light background container with 24–32px internal padding, spanning the width of the left column. A 24px vertical gap separates it from the preceding form card or notes section to maintain visual hierarchy. Internally, panel model and quantity fields are grouped into logical clusters with 16–24px vertical spacing, creating a slightly denser, data-focused card that parallels the prominence of the large project image in the original mockup (this card becomes one of the key content anchors on the left side). When this step is active, the card expands to its full height; when inactive, it may be collapsed into a slimmer bar that still occupies the same alignment in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left-column sequence; scrolls normally, with its height changing based on active/inactive state but its horizontal and vertical position relative to other forms kept consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The battery selection form continues the vertical stack of forms in the left column, placed directly beneath the panel selection form with a 24px vertical gap. It also spans the full width of the left column as a structured card with 24–32px padding, preserving the consistent card rhythm established above. Option groups (battery types, capacities, counts) are arranged in a top-to-bottom flow, with 16–24px spacing between rows or grouped controls. Its visual weight is slightly less than the panel selection form—more like a midsize content block that sits between the major image analogue (panel selection) and the notes area—yet it’s still clearly a primary work area within the left column. When active, it occupies enough height to show all key inputs without feeling cramped but does not extend as tall as a hero-like block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; appears in sequence after panel selection, expanding or collapsing with the step state while maintaining its slot in the vertical order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inverter selection form appears as the next card in the left column, aligned flush with the other form cards and separated from the battery selection form by a 24px gap. It shares the same full-width card styling—24–32px internal padding, subtle shadow or border, and consistent typography—so all configuration steps feel part of a cohesive set. Within the card, inverter type and related fields are laid out vertically, using 16–24px spacing between field groups. This form’s overall height is moderate, in keeping with the slightly denser feel of the main content area from the mockup, and it functions as another segment in the left-side content column that collectively balances against the single, taller sidebar card on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; retains its position in the form sequence under the battery selection card, expanding or collapsing based on step activity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The design notes section lives at the bottom of the left-column stack of configuration steps, following the inverter selection form with a 24–32px vertical gap to indicate a slight shift toward wrap-up and commentary. It uses the same card-style container as the other forms, spanning the full left-column width with 24–32px padding. Unlike the more structured forms above, this card prioritizes a larger text area for freeform notes, so the main body is a textarea occupying most of the card height, framed by a label and possibly a brief helper text above it. Internal spacing keeps this area airy, with 16–24px between the label, helper text, and the textarea, giving it a feel closer to a content section than a dense form. This mirrors the way the mockup’s main content culminates in a more relaxed, textual region toward the bottom of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the left column’s main stack; scrolls with the rest of the content and does not collapse as aggressively, emphasizing that notes remain accessible even after other steps are completed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The persistence controller is non-visual but conceptually anchored to the entire left-column builder area. It is associated with the container that wraps the stepper, validation messages, and all the form cards. From a layout perspective, it does not occupy its own visible space; instead, it listens to changes across the left column and triggers save/persist interactions that are reflected via the loader overlay and potentially subtle feedback near the review-and-navigation panel. Its presence is inferred by the responsive behavior of the builder rather than seen as a discrete block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and layout-neutral; logically scoped to the left-column content area, controlling persistence for all steps without affecting spacing or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review-and-navigation panel occupies the entire right sidebar column, starting at the same vertical level as the stepper in the left column. It is rendered as a single, tall card that mirrors the ‘Categories’ sidebar card from the mockup: a white or very light background, subtle drop shadow, and rounded corners. The card spans roughly 30–35% of the central content width, with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. Inside, its content is stacked vertically: step overview/progress at the top, a compact summary of key selections beneath, and navigation controls (Next/Back, Save, maybe a primary CTA) at the bottom, each section separated by 16–24px. The top of the panel sits ~48px below the hero’s bottom edge, aligning visually with the stepper’s top to reinforce the Z-pattern from hero to left to right. Around the card, there is clear whitespace: at least 24–32px gap between the card’s left edge and the left column, and comfortable top/bottom margins so it never feels jammed against the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky sidebar on larger screens: once the user scrolls into the main builder area, the panel remains pinned within the viewport’s right side while the left-column forms scroll, up to the card’s natural bottom limit. On smaller screens, it stacks below the left-column content and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation message list sits within the left column between the stepper and the active form card. It spans the full width of the left column and appears as one or more horizontally full-width alert bars or a compact card with 16–24px internal padding and a slightly tinted background to stand out against the main page background. There is a 16–24px gap between the bottom of the stepper and the top of this component, and another 16–24px gap between its bottom and the top of the first form card (such as the System Type & Roof form). When multiple validation messages are shown, they are stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing, keeping them visually grouped but not crowded. The overall height of this area grows only as needed, maintaining the moderately dense but organized feel of the main content region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left-column flow; appears and disappears based on validation state, expanding or collapsing without changing the positions of the stepper or forms above and below.

name: System Summary
title: System Summary
description: Displays a configured solar system design with key specifications, estimated performance, and actions to save the design as a quote, edit the design, download a summary, or schedule a consultation.

core_features: Load a SystemDesign by designId from the URL using getSystemDesignSummary and display all key specifications (panels, capacity, battery, inverter, roof details, notes).
Show derived system metrics from getSystemDesignSummary (total DC capacity, estimated AC output, estimated annual production, estimated annual savings, estimated system cost).
Allow the user to save the current SystemDesign as a quote by calling saveSystemDesignAsQuote with an optional nickname, then navigate to quote_detail.html with the generated quotePlanId.
Provide navigation back to system_builder.html with the same designId so the user can edit the design.
Provide navigation to consultation_booking.html with a source parameter indicating that the user came from the System Summary.
Allow the user to download or print a summary of the design and derived metrics using client-side export functionality based on the loaded data.
user_workflows:
View system summary:
1. User navigates to system_summary.html with a designId URL parameter (e.g., system_summary.html?designId={id}).
2. On page load, the page reads designId from the URL.
3. If designId is present, the page calls getSystemDesignSummary(designId).
4. While the request is in progress, a loading state is shown instead of system data.
5. When getSystemDesignSummary returns, the page stores design and derived in state and displays system specifications, performance metrics, and available actions.
6. If getSystemDesignSummary fails or designId is missing/invalid, an error state is shown with no system actions available.

Save design as quote and view quote details:
1. On the System Summary page, the user sees a save-as-quote form with an optional nickname field.
2. The user optionally enters a nickname for the quote.
3. The user submits the form (e.g., clicks a "Save as quote" button).
4. The page calls saveSystemDesignAsQuote(designId, nickname) using the current designId and the user-entered nickname.
5. While the request is in progress, the form is disabled and a saving state is shown to prevent duplicate submissions.
6. On success, the response returns quotePlan and savedQuote.
7. The page immediately navigates to quote_detail.html?quotePlanId={quotePlan.id} using the quotePlan.id from the response.
8. If the save fails, an error message is shown, and the user can try again.

Edit the existing design:
1. After the system summary is loaded, the user can choose to edit the design.
2. The user clicks an "Edit design" action/button.
3. The page uses the loaded design.id from getSystemDesignSummary to build a URL to system_builder.html?designId={design.id}.
4. The browser navigates to system_builder.html with that designId so the System Builder can load and allow modification of the same SystemDesign.

Schedule a consultation from the summary:
1. After the system summary is loaded, the user can choose to schedule a consultation based on the design.
2. The user clicks a "Schedule consultation" action/button.
3. The page navigates to consultation_booking.html with a URL parameter indicating the source, e.g., consultation_booking.html?source=system_summary.
4. The Consultation Booking page can use the source parameter for context (e.g., to pre-fill or annotate the user’s path).

Download or print system summary:
1. After getSystemDesignSummary has loaded data, the user sees an option to download or print the summary.
2. The user clicks a "Download summary" or "Print summary" action.
3. The page generates a summary representation (e.g., print view or file content) using the current design and derived data held in page state.
4. The browser’s download or print flow is triggered using only the dynamic data (no hardcoded system values).
interactions: On page load: read designId from the URL and, if present, call getSystemDesignSummary(designId) to populate the page; show loading and handle error states.
If designId is missing from the URL: show a validation error state and do not call getSystemDesignSummary or render actions that depend on design data.

Click "Save as quote": validate that a design is loaded; read nickname input; call saveSystemDesignAsQuote(designId, nickname); disable the button while the call is in progress; on success, navigate to quote_detail.html?quotePlanId={quotePlan.id}; on error, re-enable and show error feedback.
Click "Edit design": read design.id from the loaded SystemDesign; navigate to system_builder.html?designId={design.id}.
Click "Schedule consultation": navigate to consultation_booking.html?source=system_summary (does not require design data).

Click "Download/Print summary": use the currently loaded design and derived data in page state to construct a summary output and trigger browser download or print without calling additional interfaces.

Error handling interactions: if getSystemDesignSummary or saveSystemDesignAsQuote fails, show an error message with an option for the user to retry the action (re-triggering the same interface call with the same parameters).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the designId URL parameter and the results of getSystemDesignSummary and saveSystemDesignAsQuote. On initial load, the page parses the designId from the query string (e.g., system_summary.html?designId={id}). If designId is not present or is an empty value, the page enters a 'no-designId' error state, avoids calling getSystemDesignSummary, and hides any components that require a loaded SystemDesign. If designId is present, the page enters a 'loading' state and calls getSystemDesignSummary(designId). While this call is pending, the specifications and actions are not interactive. Upon success, the page transitions to a 'loaded' state, storing the returned design (SystemDesign) and derived object in memory (page state). All components needing system details and derived metrics bind to this in-memory state and re-render automatically when it is set. If getSystemDesignSummary fails (e.g., due to invalid designId or server error), the page transitions to an 'error-loading-design' state, where error messaging is displayed and actions that depend on design are disabled. The designId in the URL remains the single source of truth for which SystemDesign is being summarized; changing the URL (e.g., via navigation) to a different designId should re-run the load sequence. When the user submits 'Save as quote', the page must be in the 'loaded' state with a valid design; it then enters a 'saving-quote' sub-state while saveSystemDesignAsQuote(designId, nickname) is in progress. During 'saving-quote', the save action is disabled to avoid duplicate quote creation. On successful save, the page immediately navigates to quote_detail.html, adding quotePlanId={quotePlan.id} to the URL for that destination page. The System Summary page does not persist any state in localStorage or other client storage; all state is derived from the URL (designId) and the current in-memory results of the assigned interfaces. Navigation to system_builder.html includes designId in the URL so the builder page can reconstruct the same design; navigation to consultation_booking.html includes only source=system_summary as a context parameter and does not alter the System Summary state.

components.functionality: Reads the designId from the current page URL query string on load and controls initial data fetching and high-level state transitions (no-designId, loading, loaded, error). If a valid designId is found, it calls getSystemDesignSummary(designId) and stores the returned design and derived data in page state.

Displays a concise header section for the System Summary page that confirms which system is being viewed. It uses information from the loaded SystemDesign (such as system_type and other high-level attributes) and can show basic identifiers or descriptors for the design. It also reflects loading and error states by hiding or showing header content appropriately.

Shows detailed technical specifications of the configured system, including system_type, roof_type, roof_pitch (if set), panel_model_id (resolved or displayed as an identifier), panel_wattage, panel_quantity, total_dc_capacity_kw (from either SystemDesign or derived.totalDcCapacityKw), include_battery flag, battery_option_id, battery_capacity_kwh, inverter_type, and any notes from the design. It is read-only and updates whenever the SystemDesign or derived object changes in state.

Displays computed performance and savings metrics for the system using the derived object from getSystemDesignSummary. This includes totalDcCapacityKw, estimatedAcOutputKw, estimatedAnnualProductionKwh, estimatedAnnualSavings, and estimatedSystemCost. It renders these values once the summary data is successfully loaded and hides or shows placeholder content during loading or error states.

Enables the user to save the current SystemDesign as a quote. It includes a nickname input field (optional) and a submit action. On submit, it reads the current designId from state or URL, passes designId and the user-entered nickname to saveSystemDesignAsQuote, manages a saving state, and handles navigation on success or error feedback on failure.

Provides a simple way for the user to return to the System Builder with the same design. When clicked, it reads design.id from the loaded SystemDesign and navigates to system_builder.html?designId={design.id}. It is disabled or hidden when the SystemDesign has not yet been loaded or if an error state is active.

Allows the user to proceed from the System Summary to booking a consultation. When clicked, it navigates the browser to consultation_booking.html?source=system_summary. It does not require system-specific data but may be disabled or styled differently during initial loading or error states for consistency.

Triggers generation of a downloadable or printable summary of the current system configuration and performance metrics using the data loaded via getSystemDesignSummary. It gathers information from SystemDesign and derived in page state and passes it to a client-side export/print routine so the user can save or print the summary. It is available only when design and derived data are successfully loaded.

Manages and displays global loading and error states based on whether designId is present and whether getSystemDesignSummary or saveSystemDesignAsQuote calls have succeeded or failed. It can show loading indicators during API calls and error messages when there is no designId, when the system design fails to load, or when saving as a quote fails. It also controls visibility or disabled state of other components depending on the current status.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same overall pattern as the mockup: the page sits inside the existing site chrome (top info bar + nav handled by the theme), followed by a full-width hero-style System Summary header, then a main two-column content area. The left column functions as the primary 'content/media' area for specifications and performance data, occupying roughly two-thirds of the content width. The right column is a sidebar card that replaces the 'Categories' card from the mockup with a quote-save form and key CTAs. Non-visual or transient components (URL initializer, loading/error) do not disrupt this structure and overlay or sit above the main content when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same overall pattern as the mockup: the page sits inside the existing site chrome (top info bar + nav handled by the theme), followed by a full-width hero-style System Summary header, then a main two-column content area. The left column functions as the primary 'content/media' area for specifications and performance data, occupying roughly two-thirds of the content width. The right column is a sidebar card that replaces the 'Categories' card from the mockup with a quote-save form and key CTAs. Non-visual or transient components (URL initializer, loading/error) do not disrupt this structure and overlay or sit above the main content when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The System Summary header (hero) occupies about 40–45% of the initial viewport height on desktop, with generous vertical padding and a full-width background image/overlay. Below it, the main content sits in a centered, max-width container (roughly 1120–1200px). Within this container, the left column spans about 65–70% of the width (e.g., 8 of 12 grid columns), while the sidebar spans 30–35% (4 of 12). Cards like the specification panel, performance panel, and quote-save form use 24–32px internal padding and are separated by 24px vertical gaps. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero → main content, main content → footer) use 48–64px spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious marketing layout: the hero header is visually open, with a large title, comfortable line spacing, and generous padding. The main content is denser but still airy: system specs and performance tables are organized into clearly separated blocks with sufficient whitespace and card padding so they feel structured, not cramped. The sidebar card is compact but not overloaded, with clear groupings (form fields, primary CTA button). Buttons have 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, and vertical spacing between stacked items is 16–24px.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve a Z-pattern flow: users start at the top left with the page title in the hero, sweep across to any high-level metric or summary text on the right side of the hero, then move diagonally down to the left column’s system specifications (the main content anchor), and finally across to the right sidebar card that contains the quote-save form and consultation CTA. Within the main column, the flow is top-to-bottom (specifications first, then performance and savings). The loading/error state, when active, temporarily becomes the primary focal element, either overlaying or occupying the top of the main content region.

layout.overall_description: The System Summary page sits within a corporate-style solar services site. Above, the theme’s info bar and navigation remain consistent, and directly underneath them a full-width hero section presents the System Summary header over a darkened solar panel image. The header’s content is vertically and horizontally centered, with a bold white title and one or two lines of supporting text, breathing in a generous band of space that fills about the upper half of the first screen. Below this hero, the main content is housed in a centered, max-width container. Inside it, a two-column layout emerges: on the left, a wider column holds two stacked data cards—the System Specification panel at the top and the Performance and Savings panel beneath it—each styled as elevated white cards with substantial padding and clear headings. On the right, a narrower sidebar column mirrors the mockup’s categories card: a single, structured quote-save card sits near the top, with the quote save form inside and a strong consultation CTA button immediately beneath it, all aligned to the right column width. Secondary actions like editing the design and downloading the summary are positioned near the bottom left of the main content area, visually subordinate but easily found. The page maintains a clean Z-pattern: hero → specs → sidebar actions, with consistent 8px-based spacing and card paddings that keep the layout professional, legible, and marketing-friendly. When loading or errors occur, a prominent status panel temporarily overlays or occupies the central main-content area, drawing immediate attention without permanently altering the page structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy any spatial footprint in the layout. It initializes state from URL parameters before or as the page renders. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the page logic layer, but in the visual layout it is invisible and does not create any gaps, padding, or alignment shifts in the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No rendered DOM footprint; it does not affect spacing or flow of any other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The System Summary header appears directly beneath the global navigation/header area as a full-width hero band. It spans the entire browser width and occupies roughly 40–45% of the viewport height on desktop. A solar-themed background image fills the area edge-to-edge, overlaid with a semi-transparent dark blue layer to ensure text contrast. Within this band, content is centered in a max-width container aligned with the main content below. The key headline (e.g., 'System Summary') is vertically and horizontally centered, set in large, bold white type, with one or two lines of supporting text or key metrics beneath it. Vertical padding inside this hero follows the 48–64px scale above and below the text block, creating an open, high-impact area similar in feel to the original mockup’s hero. On narrower viewports, the height reduces proportionally, but it remains visually dominant at the top of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section, full-width, fixed in the normal scroll flow (not sticky). Background image scales responsively with cover behavior to maintain edge-to-edge coverage.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The System Specification panel is the first card in the main content area beneath the hero. It sits inside a centered, max-width page container with approximately 48–64px of vertical spacing separating it from the bottom of the hero. This container uses a two-column grid: the panel occupies the left column, taking about 65–70% of the total content width (e.g., 8 of 12 grid columns), starting flush with the left edge of the grid. The panel itself is a white, slightly elevated card with 24–32px padding on all sides. Its heading aligns to the left, with system details organized in rows or grouped sections inside. There is 16–24px vertical spacing between the card’s title, any subheadings, and data rows. The panel’s bottom edge is separated from the Performance and Savings panel below it by a 24px vertical gap, maintaining a clear stacked-card hierarchy in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level card within the left column of a responsive grid. On tablet and mobile, it transitions to full width above the Performance and Savings panel, preserving similar internal padding and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Performance and Savings panel is positioned directly below the System Specification panel in the left column of the main content area. It aligns perfectly in width and left margin with the System Specification panel, occupying the same ~65–70% of the content width on desktop. A 24px vertical gap separates its top edge from the bottom of the specification card. This panel is also a white, card-style container with 24–32px internal padding and a left-aligned title at the top. Inside, performance charts, numbers, and savings estimates are arranged in a moderately dense layout, with 16–24px vertical spacing between sections (e.g., between an annual production row, savings row, and any chart or visualization). The card’s bottom edge sits above any left-column action row or page footer, with around 32–48px of space below to keep the bottom of the layout from feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level card stacked within the left column. On smaller screens, it appears directly beneath the System Specification panel in a single-column flow, retaining consistent padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-save form occupies the primary card in the right sidebar column, mirroring the position of the 'Categories' card in the mockup.
