In the main content container, it starts at the same vertical level as the System Specification panel’s top edge, but aligned to the right, taking about 30–35% of the content width (e.g., 4 of 12 grid columns). The card is visually distinct: a white or very light background, slight drop shadow or border, and 24–32px internal padding. At the top of the card, a bold title (e.g., 'Save this System as a Quote') is left-aligned, followed by form fields stacked vertically (name, email, label for quote, etc.), each with 16px vertical spacing between them. The form’s own primary submit button sits at the bottom of the card, spanning most of the card width with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. There is a 24px gap below this card before any standalone buttons like the schedule consultation button. The card’s height is tuned so it feels like a single, self-contained module on the right side, visually analogous to the single 'Categories' card in the original design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level card anchored in the right column. On desktop, the sidebar column (including this card) can become sticky within the viewport after scrolling past the hero, so the card and its CTAs remain visible while the user scrolls the left column. On tablet/mobile, this card stacks below the left-column panels as a full-width card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Edit Design button is placed in the main content area as a secondary action aligned with the left column, near the bottom of the data panels. On desktop, it appears beneath the Performance and Savings panel with a 24–32px vertical gap above it, aligned to the left edge of the left column content. It can sit either alone or in a horizontal row with the Download Summary button, with 16–24px horizontal spacing between them. The button uses a less dominant style (e.g., outlined or muted-colored) relative to primary CTAs, with 8–10px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding. This location means that after scanning specs and performance, users naturally encounter the option to go back and adjust the design. On mobile, it appears in the same sequence, below the panels and before the right-column card content if stacking is linear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline button within the normal flow of the left column. It does not stick or float; it participates in the usual responsive stacking behavior, appearing above or alongside the Download Summary button depending on viewport width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Schedule Consultation button is placed in the right sidebar column immediately beneath the quote-save form card, acting as the primary action in that column and echoing the 'Get A Free Quote' CTA prominence in the mockup. After a 24px vertical gap below the quote-save form card, this button spans most of the sidebar column width, aligned with the card edges. It uses a strong visual style (solid brand blue background, white text) and slightly larger padding (10–12px vertical, 20–24px horizontal) to differentiate it from secondary buttons. The spacing above and below gives it breathing room so it reads as a clear, focal CTA. On tablet/mobile, when columns stack, this button appears directly below the quote-save form card in the single-column flow, maintaining its strong visual weight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level button within the sidebar column. If the sidebar is implemented as sticky on desktop, this button also remains visible as part of that sticky sidebar; otherwise, it scrolls naturally with the quote-save form card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Download Summary button sits near the Edit Design button in the left column, forming a small action cluster at the bottom of the main data content. On desktop, it appears to the right of the Edit Design button in a horizontal row, with 16–24px horizontal spacing between the two, aligned to the left edge of the left column. Alternatively, if horizontal space is limited, it stacks directly below Edit Design with 12–16px vertical spacing. The button style is slightly more prominent than Edit Design but still secondary to the Schedule Consultation button—often a solid but less saturated color or a smaller, medium-weight style. It uses consistent padding (8–10px vertical, 16–20px horizontal) following the standard button sizing. On mobile, it appears beneath the Edit Design button in the vertical stack, preserving clear tap targets and maintaining a modest gap from the content above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline button within the left column’s normal flow. It is not sticky and follows the same responsive wrapping or stacking behavior as the Edit Design button.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error-state panel appears within the same main content region that houses the two-column layout but temporarily takes visual precedence. On initial load or when reloading data, a loading variant of this panel appears centered within the main content area just below the hero: it spans roughly 50–70% of the content width (centered horizontally), with 24–32px padding and ample whitespace around it (at least 48px top and bottom). In a loading state, it may dim or blur the underlying main content panels or sit in their place before they render. In an error state, the panel becomes a strong, high-contrast card at the top of the main content area (still beneath the hero), stretching across most or all of the content width, with clear error messaging and any retry action buttons inside. When visible, this panel either overlays the main panels (specification, performance, quote form) with a semi-transparent backdrop or pushes them down in the flow so the message appears first, ensuring the user’s attention is immediately captured.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered overlay or top-of-content card. In overlay mode, it sits above other components with the rest of the page dimmed; in inline mode, it appears as the first card in the main content area, pushing other components down but maintaining the same horizontal alignment and padding as the main container.

name: Financing Overview & Comparison
title: Financing Overview and Comparison
description: This page explains available solar financing types and lets users generate, filter, sort, and explore financing offers for a specific project cost, with the ability to start and save a financing pre-application.

core_features: Load and display explanatory content and summaries for different financing types using getFinancingOverviewContent.
Capture project information (project cost, ZIP code, credit score range) and start a financing comparison using startFinancingComparison.
Resume an existing financing comparison when a comparisonId URL parameter is provided, using getFinancingComparisonResults.
Filter comparison results by maximum upfront payment and maximum monthly payment using getFinancingComparisonResults.
Sort comparison results by APR (and direction) using getFinancingComparisonResults.
Show detailed information about a selected financing option using getFinancingOptionDetail.
Provide an inline pre-application form for the selected financing option and save it as a draft using createFinancingPreApplication.
Allow navigation to the consultation booking page with a source URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Learn about financing types
1. User navigates to financing_overview.html without needing to enter any data.
2. On page load, the page calls getFinancingOverviewContent.
3. The page displays returned sections and financingTypeSummaries, grouped by financing type.
4. User reads explanations of loans, leases, PPAs, and key terms. No comparison is started until the user interacts with the project information form.

Workflow 2: Start a new financing comparison
1. User lands on financing_overview.html.
2. If projectCost, zip, and creditScoreRange URL parameters are present and valid, the project information form is prefilled. If all required inputs are present and valid, the page may automatically start a comparison; otherwise the user edits or completes the fields.
3. User submits the project information form.
4. The form handler validates required inputs and calls startFinancingComparison with projectCost, zipCode, and creditScoreRange from the form.
5. The API returns a FinancingComparison and an array of FinancingQuoteResult items.
6. The page updates the URL to include comparisonId from the returned comparison, and may also include projectCost, zip, and creditScoreRange if not already present.
7. The comparison results list is populated from the returned results, and the filter/sort controls become active and bound to this comparison.

Workflow 3: Resume an existing comparison and refine filters
1. User returns to financing_overview.html with a comparisonId URL parameter (for example via bookmark or internal link).
2. On page load, the page reads comparisonId (and any filter or sort URL parameters if present).
3. The page calls getFinancingComparisonResults with comparisonId and any maxUpfrontPayment, maxMonthlyPayment, sortBy, and sortDirection values obtained from the URL (or defaults when not present).
4. The API returns the existing FinancingComparison and its FinancingQuoteResult items, already filtered/sorted if applicable.
5. The project information form is populated from the FinancingComparison data (project_cost, zip_code, credit_score_range), allowing the user to see and adjust the underlying project inputs if needed.
6. User adjusts filters for maximum upfront payment or maximum monthly payment and/or changes sorting (e.g., APR low to high) in the filter/sort component.
7. On each change, the page updates the URL filter/sort parameters and calls getFinancingComparisonResults again with the new parameters.
8. The results list updates to reflect the refined filters and sort order.

Workflow 4: View financing option details and start a pre-application
1. After a comparison is loaded (via Workflow 2 or 3), the user views the list of FinancingQuoteResult items.
2. User clicks on a specific result row or a dedicated "View details" control.
3. The click handler obtains the financing_option_id from the selected FinancingQuoteResult and calls getFinancingOptionDetail.
4. The API returns the corresponding FinancingOption with full details.
5. The page opens a detail panel or modal bound to this FinancingOption and the originating FinancingQuoteResult (for context such as project cost, term, APR, estimated payments).
6. Within the detail panel, an inline pre-application form is displayed. Default values (when appropriate) are derived from the FinancingQuoteResult and/or FinancingComparison (for example, loan amount may be initialized from project_cost and term from term_years).
7. User edits loan amount and term years as desired and submits the pre-application form.
8. The form handler calls createFinancingPreApplication with financingOptionId, loanAmount, termYears, and contextual values from the current FinancingComparison (project_cost, zip_code, credit_score_range).
9. The API returns the created FinancingPreApplication in draft status.
10. The page shows a confirmation state in the detail panel indicating that the draft has been saved and can be completed later.

Workflow 5: Navigate to schedule a consultation
1. At any time, the user can click a call-to-action such as "Talk to an expert" or "Schedule consultation" on the financing page.
2. The click handler navigates the browser to consultation_booking.html with a source URL parameter set to financing_overview.
3. The consultation booking page can then use the source parameter to tailor its experience.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameters (projectCost, zip, creditScoreRange, comparisonId, and any filter/sort parameters) and determine whether to call getFinancingOverviewContent only or also load/resume a comparison.
On page load, always call getFinancingOverviewContent and bind returned sections and financingTypeSummaries to the overview components.
On project information form change, update local form state but do not call APIs until the form is submitted.
On project information form submit, validate inputs and call startFinancingComparison, then update URL with comparisonId and re-render the comparison-related components with returned data.
On filter control changes (max upfront payment, max monthly payment), update the corresponding URL parameters and call getFinancingComparisonResults with comparisonId and new filter values.
On sort control changes (sort by APR, sort direction), update URL parameters and call getFinancingComparisonResults with comparisonId and new sort values.
On click of a financing result row or detail trigger, call getFinancingOptionDetail with financingOptionId from the selected FinancingQuoteResult and open the option detail component.
On pre-application form submit, validate required fields and call createFinancingPreApplication, then display confirmation or error states based on the response.
On click of the consultation call-to-action, navigate to consultation_booking.html with the source=financing_overview URL parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is managed entirely via URL parameters and API responses. On initial load, the page reads projectCost, zip, creditScoreRange, and comparisonId from the query string of financing_overview.html. If comparisonId is present, the page prioritizes resuming that comparison by calling getFinancingComparisonResults with comparisonId and any filter or sort parameters found in the URL (for example, maxUpfrontPayment, maxMonthlyPayment, sortBy, sortDirection). The returned FinancingComparison becomes the canonical source for project_cost, zip_code, and credit_score_range; the project information form is populated from this object. If comparisonId is not present, the page uses projectCost, zip, and creditScoreRange URL parameters (if available) only to prefill the project information form. When the user submits the form and startFinancingComparison returns a new FinancingComparison, the page updates the URL to include comparisonId and, optionally, reflects current projectCost, zip, and creditScoreRange in the query string. Any change to filter or sort controls updates corresponding URL parameters and triggers a new call to getFinancingComparisonResults using the current comparisonId, ensuring that the URL always represents the current filter/sort state and can be bookmarked or shared to restore the same view. Selection of a specific financing option for details does not need to be reflected in the URL, but can be if desired via a selectedOptionId parameter; if implemented, the page should read this parameter on load and automatically open the corresponding detail view after results are loaded. No state is stored in localStorage; all persisted state is derived from URL parameters and the underlying FinancingComparison, FinancingQuoteResult, FinancingOption, and FinancingPreApplication data returned by the APIs.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches explanatory content and financing type summaries and provides them to overview display components.
Displays informational sections and key-term explanations for financing using the sections data returned by getFinancingOverviewContent.
Shows a concise list of financing types (loan, lease, PPA) with titles and summaries to help users quickly understand the differences.

Evaluates URL parameters to decide whether to resume an existing financing comparison or await user input to start a new one. If comparisonId is present, calls getFinancingComparisonResults; otherwise, only pre-populates the project information form with any provided projectCost, zip, and creditScoreRange parameters.

Captures project cost, ZIP code, and credit score range. It can be prefilled from URL parameters or from an existing FinancingComparison and, when submitted, creates a new financing comparison by calling startFinancingComparison.
Handles submission of the project-info-form by calling startFinancingComparison with the current form values and then updating the URL (including comparisonId) and comparison-related components.

Provides inputs for maximum upfront payment, maximum monthly payment, and sort options (such as APR and direction). When values change, updates corresponding URL parameters and refreshes results via getFinancingComparisonResults.

Given a comparisonId and current filters/sort options (from either URL parameters or the filter-sort-controls component), calls getFinancingComparisonResults and exposes the latest FinancingComparison and FinancingQuoteResult list to the results display.

Displays the list of financing offers (FinancingQuoteResult items) for the active comparison, including key values such as estimated upfront payment, estimated monthly payment, APR, and term. Each row exposes controls to view full option details and start a pre-application.

When a user selects a result, retrieves and displays the underlying FinancingOption details using getFinancingOptionDetail. Shows full product information and contextual data from the associated FinancingQuoteResult and FinancingComparison.

Embedded within the financing-option-detail-panel, this form lets the user specify loan amount and term years for the selected financing option and submit a draft pre-application using createFinancingPreApplication.
Receives validated data from the pre-application-form, calls createFinancingPreApplication, and returns the created FinancingPreApplication to the UI for confirmation and any follow-up messaging.
Provides a user-triggered navigation path from the financing page to the consultation booking page with a fixed source URL parameter.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width top info bar, followed by a full-width white navigation bar, then a full-width hero section with a centered page headline ('Financing Overview & Comparison'). Below the hero sits the main content area in a two-column layout: a dominant left column (~65–70% width) for the financing workflow (overview, project info, comparison tools, results, and detail/pre-application), and a right column (~30–35% width) for a single, tall sidebar card featuring a consultation CTA and supporting guidance. Components are stacked vertically within their columns in a clear, logical order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width top info bar, followed by a full-width white navigation bar, then a full-width hero section with a centered page headline ('Financing Overview & Comparison'). Below the hero sits the main content area in a two-column layout: a dominant left column (~65–70% width) for the financing workflow (overview, project info, comparison tools, results, and detail/pre-application), and a right column (~30–35% width) for a single, tall sidebar card featuring a consultation CTA and supporting guidance. Components are stacked vertically within their columns in a clear, logical order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top info bar occupies about 8–10% of the viewport height, the navigation bar around 10–12%, and the hero roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height with a large, centered headline over a darkened image. The main content section fills the remaining vertical space and scrolls. Within the main section, cards (forms, lists, overview blocks) use 24–32px internal padding and are separated vertically by 32–48px. The left column is given most of the horizontal space for interaction-heavy components, while the right sidebar is a single vertically elongated card that is visually lighter and slightly narrower, echoing the 'categories' card in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero is open and spacious with prominent typography and generous padding. The main content is moderately dense: form fields, filter/sort controls, and result rows are grouped into clearly separated card-like sections with 16–24px vertical spacing between internal elements, avoiding clutter. Loaders occupy the same footprints as their respective content sections to maintain layout stability. The sidebar card is concise, with fewer elements, emphasizing clarity and a strong CTA rather than dense information.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow maintains a Z-pattern: the eye starts at the top left (logo/info bar), moves right across to the top CTA in the navigation, then diagonally down to the centered hero headline. From there, attention drops down to the top of the left column (financing overview and project info form), flows through the comparison workflow (filters and results), and then shifts right to the sidebar consultation card. Within the left column, the flow is vertical: overview → summaries → project info form + compare CTA → comparison state/loader → filter/sort bar → results list → detail panel → pre-application. The sidebar CTA and its elevated card act as a persistent secondary focal point on the right, similar to the categories card in the mockup.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing site-wide top info bar and white navigation bar, a full-width hero section stretches across the screen with a high-contrast solar-themed background image and a semi-transparent dark overlay. Centered both horizontally and vertically in this hero is a large, bold white headline announcing 'Financing Overview & Comparison,' with a short explanatory subline beneath it; the generous vertical padding and open space ensure the headline dominates the first view. Scrolling down, the hero gives way to a clean white main content area contained within a centered, fixed-width layout. Here, a two-column structure appears: on the left, a wide content column carries the entire financing journey from education to comparison and pre-application, stacked in clear card-like sections with consistent padding and generous spacing. On the right, a single tall sidebar card—visually similar to a 'categories' card—floats slightly above the background with subtle shadow, featuring a strong 'Schedule a Free Financing Consultation' link near the top and concise supporting copy. As users move down the page, they naturally follow the left column through an introductory financing overview, a project info form, filters, and a scrollable list of financing offers, ending with an expandable detail panel housing offer details and a pre-application form. The right sidebar remains visually stable and, on larger screens, gently sticky, providing a constant, reassuring consultation CTA. The overall effect is professional and structured, balancing rich interactive content with clear hierarchy and comfortable whitespace.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing overview loader sits at the top of the left main content column, directly below the hero section’s bottom edge and within the central page container. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the page content width), presented as a shallow rectangular placeholder card with rounded corners, 24–32px internal padding, and a light gray background or skeleton shimmer. Its height roughly matches what the eventual overview content will occupy (about 1/4 of the viewport height or enough for a heading and 2–3 paragraphs), ensuring the layout does not shift abruptly when content appears. There is 48–64px of vertical whitespace above it (between the hero and the start of the main content) and 24–32px below it separating it from the next block (financing-overview-content or the financing-type-summary-list). It aligns flush with the left edge of the main grid, leaving a comfortable gutter to the right sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the left column; it occupies the same footprint as the financing-overview-content. When loading completes, it fades out and is replaced in-place by financing-overview-content without causing any vertical reflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing overview content occupies the topmost functional block of the left main column, directly under the hero. It is contained within a white, card-like section that spans 100% of the left column width (about 65–70% of the content width), with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. Inside, a medium-large section heading sits at the top, followed by 1–3 short paragraphs explaining the financing options and how the comparison works; these internal elements are stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing. The card’s top aligns visually with the hero’s bottom, with a 48–64px gap between sections. Below this overview block, there is approximately 24–32px of vertical spacing before the financing-type-summary-list appears. The right edge of this card aligns with the inner edge of the central grid, leaving a consistent vertical gutter between it and the sidebar card to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column. On narrower viewports (tablet and mobile), it becomes a full-width block above all other left-column content, stacking naturally above the project-info-form and results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing-type-summary-list is placed immediately below the financing-overview-content in the left main column, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the left column and presents 3–6 key financing types (e.g., cash, loan, lease, PPA) in a horizontally or vertically arranged set of compact cards. On desktop, the cards align in one or two rows within this block, each card sharing a uniform width and height, with 16–24px of internal padding and 16px horizontal/vertical gaps between cards. The entire list block reads as a single section visually anchored beneath the overview, about 1/5 of the viewport height tall depending on the number of items. There is a clear 32–40px vertical gap between this list and the project-info-form that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, reflowing responsively: on smaller screens, the summary cards stack vertically, still within a single block, maintaining 16–24px vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-state-resolver occupies a discrete, slim status area in the left column, positioned between the project-info-form and the comparison-filter-sort-controls. It appears as a small horizontal strip or inline message spanning the full width of the left column, with 16–24px vertical padding and left-aligned text or iconography indicating the current comparison state (e.g., ready, loading, no offers). Vertically, it sits about 16–24px below the bottom edge of the project-info-form and 16–24px above the filter/sort controls. When performing background checks or transitions, it may show a subtle spinner and status text without visually overpowering adjacent sections, occupying roughly the height of one to two lines of text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element that appears and disappears in place, without overlaying other components. It expands or collapses vertically based on content, but the height remains modest to preserve the main form and results as primary focal points.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-info-form is a central interactive card in the left column, placed below the financing-type-summary-list with approximately 32–48px of vertical spacing. It spans the full width of the left column and resembles a clean, elevated form card with a white background, subtle shadow, and 24–32px internal padding on all sides. At the top of the card sits a section title (e.g., 'Enter Your Project Details'), followed by form fields arranged in one or two columns depending on viewport width, with 16–24px vertical spacing between rows and 16px horizontal spacing between columns. Typical fields might include project cost, system size, location, and credit profile. The bottom of the card features the primary 'Compare Financing Options' button aligned to the right (or centered on mobile), with about 16–24px padding above it so it breathes. This button visually stands out with a strong blue background. The entire card is approximately 1/3 of the viewport height on desktop, substantial enough to feel important but not overwhelming, and there is a 24–32px gap below it before any status elements appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column. The form’s button is the key driver into the comparison flow. On smaller screens, any multi-column fields collapse into a single column, extending the card vertically while preserving internal spacing. The card stays in the natural scroll flow without sticking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-starter is visually represented as the main CTA button within the project-info-form’s bottom area. It occupies the bottom-right corner of the form card, spanning roughly 25–35% of the card’s width on desktop (sized by its label and padding rather than full-width), with 10–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding, following the base spacing system. The button is clearly distinguished through a bold blue fill and white text, creating a strong visual anchor at the end of the form. It sits about 16px above the bottom of the card and about 16–24px below the last form field, providing breathing room while making it unmistakably the next step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, anchored inside the project-info-form. When activated, it triggers loading states downstream (comparison-state-resolver and comparison-results-loader) without moving from its position. On mobile, the button stretches closer to full width within the form for ease of tapping, but remains within the form’s confines.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-filter-sort-controls component sits in the left column directly above the financing results, approximately 16–24px below the comparison-state-resolver area. It spans the full width of the left column and appears as a low-profile bar or shallow card with 16–24px internal vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. Within this bar, filter dropdowns, toggle chips, and sort selectors are arranged horizontally from left to right on desktop, with around 16px spacing between controls, and they wrap onto a second row if needed. The bar visually anchors the top of the results area and is roughly 60–80px tall depending on content. There is a 16–24px gap between the bottom edge of this control bar and the top of the comparison-results-loader or financing-results-list that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the filter bar becomes sticky within the left column: once the user scrolls such that it reaches the top of the viewport, it remains fixed at the top of the content area while the results list scrolls beneath it, preserving continuous access to filtering. On smaller screens, it scrolls naturally with the content to avoid covering too much vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-results-loader occupies the same rectangular region as the financing-results-list in the left column. It sits directly below the comparison-filter-sort-controls, separated by about 16–24px, and spans the full width of the left column. Visually, it appears as a tall block with several horizontal skeleton rows or a centered spinner over faint placeholder lines, mimicking the height and general structure of the final results list — likely around 40–60% of the viewport height depending on the number of placeholder rows displayed. The loader has 24–32px padding on the left and right to match the card-like feel of the list area, and 24px padding top and bottom to keep the placeholder from feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the scrollable left column, occupying the results area while offers are being fetched or recalculated. It disappears and is replaced by the financing-results-list without changing the surrounding layout or the sticky behavior of the filter bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing-results-list is the primary content block below the filter/sort bar in the left column. It fills the entire width of the left column and extends vertically as far as needed to display available financing offers, typically occupying anywhere from half to more than a full viewport height, requiring scrolling. Results are presented as vertically stacked cards or row-style entries, each with 16–24px vertical padding and clear separation (16–24px gaps) between rows. The list area itself has 24–32px horizontal padding to align visually with the other card-based sections and a subtle top border or separation just below the filter bar. The first result sits about 16–24px below the filters. Each row includes key terms (rate, term length, monthly payment) aligned in a grid-like layout and a 'View Details' or 'Select' affordance aligned to the right, maintaining a tidy, corporate table-like feel. There is a 32–40px vertical gap below the last row and the start of the financing-option-detail-panel area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, forming the main scrolling content under the sticky filter bar. On narrower screens, columns within each row stack vertically while preserving consistent spacing, making each result row taller but still clearly separated.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing-option-detail-panel is positioned directly beneath the financing-results-list in the left column, separated by approximately 32–40px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the left column and appears as a prominent elevated card with a white background, subtle shadow, and 24–32px padding on all sides. When no offer is selected, the panel is either collapsed to a slim informational bar (e.g., 'Select an offer to see details') around 56–72px tall or entirely hidden. When an offer is selected, the panel expands to approximately 35–45% of the viewport height, revealing structured content: a header row showing the offer name and key terms at the top, followed by sections for payment breakdown, fees, and eligibility, each separated by subtle dividers and 16–24px internal spacing. At the bottom of this panel, a visually distinct sub-section introduces the path into the pre-application form (e.g., 'Start Pre-Application'), keeping the flow contextual. This panel creates a strong visual anchor at the bottom of the main results area without overshadowing the results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column but collapsible/expandable in height based on selection. On long pages, it scrolls naturally; when fully expanded, its top may scroll out of view as the user interacts with the pre-application content inside. On mobile, it becomes a full-width section below the list, stacking content vertically while preserving comfortable padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pre-application-form lives inside the lower portion of the financing-option-detail-panel, visually nested yet clearly delineated. Within the expanded detail panel, it begins roughly 24–32px below the offer’s main details, separated by a subtle divider line or a distinct subheading like 'Pre-Application'. The form itself is arranged as a compact card section with 24px internal padding, using single-column fields stacked vertically for clarity, each separated by 16–24px. It typically occupies about the bottom half of the expanded detail panel, though it can grow taller as necessary on more complex forms. The bottom of the pre-application-form includes primary and secondary actions (e.g., 'Submit Pre-Application', 'Save & Finish Later') aligned to the right (or centered on mobile) with 16–24px spacing above and around them, maintaining the page’s structured corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Nested inside the financing-option-detail-panel, the form expands the height of the panel as needed. It scrolls together with the panel content; there is no independent scrolling region within the form to avoid nested scrollbars. On mobile, the form stretches to full-width within the panel and may occupy an entire screen or more, but remains part of the normal scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pre-application-handler is represented visually by the main action controls at the bottom of the pre-application-form—typically the 'Submit' and optionally 'Save & Continue Later' buttons. These buttons sit in the bottom-right area of the form’s content block, spanning only as wide as their labels require, with 10–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding each. They are separated by about 12–16px of horizontal space. The primary action button uses the brand’s strong blue color, mirroring the comparison-starter, while the secondary action uses an outlined or lighter style. This button cluster occupies a horizontal strip around 56–72px tall within the form’s lower edge, ensuring sufficient touch target size and visual clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the pre-application-form, anchored to its bottom. When triggered, it may cause transient inline status messages above the buttons (e.g., 'Saving...') but does not change the overall layout. On mobile, the primary button may span more of the width for ease of tapping while still sitting at the bottom of the form section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation-cta-link is the focal element of the right-hand sidebar and is placed near the top of a tall, card-style 'Consultation & Help' panel that mirrors the 'Categories' card pattern from the mockup. This sidebar card begins at roughly the same vertical level as the financing-overview-content in the left column, with a small 48–64px gap below the hero. The card spans the full width of the right column (about 30–35% of the page content width) with 24–32px internal padding and a subtle elevation. Inside the card, a short title and 1–2 lines of reassuring copy appear above the consultation-cta-link, spaced with about 16–24px between them. The consultation-cta-link itself is styled as a prominent button-like link centered horizontally within the card, occupying 60–80% of the card’s width, with 10–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding and strong blue coloring. Below the button, supporting information such as a phone number or small bullet list can occupy the remaining vertical space, giving this sidebar card a height of roughly 40–60% of the viewport. The card’s right and left edges align with the main grid, maintaining a consistent gutter between it and the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sidebar card containing the consultation-cta-link is gently sticky on larger viewports: once scrolled into view, it remains visible along the right side as the user navigates through the form and results in the left column, up to the end of the main content section. On tablet and mobile, when the layout collapses to a single column, this card (and thus the consultation-cta-link) moves below the primary financing content and scrolls normally as a full-width block.

name: About Us
title: About Us
description: Displays dynamically loaded information about the solar company’s background, mission, credentials, and service areas, and provides clear paths to schedule a consultation or get a quote.

core_features: Fetch and display About Us content (company name, mission, history, credentials, service areas, and primary actions) using the getAboutUsContent interface.
Provide structured sections for mission, company history, credentials, and service areas that are driven entirely by the data returned from getAboutUsContent.
Render a dynamic list of primary actions (such as scheduling a consultation or getting a quote) based on the primaryActions array from getAboutUsContent.
Enable users to navigate directly to specific sections of the About Us content via URL parameters (for example, a section selector).
Route users to the consultation booking page or quote page with appropriate URL parameters when they click corresponding primary actions.
user_workflows: View company background and mission: 1) User opens about.html (via footer link or direct URL). 2) The page calls getAboutUsContent with no parameters. 3) Once data is returned, the page displays the companyName and high-level context (e.g., label or heading), followed by missionHtml, historyHtml, credentials, and serviceAreas rendered into their respective sections. 4) The section-navigation component reads any existing section URL parameter; if present, it highlights the corresponding section and scrolls it into view.

Explore credentials and service areas: 1) User clicks a navigation control (e.g., a tab or in-page link) for the credentials or service areas section. 2) The section-navigation component updates the URL to about.html?section=<selectedSectionKey> using pushState or by changing window.location.search. 3) The section-navigation component updates its internal state based on the new section value and scrolls/focuses the corresponding content section (credentials-list or service-areas-list). 4) The user can repeat this to move between mission, history, credentials, and service areas sections without reloading data.

Schedule a consultation from About Us: 1) User scrolls to or views the primary actions area populated from primaryActions in getAboutUsContent. 2) User clicks the action item whose actionKey is schedule_consultation (label displayed from the API data). 3) The primary-actions-list component intercepts the click and navigates the browser to consultation_booking.html?source=about. 4) The user continues the consultation booking process on the consultation_booking.html page.

Get a quote from About Us: 1) User scrolls to the primary actions area. 2) User clicks the action item whose actionKey is get_quote (label displayed from the API data). 3) The primary-actions-list component navigates the browser to get_quote.html (URL may also include any additional query parameters defined globally by the application, but not set by this page). 4) The user completes the quote request process on get_quote.html.
interactions: On initial page load, the about-data-loader component automatically invokes getAboutUsContent and, upon success, dispatches the resulting data to child components that render mission, history, credentials, service areas, and primary actions.

Users can click in-page navigation elements (for example, links representing 'Mission', 'History', 'Credentials', or 'Service Areas') that trigger the section-navigation component to update the section URL parameter and focus the corresponding section.

Users can click on any item in the primary-actions-list; the component interprets the actionKey and performs navigation. For actionKey 'schedule_consultation', it navigates to consultation_booking.html with the URL parameter source=about. For actionKey 'get_quote', it navigates to get_quote.html.

If the page is loaded with a section parameter in the URL, the section-navigation component reads it on initialization and immediately sets the active section state and scrolls that section into view.

If getAboutUsContent fails to load, the about-data-loader component exposes an error state that can be handled by showing a generic error message and an option to retry the data fetch (retrying the same interface call).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is primarily controlled via URL query parameters and the data returned by getAboutUsContent. The key URL parameter is 'section', used to indicate which About Us subsection should be considered active on load or after navigation:
- section=<sectionKey>: A string that the client uses to determine which subsection of the page to emphasize or scroll into view. Typical internal values might include 'mission', 'history', 'credentials', or 'service-areas', but the exact mapping is implemented in the client and does not require any static content from the API. If this parameter is absent, the client can default to a predefined internal section (for example, an overview or mission section).

State transitions:
- Initial Load: When about.html loads, the about-data-loader component calls getAboutUsContent. In parallel, the section-navigation component parses window.location.search to read the 'section' parameter and sets its activeSection state accordingly. Once the content is loaded, the page renders all sections, and the section-navigation component scrolls the active section into view.
- Section Change: When the user selects a different section via in-page navigation, the section-navigation component updates the URL query string with the new 'section' value (e.g., about.html?section=credentials) using pushState or by replacing the current location, without reloading the page or re-calling getAboutUsContent. The activeSection state is updated, and the component scrolls to the corresponding section.
- Navigation to Other Pages: When the user triggers a primary action, the primary-actions-list component does not modify any internal state on this page; instead, it performs a full navigation to the target page. For 'schedule_consultation', it navigates to consultation_booking.html?source=about, adding the 'source' query parameter as required. For 'get_quote', it navigates to get_quote.html (without additional parameters from this page). Any state or context passing beyond these URL parameters must be handled on the target pages.

All content displayed on this page (such as company name, mission, history, credentials list, service areas list, and action labels) is bound directly to the data returned from getAboutUsContent. No static content or hardcoded company information is used, ensuring that the page always reflects the dynamic API data.

components.functionality: Initializes the About Us page by calling getAboutUsContent and exposing the loaded data, loading state, and error state to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when about.html is loaded; invokes getAboutUsContent with no parameters. On success, stores the returned object in a shared state (e.g., a context or top-level state) accessed by child components. On failure, sets an error flag and error message for display.
onRetry: Triggered when a user activates a retry control (e.g., a button) in an error state; re-calls getAboutUsContent and updates state based on the new result.

components.functionality: Manages in-page navigation between About Us sections (mission, history, credentials, service areas) and synchronizes the active section with the 'section' URL query parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads the 'section' query parameter from the current URL. Sets an internal activeSection state based on this value or a default if none is provided. After about-data-loader has completed loading and rendering of sections, scrolls or focuses the corresponding section.

onSectionClick: Triggered when a user clicks an in-page navigation element associated with a section key (for example, 'mission', 'history', 'credentials', or 'service-areas'). Updates the activeSection state and uses history.pushState or an equivalent method to update the URL to about.html?section=<sectionKey> without reloading the page. Scrolls/focuses the chosen section.

onUrlChange: If the application listens to popstate events (e.g., back/forward browser buttons), this handler updates the activeSection state whenever the URL's 'section' parameter changes externally and scrolls the corresponding section into view.

components.functionality: Displays the companyName and a concise textual introduction of the About Us content, using only the values provided by getAboutUsContent.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When about-data-loader updates the getAboutUsContent data, this component re-renders the companyName and any derived or contextual text using that value.

components.functionality: Renders the company's mission content in its own identifiable section, using missionHtml from getAboutUsContent.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When missionHtml is updated by about-data-loader, the component re-renders the HTML safely (e.g., via a sanitized HTML renderer).
onSectionActivated: Triggered by section-navigation when activeSection is set to the key representing the mission section; scrolls or focuses this section into view.

components.functionality: Renders the company's history content within the About Us page using historyHtml from getAboutUsContent.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When historyHtml is updated, the component re-renders the HTML content.
onSectionActivated: Triggered by section-navigation when activeSection is set to the key representing the history section; scrolls or focuses this section into view.

components.functionality: Displays a dynamically generated list of the company’s credentials (such as certifications, partnerships, memberships) based on the credentials array from getAboutUsContent.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When getAboutUsContent.credentials changes, the component rebuilds the list of credentials items, rendering each string as a separate list entry.
onSectionActivated: Triggered by section-navigation when activeSection is set to the key representing the credentials section; scrolls or focuses this section into view.

components.functionality: Displays all serviceAreas returned from getAboutUsContent as a structured list, enabling users to confirm whether their region is served.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When getAboutUsContent.serviceAreas changes, the component re-renders the list of service areas, ensuring that all items from the array are displayed.
onSectionActivated: Triggered by section-navigation when activeSection is set to the key representing the service areas section; scrolls or focuses this section into view.

components.functionality: Renders the list of primary actions provided in getAboutUsContent.primaryActions and handles navigation to other pages based on each action’s actionKey.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When getAboutUsContent.primaryActions changes, the component rebuilds the list of actions, assigning each action a click handler that uses the actionKey and label defined by the API.

onActionClick: Triggered when a user clicks a primary action item. The handler reads the actionKey and performs the corresponding navigation logic without hardcoding display labels:
- If actionKey is 'schedule_consultation', navigate to 'consultation_booking.html?source=about'.
- If actionKey is 'get_quote', navigate to 'get_quote.html'.
- For any other actionKey values that may be introduced in future API responses, the component can be structured to either ignore them, handle them via a configurable mapping, or fall back to a default behavior as defined elsewhere in the application.

components.functionality: Displays an error message and retry control when getAboutUsContent fails to load.
event_handlers: onErrorStateChange: When the about-data-loader component sets or clears an error state, this component shows or hides an error message accordingly.
onRetryClick: Triggered when the user activates a retry control; calls the about-data-loader.onRetry handler to attempt to fetch getAboutUsContent again.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same vertical hierarchy as the mockup: a global top info bar, a full-width navigation bar, then a full-width hero (for the About page intro). All About-specific components sit below the hero inside a two-column content area, with the main narrative content stacked in the wider left column and a single structured sidebar card in the narrower right column. The left column hosts the company story flow in this order: company-header-summary at the top, then mission-section, history-section, credentials-list, service-areas-list, and finally a strong primary-actions band near the bottom. The right column is primarily reserved for section-navigation, styled as a sidebar card that mirrors the original 'Categories' card behavior. System-level components (about-data-loader and about-error-notice) overlay or appear above the two-column content without disrupting the underlying structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same vertical hierarchy as the mockup: a global top info bar, a full-width navigation bar, then a full-width hero (for the About page intro). All About-specific components sit below the hero inside a two-column content area, with the main narrative content stacked in the wider left column and a single structured sidebar card in the narrower right column. The left column hosts the company story flow in this order: company-header-summary at the top, then mission-section, history-section, credentials-list, service-areas-list, and finally a strong primary-actions band near the bottom. The right column is primarily reserved for section-navigation, styled as a sidebar card that mirrors the original 'Categories' card behavior. System-level components (about-data-loader and about-error-notice) overlay or appear above the two-column content without disrupting the underlying structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top info bar and navigation bar remain relatively shallow (each around 8–12% of viewport height) to keep focus on the hero. The hero for the About page occupies roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height with a large centered headline and brief supporting text. Below the hero, the About content area sits in a fixed-width central container. Within this, the left main column uses about 65–70% of the content width (e.g., 8/12 grid columns) and the right sidebar uses 30–35% (e.g., 4/12). Vertical spacing between the hero and the start of the About content follows the 48–64px section margin; vertical spacing between each stacked section in the left column is 24–32px. The sidebar card has consistent 24–32px internal padding and a margin of 24px from the top of the content row. The full-width primary-actions-list near the bottom uses a slightly taller band with generous padding (32–48px) to visually stand out.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero is spacious and typography-led, with ample whitespace around the headline and subcopy. The two-column About content below is moderately dense: the left column includes multiple sections but each is separated by 24–32px gaps, clear headings, and comfortable line lengths. The sidebar card is visually lighter with adequate padding and a concise list of navigation links, echoing the 'Categories' card density. Lists such as credentials and service areas are slightly denser but still use 16–24px vertical spacing between items and generous left padding to avoid a cluttered feel. The primary-actions-list is more open, with large tappable CTAs and extra padding to reduce cognitive load around key actions. The loader and error notice appear as focused, minimal overlays or bars without introducing extra visual noise.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the Z-pattern: the user first scans the top left info/logo, moves to the right-side contact/CTA in the global header, then drops diagonally down to the centered hero headline. From the hero, the eye is drawn down-left into the main About content, starting with the company-header-summary. As the user scrolls, the large left column content (mission, history, credentials, service areas) forms the main reading path. Intermittently, the eye is gently pulled to the right sidebar where the section-navigation card offers quick jumps to specific sections. Near the end of the content, attention is intentionally redirected to the full-width primary-actions-list band, which cuts across both columns and provides a strong final call-to-action. Error and loading states briefly interrupt or overlay this flow at the top of the content area without reordering core components.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global top info bar and white navigation bar, the About Us page opens with a full-width hero image with a subtle dark overlay. Centered over this hero, a large, bold white “About Us” headline and a short supporting line introduce the solar company’s story, occupying roughly the upper half of the initial viewport. As users scroll past the hero, they enter a two-column content area constrained within a central max-width container. The left column, about two-thirds of the width, carries the full narrative: a concise company-header-summary at the top, followed by deeper mission and history sections, then structured lists of credentials and service areas. Each section is clearly separated vertically with 24–32px gaps, giving a calm but information-rich feel. To the right, a narrower sidebar column hosts a single elevated section-navigation card, visually similar to a WordPress “Categories” card, allowing users to jump to Mission, History, Credentials, and Service Areas. This sidebar card starts near the top of the content and can remain sticky as the user scrolls. At the base of the main content, a full-width accent band crosses both columns: the primary-actions-list, with prominent buttons for scheduling a consultation or requesting a quote. Behind the scenes, a subtle loading overlay can cover the two-column area while data is fetched, and any error appears as a noticeable but unobtrusive alert just below the hero, ensuring that the clean corporate look and clear reading path stay intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader component is positioned logically at the top of the About page’s main content container, immediately below the hero section and above the two-column layout. In its idle state it occupies no visible space, but when active it introduces a semi-transparent overlay that spans the full width of the content container (covering both the left main column and the right sidebar) and extends vertically from just beneath the hero down to the bottom of the content area currently in view. A centered loading indicator (spinner or progress message) is placed in the middle of the viewport, horizontally centered within the content container, with ample surrounding whitespace (at least 32px in all directions) to keep it readable against the dimmed background content. The overlay respects the page’s padding: it aligns with the same left/right content margins used by the two-column layout so it feels integrated rather than full-bleed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-content overlay within the main About content region when active, fixed relative to the viewport while loading so the spinner remains centered even when the user attempts to scroll; otherwise collapses to an invisible, non-space-taking state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation component is placed in the right-hand sidebar, aligned to the top of the main content row, mirroring the 'Categories' card from the mockup. It sits approximately one gutter width (16–24px) to the right of the main left column, and occupies about 30–35% of the content width (roughly 4 of 12 grid columns). The card has generous internal padding of 24–32px on all sides and is visually elevated with a subtle shadow or light border against the white page background. At the top of the card, a small, bold title like 'On This Page' is left-aligned, with 16–20px of space above the first navigation link. The navigation links themselves are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing, creating a concise yet breathable list pointing to Company Summary, Mission, History, Credentials, and Service Areas sections. There is at least 24px of margin between the bottom of the card and the next element below (if any appears in the sidebar region), so it reads as a single focused panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies a fixed-width column in the sidebar and uses a sticky behavior: after the user scrolls slightly, the card remains pinned near the top of the viewport within the right column, stopping before overlapping the footer or the primary-actions band; it does not overlap the left column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The company-header-summary component is the first element in the left main column below the hero. It begins roughly 48–64px beneath the bottom edge of the hero section, aligned to the left margin of the central content container and occupying about 65–70% of the content width (around 8 of 12 grid columns). Inside this area, a prominent section heading such as 'Who We Are' or 'About [Company Name]' appears at the top, with 16–24px of space below, followed by a short descriptive paragraph or two. If there is any visual element (e.g., a small team or installation image), it sits directly below or to the right of the text but remains within this left column, never crossing into the sidebar space. Overall vertical height of this section is moderate, with 24–32px of padding at the top and bottom relative to adjacent sections, creating a clear starting point for the About story. The top-left alignment and strong typography ensure this module introduces the content flow and anchors the reader’s eye after the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard block element at the top of the left column, pushing subsequent sections downward; it is fully responsive, expanding or contracting in height based on content without affecting the fixed width of the two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission-section component is stacked directly below the company-header-summary in the left main column, maintaining the same width (about 65–70% of the content area) and left alignment. There is a clear vertical gap of approximately 24–32px between the bottom of the company-header-summary and the top of the mission-section title. The section begins with a bold, left-aligned heading like 'Our Mission', followed by mission copy in paragraph form. Text is arranged in one or two short paragraphs with 16–24px line spacing and no more than 10–12 words per line for readability. If supporting icons or small visuals are included, they are aligned either inline with the text or in a small row beneath, but always bounded within this column. The mission-section’s vertical size remains balanced: more substantial than the header summary but not overwhelming, so that users can scan it quickly while scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal stacked section in the left column, pushing content below it downward; it maintains consistent top and bottom margins (24–32px) to align with the spacing rhythm of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The history-section component sits immediately below the mission-section in the left column, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap. It occupies the same column width (about two-thirds of the content area) with left-aligned text. A section heading like 'Our History' appears at the top with 16–20px of space below before any body content. The body may be chronological text or a short timeline, but spatially it is treated as a single vertical block: if a timeline is used, it runs vertically with items stacked and 12–16px spacing between them. The section is slightly taller than the mission-section if it contains more detail, but the layout keeps the left margin and right edge aligned with other left-column sections. There is a consistent 24–32px space below this section before the credentials-list begins, maintaining a clear separation between historical narrative and credentials.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard content block within the left column, expanding vertically as needed while preserving the column’s width and alignment relative to the sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The credentials-list component is placed below the history-section in the left main column, again separated by 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the same width as the previous left-column sections. A heading like 'Certifications & Credentials' is positioned at the top left, with 16–20px of space before the list begins. The credentials themselves are presented as a vertical list, using bullets or checkmarks aligned along a consistent left edge, with 12–16px vertical spacing between each item. The list is visually grouped as a card-like block: a subtle background tint or faint border can be used, with 24px of internal padding on all sides to echo the card treatment of the sidebar navigation while still feeling part of the main content. There is at least 24–32px of space below this list before the service-areas-list starts, to prevent the two lists from visually merging.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a vertically expanding list within the left column; it maintains the same left and right alignment as other main sections and does not affect the position of the right sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-areas-list component follows directly after the credentials-list in the left column, with 24–32px of vertical separation. It occupies the same width (approximately 65–70% of the content container) and is left-aligned with the previous sections. A heading such as 'Service Areas' appears at the top with 16–20px of space before the list items. The service areas are laid out as a vertical list for clarity; on wider screens, they may be arranged into two columns within this left column, but always staying within its boundaries. Vertical spacing between items is 12–16px. A light divider line or subtle rows can be used to distinguish items if the list is long, but padding around the entire block (24px) ensures it reads as one cohesive module. A larger vertical gap of 32–40px is placed below this section before the primary-actions-list band, emphasizing the transition from informational content to action-oriented content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains a standard stacked list in the left column, expanding in height as needed; in responsive layouts, it may gracefully switch between single- and multi-column internal list layouts while preserving its overall column position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-actions-list component forms a visually distinct band at the bottom of the About content area. It starts just below the service-areas-list, with a pronounced vertical gap of about 32–40px to signal a shift from reading to acting. Unlike the other components, this band spans the full width of the content container, bridging both left main column and right sidebar (effectively occupying all 12 grid columns). The band uses a contrasting background color (e.g., a soft blue tint that complements the site’s strong blues) and 32–48px of internal vertical padding, with 24–32px of horizontal padding on each side. Inside, the main call-to-action text (e.g., 'Ready to go solar?') is left-aligned or center-left at the top, with 16–24px below before the actions themselves. The actions are arranged as 2–3 prominent buttons or links (such as 'Schedule a Consultation' and 'Get a Free Quote'), aligned horizontally on larger screens with 16–24px horizontal spacing between them, and stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing on smaller screens. This section extends visually to the same left and right bounds as the main content container, creating a solid anchor at the bottom of the page’s narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-width content band within the page’s central container, visually overriding the two-column split beneath it; it remains non-sticky and scrolls naturally but anchors the end of the About content before the site footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-error-notice component is positioned at the top of the About content area, directly below the hero section and above the two-column layout, sharing the same width as the central content container. When present, it appears as a horizontal alert bar or card spanning nearly the full content width (with standard left/right container padding), occupying roughly the height of one to two lines of text plus padding (total height around 56–80px). The message is left-aligned with an icon on the left and text to the right, with 16–24px padding on all sides to keep it legible. There is about 24px of vertical space between the bottom of this notice and the top of the first content block (company-header-summary), ensuring it feels visually distinct and not fused with the main content. When hidden, it collapses completely and the layout shifts upward, keeping the company-header-summary as the first visible block under the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a non-sticky inline alert block that pushes the main content downward when visible; it does not overlay content but occupies its own vertical space within the scrolling flow.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Provides general contact information and a form for submitting inquiries about solar energy installation services, with optional topic preselection via URL parameters.

core_features: Load and display current contact details (phone, email, office address) using getContactPageContent.
Render a general contact form with fields for name, email, phone, topic, and message.
Populate the topic selector dynamically from topicOptions returned by getContactPageContent.
Optionally preselect the topic field based on the URL parameter `topic` when it matches a value from topicOptions.
Allow users to modify the selected topic and update the URL parameter `topic` accordingly for sharable/returnable links.
Submit the contact form using submitContactForm and handle success/error responses, including displaying a confirmation with ticketId when available.
After successful submission, present an optional navigation link to return to the home page (index.html).
Provide optional quick links to support resources/FAQs (links supplied via external configuration, not hardcoded in this specification).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View contact information
1. User navigates to contact.html, optionally with a `topic` URL parameter.
2. On page initialization, the Contact Page Loader component calls getContactPageContent.
3. Once the response is received, the Contact Info Panel displays the supportPhone, supportEmail, and officeAddress fields.
4. The Contact Form component receives topicOptions and checks the current URL for a `topic` parameter.
5. If the `topic` parameter matches one of the topicOptions, the corresponding option is selected in the topic field; otherwise, the topic field is left unselected or set to a default state.
6. The user can click the displayed phone number or email address to initiate a call or email using the dynamic values returned by getContactPageContent.

Workflow 2: Submit a general inquiry
1. User opens contact.html (with or without a `topic` URL parameter).
2. The page fetches data via getContactPageContent and initializes the Contact Form with the resolved topic selection as in Workflow 1.
3. The user fills in the required form fields: name, email, phone, topic (if not already selected), and message.
4. On form submission, the Contact Form validates that required fields are non-empty and that email and phone formats meet client-side validation rules.
5. If client-side validation fails, the form shows inline validation messages and prevents the submitContactForm call.
6. If validation passes, the form triggers submitContactForm with the current field values (name, email, phone, topic, message).
7. While the request is in progress, the form’s submit button is disabled and a loading state is shown (e.g., spinner or status text).
8. When submitContactForm returns:
   - If success is true: the Submission Result Banner shows a confirmation message using the response.message and ticketId. The form can optionally be reset or disabled to avoid duplicate submissions. A Back to Home link to index.html is displayed.
   - If success is false: the Submission Result Banner shows an error state using the response.message. The form remains editable so the user can correct inputs and resubmit.

Workflow 3: Change or share inquiry topic using URL parameters
1. User navigates to contact.html with a `topic` query parameter or selects a topic from the topic dropdown in the Contact Form.
2. On initial load, the Contact Form resolves the selected topic from the URL parameter as described earlier.
3. When the user changes the topic selection in the form, the Topic Change handler updates the page URL’s `topic` parameter using the browser history API (e.g., pushState/replaceState) without reloading the page.
4. If another user opens the updated URL (contact.html?topic=<value>), the Contact Page Loader again calls getContactPageContent.
5. After loading, the Contact Form reads the `topic` URL parameter and preselects it if it matches one of the topicOptions, ensuring a consistent shared topic context.

Workflow 4: Browse support resources (if configured)
1. User opens contact.html.
2. The Support Links Panel receives a list of support/FAQ links from external configuration (not from the page’s interfaces).
3. The panel renders a list of navigable items (e.g., common help topics or FAQ categories).
4. When the user clicks a support link, the browser navigates to the configured URL, which may include query parameters as defined elsewhere in the application.
interactions: Click on phone number: Uses the dynamic supportPhone value from getContactPageContent to initiate a phone call via a tel: link.
Click on email address: Uses the dynamic supportEmail value from getContactPageContent to open the default email client via a mailto: link.
Form field input: Users can type into name, email, phone, and message fields; the component maintains an internal state for each field.
Topic dropdown change: When the user selects a topic from the topicOptions list, the form updates its internal selected topic state and updates the `topic` URL parameter to match the new selection.
Form submission: When the user activates the primary submit action, the form validates input fields and, if valid, calls submitContactForm with the current field values.

Form submission success handling: On a successful response from submitContactForm, the Submission Result Banner displays confirmation details including ticketId, and the form is optionally cleared or disabled to prevent immediate resubmission.

Form submission error handling: If submitContactForm returns success as false or the network request fails, the Submission Result Banner shows an error message and the form remains interactive for corrections and retries.
Back to Home navigation: After a successful submission, the user can click a Back to Home link that navigates to index.html without relying on local storage.
Support link click: If support/FAQ links are configured, clicking a link navigates the user to the corresponding resource URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s state is driven by URL parameters and interface responses, without using localStorage. On initial load of contact.html, the page reads the `topic` query parameter (if present). The Contact Page Loader then calls getContactPageContent and stores the returned data in memory for this page instance: supportPhone, supportEmail, officeAddress, and topicOptions. After data is loaded, the Contact Form component compares the `topic` URL parameter value with the list of available topicOptions. If there is an exact match, that topic is set as the selected value in the topic field; otherwise, the topic field is left unselected or set to a neutral default. When the user changes the topic via the dropdown, the page updates the browser URL to contact.html?topic=<selectedTopic> using the history API, ensuring that the state can be shared or revisited via URL alone. The form’s transient state (current field values, validation errors, loading indicators) is kept in component state only and is not persisted in URL or storage. On submission, the page calls submitContactForm with the current form state. The response from submitContactForm defines a submission sub-state: success (true/false), ticketId (if present), and response.message. This sub-state controls which UI is shown in the Submission Result Banner. A successful submission can optionally set a flag that disables or clears the form fields but does not modify the URL. The Back to Home link is a static navigation to index.html that does not depend on state. All dynamic business data (contact details, topic labels, messages, ticketId) comes exclusively from getContactPageContent and submitContactForm responses, or from external configuration for support links, avoiding any hardcoded values in this design.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact page by calling getContactPageContent, handling loading and error states, and passing the retrieved data (contact details and topicOptions) to child components.
Displays the dynamically loaded contact details, including supportPhone, supportEmail, and officeAddress, and makes the phone and email values interactive.

Provides a general inquiry form with fields for name, email, phone, topic, and message. It preselects the topic based on the `topic` URL parameter when possible, validates inputs, submits data via submitContactForm, and updates submission state.
Optionally displays quick links to support resources or FAQ pages. The list of links is provided via external configuration or higher-level props, not via the Contact page interfaces.

Shows the result of a contact form submission, including success or error messages, and displays the ticketId when available. On successful submission, it also displays an optional Back to Home navigation control.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: (1) a global top info bar and (2) a white navigation bar (both outside this component list but assumed), (3) a full-width hero section for the Contact page with a background image and centered headline, then (4) the main contact content in a two-column layout where the left column (about 2/3 width) holds the core contact content (contact-info-panel followed by contact-form) and the right column (about 1/3 width) holds a single, structured card (contact-support-links-panel) acting as the sidebar. The contact-submission-result-banner sits between the hero and the two-column content. The contact-page-loader overlays the main content area when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: (1) a global top info bar and (2) a white navigation bar (both outside this component list but assumed), (3) a full-width hero section for the Contact page with a background image and centered headline, then (4) the main contact content in a two-column layout where the left column (about 2/3 width) holds the core contact content (contact-info-panel followed by contact-form) and the right column (about 1/3 width) holds a single, structured card (contact-support-links-panel) acting as the sidebar. The contact-submission-result-banner sits between the hero and the two-column content. The contact-page-loader overlays the main content area when active.
