layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top info and nav bars together occupy roughly 18–22% of the initial viewport height, the hero section occupies about 40–45%, and the remaining vertical space is used by the scrollable main content. Within the main content container, the central content width is constrained to a typical marketing-site max width (around 1080–1200px) and centered. The left column takes about 65–70% of this width, housing the info panel and a tall, dominant form; the right sidebar takes about 30–35% for the support links card. Vertical spacing between the hero, banner, and main content uses 48–64px gaps, while spacing between stacked components (info panel, form) uses 16–32px. Cards and panels use 24–32px internal padding on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero is visually open with a large, bold headline and generous padding, reflecting a spacious, high-impact marketing style. The contact-submission-result-banner is compact but readable, with moderate padding and clear typography. The main content area is slightly denser: the contact-form takes up more vertical space with many fields, but fields and labels are spaced 16–24px apart to avoid clutter. The contact-info-panel and contact-support-links-panel are card-like, with 24–32px padding and clear separation from the background, maintaining a professional, moderately spacious corporate feel rather than a tight, utilitarian layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow keeps the mockup’s Z-pattern: users start at the top-left logo and info bar, move right to key contact/CTA elements, then diagonally down to the centered hero headline ('Contact Us' or similar), then down and back left into the main contact content (info panel and form) as the primary interaction area, and finally across to the right sidebar where the support links card lives. When present, the contact-submission-result-banner briefly becomes the first focal point after the hero before the eye continues down into the left column form. The loader, when active, temporarily interrupts this flow by overlaying the main content area with a centered indicator, but it is visually restrained so it doesn’t compete with the hero styling.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s top info bar and clean white navigation, a full-width hero image with a subtle dark overlay stretches across the screen, occupying nearly half the initial viewport height. In the center of this hero, a large, bold white 'Contact' headline and a short supporting line sit vertically and horizontally centered, surrounded by generous breathing room. Directly beneath the hero, and aligned to the same central content width, a slim horizontal notification band appears only when there is a submission result to show, spanning the full content width. Below this, the main contact section forms a two-column block: on the left, a wide column holds a concise contact-info panel at the top and a larger, vertically generous contact-form beneath it; on the right, a narrower sidebar column contains a single elevated card listing support topics or quick links. The columns sit on a shared baseline and maintain a clear gutter between them, with consistent card padding and vertical spacing creating an orderly, businesslike composition. If the loader is active, a soft overlay covers the entire two-column area beneath the hero, with a centered spinner, while the rest of the page (header, hero) remains visible but visually secondary. On smaller screens, the same elements stack in a single column: hero, banner (if present), info panel, form, and finally the support links card, preserving the visual hierarchy while adapting to the narrower viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as an overlay strictly over the main content section (the area containing the contact-info-panel, contact-form, and contact-support-links-panel), starting immediately below the contact-submission-result-banner (or directly below the hero if no banner is shown) and extending down to just above the footer. It spans 100% of the main content container’s width, matching the centered content bounds (roughly 1080–1200px wide on large screens, with equal left and right margins relative to the viewport). A semi-transparent white or light overlay slightly dims the underlying two-column layout while keeping its structure faintly visible. At the visual center of this overlaid region, a circular spinner or progress indicator is placed, horizontally centered and vertically centered within the visible portion of the main content area. On desktop, the loader sits above both columns equally; on mobile, it still covers the full stacked content area. Once data loading completes, the overlay disappears entirely, revealing the two-column content beneath without shifting their positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute (or fixed-within-container) overlay anchored to the top of the main content container, covering the entire contact section while active. It does not push other components down; instead, it visually sits on top of the contact-info-panel, contact-form, and contact-support-links-panel. It scrolls with the page if the user scrolls while it is visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel sits at the very top of the main two-column content region, anchored to the left column. It occupies approximately 65–70% of the content width on desktop, extending from the left edge of the centered content container to the internal column gutter on the right. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top edge of the contact-support-links-panel in the right column, giving a clean, grid-based feel. The panel itself is a white or very light card with a subtle shadow or border, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. It is relatively shallow in height compared to the form beneath it—typically about 15–25% of the total height of the left column content—providing a concise summary of key contact details (phone, email, office address, business hours) in 1–2 short rows or a compact vertical stack. There is a vertical gap of 16–24px between the bottom of this info panel and the top of the contact-form, following the internal section spacing from the mockup. On smaller screens, the contact-info-panel becomes a full-width card that appears directly under the hero (and under any active submission result banner), above the contact-form, maintaining the same padding and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level card within the normal document flow, anchored to the top of the left column. It does not stick or float; it moves upward or downward with the page scroll along with the rest of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form is placed directly beneath the contact-info-panel in the left column, separated by a deliberate vertical gap of approximately 24px to maintain hierarchy and breathing room. It spans the full width of the left column (about 65–70% of the content width on desktop), aligning flush with the left edge of the info panel above and stopping at the column gutter on the right. Visually, it is the largest and tallest block in the main content area—its height commonly exceeds that of the right sidebar card—making it the primary interactive focal point. The form is contained within a white or light card with 24–32px internal padding and a clear, bold section title at the top, followed by stacked form fields (name, email, phone, topic dropdown, message, etc.). Fields are arranged in a single column for clarity, with 16–24px vertical spacing between each field group and 24px above the submit button row. The submit button is aligned left within the form, with at least 16–24px of padding around it inside the card. The bottom of the form has 48–64px of space before any content that follows (such as the footer) to avoid crowding. On mobile and tablet widths, the contact-form becomes a full-width block directly below the contact-info-panel, with the same internal spacing and a comfortable outer margin (16–24px) to the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the normal flow, anchored to the left column (or full-width column on small screens). It scrolls normally and does not have sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-support-links-panel occupies the right sidebar column of the two-column layout, starting at the same vertical position as the top of the contact-info-panel in the left column, creating a shared horizontal baseline. On desktop, it uses roughly 30–35% of the content width and sits flush against the right edge of the centered content container, separated from the left column by a clear gutter of approximately 32px. This panel appears as a single, vertically oriented card with a white or light background and a subtle shadow or border, matching the visual treatment of the left-side cards. It has 24–32px of internal padding and contains a heading such as 'Support Topics' or 'How Can We Help?', followed by a vertical list of navigation links (for example, 'Installation Questions', 'Billing & Financing', 'Technical Support') spaced 12–16px apart. The card’s height is driven by the number of links, but it is generally shorter than the contact-form, allowing the left column to visually dominate while the sidebar reads as a supportive, secondary navigation module. Below the card, there is at least 48px of whitespace before any subsequent sidebar content or footer area. On smaller screens, this panel drops below the contact-form, converting the layout into a single column; it becomes a full-width card with the same internal padding and a 32–48px vertical gap above it to clearly separate it from the end of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static right-column sidebar card within the normal flow. It is not sticky by default; it scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the page, consistent with conventional WordPress-style sidebars.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-submission-result-banner is a full-width notification band positioned between the hero section and the main two-column content container. It is only rendered when there is a submission result (success or error) to display. Horizontally, it aligns with the main content container, matching its max width (around 1080–1200px) and centering within the viewport, with consistent left and right margins that match the hero’s inner content alignment. Vertically, there is a 24–32px gap between the bottom of the hero and the top of this banner, and another 24–32px gap between the bottom of the banner and the top of the contact-info-panel and contact-support-links-panel row. The banner’s height is flexible but typically medium-high (roughly 72–96px on desktop), driven by its message content. Inside, it has 16–24px of vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. An icon or status indicator appears on the left, the main message text sits center-left in one or two lines, and a close/dismiss control is right-aligned, all aligned on a single baseline. The background color contrasts with the white cards (e.g., green for success, red/orange for error, or blue for info) while maintaining the site’s corporate solar palette. When the banner is dismissed or not needed, it is completely removed from the DOM or collapsed so that the main two-column content shifts upward seamlessly beneath the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static band in the normal vertical flow, anchored between the hero and the main content. It does not remain fixed on scroll; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

name: Legal & Privacy
title: Legal & Privacy
description: Provides users with the website’s legal information, including privacy policy, terms of use, and disclosures related to financing offers and installer listings. Content is dynamically loaded via getLegalContent and can be deep-linked and navigated using URL parameters.

core_features: Load all legal content (privacy policy, terms of use, financing disclosures, installer listings disclaimer) from the getLegalContent interface on page initialization.
Display legal content in clearly separated sections that can be switched via tabs or navigation links (e.g., Privacy, Terms of Use, Financing Disclosures, Installer Listings Disclaimer).
Support deep-linking to a specific section (e.g., privacy, terms, financing, installers) via URL parameters so users can land directly on the relevant legal content.
Provide an at-a-glance indication of when the legal content was last updated using the lastUpdatedDate from getLegalContent.
Allow users to quickly navigate within a selected section using a table of contents that reflects headings contained within the dynamically loaded HTML content.
Offer a print-friendly or consolidated view of all legal content controlled via URL parameters (e.g., a view mode parameter) without duplicating or hardcoding text.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Viewing the Legal & Privacy page for the first time
1) User navigates to legal.html from a footer link or direct URL.
2) The page initializes and triggers the getLegalContent interface with no parameters.
3) On success, the page stores privacyPolicyHtml, termsOfUseHtml, financingDisclosuresHtml, installerListingsDisclaimerHtml, and lastUpdatedDate in page state.
4) The page reads the URL parameter `section` (if present). If absent or unrecognized, it defaults to a primary section (e.g., privacy policy) purely via logic, not hardcoded content.
5) The matching section tab is activated and its HTML content is rendered.
6) The Last Updated indicator shows lastUpdatedDate so users know when the policies were last revised.

Workflow: Deep-link to a specific legal section (e.g., from another page or email)
1) User opens a link such as legal.html?section=financing.
2) The page initializes, reads `section=financing` from the URL, and requests content via getLegalContent.
3) After getLegalContent returns, the page validates the `section` value against the available sections.
4) If valid, the corresponding section (e.g., financingDisclosuresHtml) is displayed as the active tab and the viewport is scrolled to that section.
5) If invalid, the page falls back to the default section and keeps the URL intact or normalizes it to a supported section value.

Workflow: Switching between legal sections using tabs or navigation links
1) While viewing any section, the user clicks on another section tab (e.g., from Privacy to Terms of Use).
2) The tab click handler updates the selected section in page state.
3) The same handler updates the URL parameter `section` (e.g., legal.html?section=terms) using the browser history API so the current view can be bookmarked.
4) Based on the updated state, the page renders the corresponding HTML (e.g., termsOfUseHtml) in the main content area.
5) The in-section table of contents updates (if implemented) to reflect headings found in the newly active section.

Workflow: Using the in-section table of contents to jump within long legal text
1) Once a section is active and its HTML is rendered, the page parses the HTML headings to build an in-page table of contents (without altering the original HTML content).
2) The user clicks a heading entry in the table of contents.
3) The click handler locates the related anchor or heading within the currently rendered HTML and scrolls it into view.
4) Optionally, the handler updates an internal fragment-like state (e.g., `subsection` in the URL query or hash) to allow deep-linking to that subsection.

Workflow: Switching to or loading a print-friendly view
1) User clicks a "Print / Full View" control on the Legal & Privacy page.
2) The control toggles or sets a URL parameter, e.g., `view=print`, by updating the current URL (legal.html?view=print&section=privacy or similar).
3) The page responds to the change in `view` by adjusting layout logic to show all sections in one continuous flow or a print-optimized layout, while still using the same HTML values from getLegalContent.
4) The user can then use the browser’s print functionality; no additional API calls or hardcoded content are required.
5) If the user returns to normal view (e.g., toggling off or loading legal.html without `view=print`), the page restores the section-based layout using the `section` parameter to determine the active section.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial content load
- Trigger: Page load event for legal.html.
- Behavior: Call getLegalContent once; handle loading state (e.g., disable navigation controls until content is available) and error state (e.g., show retry control) without displaying any hardcoded legal text.

Interaction: Section tab click
- Trigger: User clicks on one of the section navigation controls (e.g., Privacy, Terms of Use, Financing Disclosures, Installer Listings Disclaimer).
- Behavior: Update the active section in page state; update `section` URL parameter; render the corresponding HTML from getLegalContent in the content view; scroll to top of the section container.

Interaction: Deep link navigation / browser back-forward
- Trigger: User navigates with browser back/forward buttons or opens a bookmarked URL that contains a `section` or `view` parameter.
- Behavior: On URL change, the page reads the current query parameters; if `section` maps to a known section, update active section; if `view` is set to a recognized mode, update layout accordingly; avoid re-calling getLegalContent if content is already loaded and not expired per page logic.

Interaction: In-section table of contents click
- Trigger: User clicks a heading entry in the generated in-page TOC.
- Behavior: Prevent full page reload; scroll to the target element within the active section’s HTML; optionally update a `subsection` indicator (e.g., via hash or query parameter) for deep-linking, without changing which main section is active.

Interaction: Retry loading legal content after an error
- Trigger: If getLegalContent fails, the user clicks a "Retry" or equivalent control.
- Behavior: Re-invoke getLegalContent; on success, clear error state and re-render sections based on existing URL parameters; on repeated failure, maintain an error state without substituting hardcoded policy content.

Interaction: Print view toggle
- Trigger: User clicks a control to enter or exit a print-friendly or combined view.
- Behavior: Update `view` parameter in the URL; respond by rendering either a single-section view (using `section`) or a combined/all-sections view (ignoring `section` or using it only to control initial scroll); all text continues to come from the existing getLegalContent response.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven primarily by URL parameters and the result of getLegalContent. The core URL parameters are: (1) `section`: a string that identifies which legal section is active. Accepted values are determined by the implementation (e.g., `privacy`, `terms`, `financing`, `installers`) and mapped to the corresponding HTML fields in the getLegalContent response. If `section` is missing or invalid, the page selects a default section purely through logic, not content. (2) `view`: a string indicating layout mode, such as a normal tabbed view versus a print or full-content view (e.g., `view=print`). When `view` is set to a print-friendly value, the page may display all available sections in sequence using the HTML fields returned from getLegalContent, or adjust layout behavior while still respecting the chosen `section` where applicable. Optional state may also be represented with a `subsection` parameter or URL hash pointing to a specific heading inside the active section; when present, the page scrolls to the corresponding anchor after rendering the section. On initial load, the page reads `section`, `view`, and any subsection indicator from the URL, then calls getLegalContent and applies the parameters once content is available. On subsequent navigation, UI interactions update these URL parameters via the history API to keep the URL in sync with the current view. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; the URL acts as the single source of truth for navigation state.

components.functionality: Initializes the Legal & Privacy page by calling getLegalContent, managing loading and error states, and exposing the retrieved HTML segments and lastUpdatedDate to child components.

Provides top-level navigation between legal sections (e.g., Privacy, Terms of Use, Financing Disclosures, Installer Listings Disclaimer). It reads the current section from the URL, sets the active tab accordingly, and updates the URL when the user clicks different tabs.

Renders the HTML for the currently active legal section using the appropriate field from getLegalContent (privacyPolicyHtml, termsOfUseHtml, financingDisclosuresHtml, or installerListingsDisclaimerHtml). It listens to section state changes and re-renders content accordingly.

Generates and displays an in-page table of contents for the currently active section by inspecting or parsing the rendered HTML headings. Allows users to click items to scroll to specific subsections and can update an optional subsection indicator in the URL.
Shows the date when the legal content was last updated using lastUpdatedDate from getLegalContent, and optionally provides a brief label or tooltip (without embedding any hardcoded policy text).
Allows users to switch between the default section-based view and an alternative view mode (such as a print-friendly or full-content view) by toggling the `view` URL parameter.
Displays loading, error, or unavailable states related to getLegalContent and provides a user-triggered retry mechanism without substituting any static legal text.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, typical of Bootstrap/WordPress themes, with a main content column and a narrower sidebar column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the existing global shell from the mockup (top info bar, navigation bar, and a full-width hero with a centered 'Legal & Privacy' headline). All legal-specific components live in the main content section below the hero, which follows the same two-column pattern: the left column (about two-thirds width) holds the primary interactive controls and legal text (tabs, view mode toggle, last updated indicator, content, loader, error panel), while the right column (about one-third width) is a single, structured card containing the legal table of contents sidebar.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the existing global shell from the mockup (top info bar, navigation bar, and a full-width hero with a centered 'Legal & Privacy' headline). All legal-specific components live in the main content section below the hero, which follows the same two-column pattern: the left column (about two-thirds width) holds the primary interactive controls and legal text (tabs, view mode toggle, last updated indicator, content, loader, error panel), while the right column (about one-third width) is a single, structured card containing the legal table of contents sidebar.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero retains a tall presence, occupying roughly 40–45% of the initial viewport height. A 48–64px vertical gap separates the hero from the main content container. Within the container, the left column spans roughly 65–70% of the width and the right column 30–35%. Cards and interactive regions (tabs block, content view, TOC sidebar, error panel) use 24–32px internal padding and sit on a slightly off-white background with clear margins (24–32px) from the page edge. Vertical spacing between stacked items inside the left column (tabs row, last updated banner, content, status panels) follows a 16–24px rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The legal page is text-heavy, so the density is slightly higher than in a purely marketing section but still keeps a calm, corporate feel: large, readable typography with comfortable line height and 24–32px padding around text blocks. The top controls (tabs and view toggle) are compact but not cramped, with clear separation between each control. The TOC sidebar is denser, listing multiple links but enclosed within a card with generous padding and 16px vertical spacing between link groups to avoid clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows the same Z-pattern as the mockup: the eye starts at the hero’s centered 'Legal & Privacy' headline, then moves down into the main content, first catching the left-aligned section tabs, then sliding right to notice the view mode toggle and the TOC sidebar card. From there, attention moves diagonally back down to the large legal content block on the left. Within the left column, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: tabs and view mode toggle row, then the last updated indicator, then the main content or error panel. The loader and error states temporarily interrupt this flow by visually dominating the main content region while preserving the overall layout structure.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing top info bar, white navigation bar, and a full-width hero image with a centered 'Legal & Privacy' headline, the page transitions into a structured two-column legal content area. A wide, centered content container sits about 56px below the hero, aligned to the same grid as the rest of the site. The left column, occupying roughly two-thirds of the container, starts with a horizontal control bar: section tabs aligned to the left and a view mode toggle aligned to the right. Directly beneath this bar, a slim, lightly tinted banner shows the last updated date, running full width across the left column. Below that, a large, white card-like block holds the main legal text, filling the rest of the vertical space with a scrollable flow of headings and paragraphs. When data is loading or fails, a loader or an error-and-retry panel temporarily takes over this same content area without changing the surrounding structure. On the right, a slightly narrower column houses a single, elevated TOC sidebar card that aligns with the top of the left column controls and remains visible as the user scrolls, echoing the 'Categories' card in the mockup. The overall impression is that of a professional, corporate legal page with a strong hero, a clear two-column layout, and well-separated functional chunks that guide the user smoothly from high-level sections into detailed legal content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is visually anchored to the left main content column, just below the hero and aligned within the same 65–70% width content area. When active, it appears as a slim horizontal progress indicator (2–4px high) running along the top edge of the left column’s main content region, directly under the tab row and spanning nearly the full width of the left column with 24px horizontal padding from the container edges. Optionally, while content is loading, a light overlay sits on top of the legal-content-view area: a semi-transparent white layer that covers the card where the legal text would appear, with a centered spinner positioned slightly above the vertical center of that card so it remains clearly visible above the fold. The right TOC sidebar remains visible and unaffected, reinforcing the sense that loading is localized to the legal text area rather than the entire page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally hidden; when active, it occupies the top edge of the left content area and may overlay the legal-content-view with a semi-transparent layer. It scrolls with the page (not fixed), staying bound to the left column region so that its perceived scope matches the area of content being loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section tabs sit at the very top of the main content block in the left column, directly beneath the hero and its intervening vertical spacer. They are aligned flush with the left edge of the content container, spanning roughly 70–80% of the left column’s width, leaving enough room on the right side of the same horizontal row for the view mode toggle. The tabs form a horizontal strip approximately 48–56px tall, with individual tab labels spaced evenly and separated by 16–24px. The tab row has 24px horizontal padding on both sides and 8–12px vertical padding inside, visually attached to the top of the legal content card (it feels like the tab bar is the header of the content area). A 16–24px vertical gap below the tab row separates it from the last updated indicator, keeping the overall feel open and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable content column. On smaller viewports, the tab row remains on top of the left column stack but may wrap to multiple lines or convert to a horizontally scrollable strip while keeping its top-of-column placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal content view is the dominant block within the left column, positioned directly beneath the last updated indicator with a 16–24px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the total content container width) and appears as a tall, white or very light card with subtle elevation, visually echoing the large project photo area from the mockup but filled with text content instead of imagery. Internal padding is generous—around 24–32px on all sides—creating a comfortable text frame. The content flows vertically: section headings separated by 24–32px, paragraphs with 16–20px spacing between them, and any inline lists aligned to the left margin. As users scroll, this block extends downward, forming the main reading area of the page, while the surrounding layout (tabs, TOC sidebar) remains spatially consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the page and expands vertically as needed. It shares its space with the error-and-retry panel and any loader overlay, which temporarily replace or cover this region without changing its position or width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal TOC sidebar lives in the right column of the main content area, aligning its top edge with the top of the left column’s tab row. It occupies the full width of the right column, which is roughly 30–35% of the main content container. Visually, it is a single, vertically oriented card with a light background and subtle drop shadow, mirroring the 'Categories' card from the mockup. Inside the card, there is 24–32px padding on all sides. A title at the top is followed by a vertically stacked list of links to sections within the legal content, with each link separated by 12–16px and grouped with 16–24px spacing between logical clusters. There is comfortable whitespace at the bottom of the card (24–32px) so it does not feel cramped even when filled with many entries. The card floats over a neutral page background, clearly distinguished from the main text area but visually balanced with it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky sidebar on desktop and tablet: once the top of the TOC card reaches a point slightly below the top navigation (e.g., with a 80–96px offset from the viewport top to clear the header), it remains fixed in place while the legal-content-view scrolls vertically beside it. On smaller screens, it drops below the main content, becoming a full-width card stacked underneath the legal text and losing the sticky behavior for simplicity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last updated indicator is a slim, full-width banner that sits in the left column directly beneath the section tabs and above the main legal content, separated by a 16–24px vertical gap from the tabs and another 16–24px from the content view. It spans the entire width of the left column with 16–24px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, creating a band about 32–40px tall. The background is a subtle tinted strip (e.g., a very light blue-gray) that contrasts just enough with the white content card beneath it to be noticeable but not distracting. The text is left-aligned, small-to-regular in size, and vertically centered within the strip, giving users a quick, at-a-glance sense of policy freshness before they dive into the detailed content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrolls with the content as part of the left column stack. It remains in a fixed vertical relationship between the tabs row and the content view, and does not become sticky or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view mode toggle shares the same horizontal row as the section tabs at the top of the left column. It is right-aligned within that row, occupying roughly 20–30% of the left column’s width, with at least 16–24px horizontal space between the rightmost tab and the toggle control so they are visually distinct. The toggle might appear as a segmented control or a pair of labeled buttons ('Standard', 'Print-friendly', etc.) with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding around the labels. The entire control sits vertically centered within the 48–56px-tall tabs row, preserving a clean, single-line top control bar. On larger screens, it hugs the right edge of the content container with 24px padding from the column edge, giving a tidy, corporate toolbar feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, it stays inline with the tabs row at the top of the left column. On narrower screens, it may wrap to sit on a new line directly beneath the tabs but above the last updated indicator, remaining right-aligned or stretching to full width while preserving a 16–24px gap above and below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-and-retry panel occupies the same spatial footprint as the legal-content-view in the left column when an error occurs. It appears directly beneath the last updated indicator, spanning the full width of the left column with the same 24–32px internal padding as the content card. Instead of dense text, the panel uses a more compact layout: an error icon or bold heading near the top left, explanatory text below, and a prominent 'Retry' button aligned either left or centered horizontally. Vertical spacing within the panel follows the same 16–24px rhythm to maintain visual consistency. The card’s height is sufficient to feel substantial (e.g., 200–300px) but typically shorter than a full screen, leaving background whitespace visible below. It visually interrupts the standard reading flow while clearly indicating that the problem is localized to the legal content area, not the entire page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Hidden under normal operation. When shown, it replaces or pushes out the legal-content-view within the left column, occupying that block’s space. It scrolls with the page like any other card and does not become fixed or floating, keeping the layout stable even in error states.
